**Transparency** is the concealment of distribution aspects from the user. The major sub-types (as per ISO/OSI reference model) are:
- **Access transparency** — hides data representation differences.
- **Location transparency** — hides physical location of resources.
- **Migration transparency** — hides relocation of resources.
- **Replication transparency** — hides the existence of multiple copies.
- **Concurrency transparency** — hides shared access by multiple users.
- **Failure transparency** — hides failures and recovery.
- **Persistence transparency** — hides storage medium.
- **Relocation transparency** — hides relocation during active use.

(Examiner expects at least **4 sub-types** for full 3 marks.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between *scalability* and *heterogeneity* in distributed systems. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

| Aspect | Scalability | Heterogeneity |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **Definition** | Ability of the system to handle growth in users, data, and geography. | Ability of the system to interoperate across diverse hardware, OS, and protocols. |
| **Focus** | Capacity and performance under growth. | Interoperability across diversity. |
| **Example** | Google Search serving billions of queries by adding more servers and sharding indexes. | A Java application on Linux calling a Python microservice on Windows through a REST API. |
| **Solutions** | Caching, sharding, replication, CDNs. | Middleware, IDLs, RPC frameworks. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the *Heterogeneity* and *Openness* challenges in distributed systems. How does middleware help in addressing heterogeneity?

**Model Answer:**

**Heterogeneity:**
Distributed systems must integrate components that differ in:
- **Networks** — Ethernet, Wi-Fi, 5G, satellite.
- **Hardware** — x86, ARM, GPUs.
- **Operating Systems** — Windows, Linux, Android, macOS.
- **Programming languages** — Java, Python, Go, C++.

**Examples:** A mobile app (iOS Swift) communicating with a backend (Java Spring Boot on Linux) which queries a database (PostgreSQL on Linux) and a cache (Redis) and an analytics engine (Python on Kubernetes).

**Openness:**
An open system is one whose key interfaces are **published as standards**, allowing third parties to add new components. Openness depends on:
- **Standardized communication protocols** (HTTP, gRPC, AMQP).
- **Standardized data formats** (JSON, XML, Protocol Buffers).
- **Standardized service interfaces** (POSIX, ODBC, JDBC).

**Middleware and Heterogeneity:**
Middleware is a software layer that sits between the application and the OS/network. It provides:
1. **Common programming interface** — hides OS-specific calls.
2. **Protocol translation** — converts between incompatible protocols.
3. **Data marshalling** — converts between representations (e.g., big-endian vs little-endian).
4. **Service abstraction** — RPC, RMI, message queues, ORBs.

**Examples of middleware:** CORBA, Java RMI, gRPC, Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "Middleware solves heterogeneity completely." Wrong. Middleware *reduces* heterogeneity for the application but does not eliminate it — the *middleware itself* may have platform-specific implementations. **(Lose 1 mark if overstated.)**

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the *CAP Theorem*. Discuss how modern NoSQL databases make trade-offs among C, A, and P.

**Model Answer:**

The **CAP Theorem**, proposed by **Eric Brewer (2000)** and formally proven by Gilbert & Lynch (2002), states that in a distributed data store, you can have at most **two of the following three guarantees simultaneously**:

- **C — Consistency:** All clients see the same data at the same time.
- **A — Availability:** Every request receives a (non-error) response, even if some nodes are down.
- **P — Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes.

**Trade-off Analysis:**

| System Type | Sacrifices | Examples | Use Case |
|-------------|-----------|----------|----------|
| **CP** | Availability | HBase, MongoDB (with majority writes), Redis Cluster | Banking, inventory |
| **AP** | Strong consistency | Cassandra, Amazon DynamoDB, CouchDB | Social media, shopping carts |
| **CA** | Partition tolerance (only single-node) | PostgreSQL, MySQL (single instance) | Traditional OLTP |

**Diagram (re-rendered as text since exam-paper is text):**
```
                Consistency (C)
                       ▲
                      / \
                     /   \
                    /     \
                   / CAP   \
                  / Triangle\
                 /           \
                /_____________\
   Availability (A) ───► Partition Tolerance (P)
```

**Modern Tunable Consistency:**
Most modern NoSQL systems offer **configurable consistency levels** (e.g., Cassandra's `ONE`, `QUORUM`, `ALL`; DynamoDB's `ConsistentRead`). This effectively allows sliding along the C–A spectrum based on the application's needs — challenging the strict CAP binary.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students wrongly state that CA systems exist in production distributed setups. CA is theoretically possible only when **no partition ever occurs**, which is not realistic in real networks. **(Lose 1 mark here.)**

---

### QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) — Alternative Selection

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss *Failure Handling* in distributed systems. Explain different types of failures and the strategies used to mask them.

**Model Answer:**

**Why Failure Handling is Hard:**
In a distributed system, **partial failures** are the norm: some components fail while others keep running. Detecting failure is non-trivial because:
- Networks can be slow, lost, or duplicate messages.
- There is **no global clock** to distinguish "slow" from "failed."
- Failure detectors can produce **false positives** (suspecting a live node).

**Classification of Failures:**

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Transient** | Temporary, self-recovering. | A dropped TCP packet. |
| **Intermittent** | Repeats irregularly. | Loose network cable. |
| **Permanent** | Requires human intervention. | Hard disk crash. |

**By Scope:**
- **Process failure** — a node crashes.
- **Communication failure** — messages lost, delayed, or duplicated.
- **Byzantine failure** — node behaves arbitrarily (sends conflicting data to different peers).

**Masking Strategies:**

1. **Retransmission with timeout** — resend lost messages; idempotent operations.
2. **Redundancy** — replicate data and services across nodes.
3. **Checkpointing & rollback recovery** — periodically save state; on failure, roll back to the last checkpoint.
4. **Consensus protocols** — Paxos, Raft ensure agreement despite failures.
5. **Heartbeat mechanisms** — periodic liveness checks; suspect nodes after $T$ missed beats.

**Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT):**
Some systems (e.g., blockchain, aerospace) must tolerate **Byzantine failures** where nodes may act maliciously. Solutions use $3f + 1$ replicas to tolerate $f$ Byzantine nodes (PBFT algorithm).

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the *Quality of Service* challenge. Discuss how SLAs, admission control, and resource reservation help in meeting QoS requirements.

**Model Answer:**

**Quality of Service (QoS)** refers to non-functional guarantees that a distributed system provides to its clients. It encompasses:
- **Reliability** — system availability, MTBF, MTTR.
- **Performance** — latency, throughput, jitter.
- **Security** — CIA triad.
- **Timeliness** — meeting deadlines (real-time systems).
- **Adaptability** — reconfiguration under load.

**Service Level Agreements (SLAs):**
An SLA is a **contract** between service provider and client specifying measurable guarantees. Example: *"99.99% monthly availability, with response latency < 200 ms for 95% of API calls."*

Components of an SLA:
- **Service Level Objectives (SLOs)** — specific quantitative targets.
- **Service Level Indicators (SLIs)** — measured metrics.
- **Penalties** — credits or refunds for violations.

**Admission Control:**
Before admitting a new request, the system checks whether it can be served **without violating existing QoS guarantees**. If not, the request is **rejected or queued**. Examples:
- Web servers rejecting requests when thread pool is saturated.
- Phone networks refusing new calls when no channels are free.

**Resource Reservation:**
Clients reserve resources (CPU, memory, bandwidth) in advance. Examples:
- **RSVP** protocol in networks.
- **Kubernetes resource requests/limits**.
- **Real-time scheduling** in embedded distributed systems.

**Trade-off:**
Strict QoS guarantees **consume resources even when idle**, reducing overall system efficiency. Hence, providers often offer **tiered QoS** (Gold, Silver, Bronze).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Confusing **QoS with Quality of Experience (QoE)**. QoS is provider-side (latency, loss). QoE is user-perceived (subjective satisfaction). Examiners may test this distinction. **(Lose 1 mark if confused.)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Notes — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ The **8 classical challenges** are: Heterogeneity, Openness, Security, Scalability, Failure Handling, Concurrency, Transparency, Quality of Service.
- ✅ **Transparency** has **8 forms** as per ISO: Access, Location, Migration, Relocation, Replication, Concurrency, Failure, Persistence.
- ✅ **Security** rests on the **CIA triad** + **Authentication, Authorization, Non-repudiation**.
- ✅ **Scalability** has three axes: **Size, Geographic, Administrative**.
- ✅ **CAP Theorem** = pick 2 of {C, A, P}; in practice, **P is mandatory**, so the real choice is C vs A.
- ✅ **Failure types**: Transient, Intermittent, Permanent. **Byzantine** is the hardest.
- ✅ **Consensus impossibility**: FLP result — no deterministic consensus in purely asynchronous systems with even 1 crash fault.
- ✅ **Availability formula**: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- ✅ **Replica reliability**: $P(\text{at least one alive}) = 1 - (1 - r)^{k}$.
- ✅ **Three nines = 8.77 hours/yr**, **Four nines = 52.6 minutes/yr**, **Five nines = 5.26 minutes/yr** downtime.
- ✅ **Middleware** (CORBA, gRPC, RMI) bridges heterogeneity.
- ✅ **QoS mechanisms**: SLA, admission control, resource reservation.
- ✅ Always cite **at least one real-world example** per challenge in KTU ESE answers — this often distinguishes a 12-mark answer from a full-mark one.
- ✅ Draw **neat labelled diagrams** (CAP triangle, middleware architecture) for full marks in 7-mark sub-questions.
- ✅ Avoid saying "Middleware solves heterogeneity completely" — overstatement loses marks.

> **End of Notes — Module 1: Challenges in Distributed Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication in Distributed Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Communication in Distributed Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Communication in Distributed Systems** refers to the set of mechanisms, protocols, and middleware abstractions that enable independent processes running on geographically dispersed, heterogeneous machines to exchange data, coordinate actions, and invoke services across a network. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, communication in distributed systems is studied across three primary paradigms: **Interprocess Communication (IPC)**, **Remote Invocation**, and **Indirect Communication**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module classifies communication into the following hierarchy: **(a) Layered Protocols** (OSI vs. TCP/IP), **(b) Types of Communication** (Persistent vs. Transient, Asynchronous vs. Synchronous), **(c) Remote Procedure Call (RPC)**, **(d) Remote Method Invocation (RMI)**, and **(e) Message-oriented and Stream-oriented Communication (Middleware)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **postal courier system** operating between two international cities:
- **Letters (Messages)** are written using a specific language (data format) and packed into standardized envelopes (protocols).
- The **postal department (Network Layer)** handles routing, addressing, and delivery guarantees (TCP reliability vs. UDP speed).
- **Registered post with acknowledgment (RPC/RMI)** ensures the recipient confirms receipt and replies — like a phone call.
- **Group broadcast (Multicast)** is like addressing a circular to an entire apartment complex.
- **A notice board (Message Queues / MOM)** is a mailbox where anyone can leave or pick up messages asynchronously, without the sender and receiver being present at the same time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this topic critical for KTU exams?**
> Communication forms the **backbone of all distributed computing**. Without standardized communication, processes cannot synchronize, share data, or invoke remote services. The KTU examiner frequently tests the difference between RPC and RMI, marshalling techniques, and the layered protocol stack.

### 1.3 Fundamental Communication Paradigms

| Paradigm | Core Idea | KTU Exam Relevance |
|----------|-----------|--------------------|
| **Interprocess Communication (IPC)** | Message passing between processes on same/different hosts | Low |
| **Remote Invocation (RPC/RMI)** | Calling procedures/objects across the network | Very High |
| **Indirect Communication (MOM)** | Decoupled communication via queues, topics, tuples | High |
| **Stream-oriented Communication** | Continuous data flow (audio/video, sensor data) | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** A typical LAN round-trip latency is **≤ 1 ms**, whereas a transcontinental WAN latency is **80–150 ms**. This 100× difference dictates whether we use synchronous RPC or asynchronous message passing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Communication Protocols

Distributed systems rely on a **protocol stack** to structure communication. The two reference models are the **OSI 7-Layer Model** and the **TCP/IP 4-Layer Model**.

#### 2.1.1 OSI Model (Open Systems Interconnection)
- **Layer 7 — Application:** User-level services (HTTP, FTP, SMTP)
- **Layer 6 — Presentation:** Data formatting, encryption, compression (XDR, ASN.1)
- **Layer 5 — Session:** Dialog control, checkpointing
- **Layer 4 — Transport:** End-to-end reliability (TCP, UDP)
- **Layer 3 — Network:** Routing, logical addressing (IP)
- **Layer 2 — Data Link:** Framing, MAC addressing (Ethernet)
- **Layer 1 — Physical:** Electrical/optical signal transmission

#### 2.1.2 TCP/IP Model (4 Layers)
- **Application Layer** (combines OSI 5/6/7)
- **Transport Layer** (TCP — connection-oriented; UDP — connectionless)
- **Internet Layer** (IP — best-effort packet delivery)
- **Network Access / Link Layer** (combines OSI 1/2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** In the OSI model, the **Transport Layer (L4)** is the most critical for distributed systems because it offers **end-to-end** service primitives. The IP layer below is **unreliable** and offers only best-effort delivery.

### 2.2 Types of Communication

The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies communication along **two orthogonal axes**:

**Axis 1 — Persistence:**
- **Persistent Communication:** Message is stored by the communication system until delivery, even if sender/receiver crash. (Example: Email, message queues)
- **Transient Communication:** Message is discarded if receiver is not running. (Example: Standard UDP socket)

**Axis 2 — Timing:**
- **Synchronous (Blocking):** Sender blocks until message is received or replied to.
- **Asynchronous (Non-blocking):** Sender continues execution immediately after dispatching the message.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** RPC is generally classified as **transient + synchronous**. Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM) provides **persistent + asynchronous** communication. This four-cell matrix is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---------|----------------|-------------|------|
| **End-to-End Delay** | $D_{total} = T_{transmit} + T_{propagate} + T_{queue} + T_{process}$ | Total time for a packet to travel from source to destination | seconds (s) |
| **Transmission Time** | $T_{transmit} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | L = packet size, R = link bandwidth | seconds |
| **Propagation Time** | $T_{propagate} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | d = distance, s = signal propagation speed (~2×10⁸ m/s in fiber) | seconds |
| **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = R \times RTT$ | Maximum amount of unacknowledged data "in flight" | bits |
| **RPC Stub Overhead** | $T_{call} = 2 \times T_{marshal} + 2 \times T_{network} + T_{execute}$ | Total RPC call latency | seconds |
| **Throughput (Little's Law)** | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{N}{T_{service}}$ | N = concurrent requests, T = service time | requests/sec |
| **Marshalling Cost** | $C_{marshal} = k \cdot n$ | k = cost per field, n = number of fields | CPU cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are essential for **performance engineering** of distributed systems. For example, in **Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)**, propagation delay dominates for cross-continental requests, whereas in **data center RPCs** (e.g., Google's gRPC), serialization cost dominates.

### 2.4 The RPC and RMI Mechanisms (Deep Dive)

#### 2.4.1 Remote Procedure Call (RPC)
RPC allows a program to invoke a function on a remote machine as if it were a local function call. The mechanism is implemented through **stubs** generated by an interface definition language (IDL) compiler.

**Steps of an RPC:**
1. **Client stub (proxy)** marshals parameters into a network message.
2. **Marshalling** converts in-memory data structures into a flat byte stream (External Data Representation).
3. The **client runtime** invokes the transport layer (e.g., TCP).
4. The **server stub (skeleton)** unmarshals the byte stream into local data structures.
5. The **server procedure** executes.
6. Results are marshalled and returned to the client.

#### 2.4.2 Remote Method Invocation (RMI)
RMI is the **object-oriented variant** of RPC, specific to Java (and similar languages). It allows invocation of methods on remote **objects** living in different Java Virtual Machines. Key differences from RPC:

| Feature | RPC | RMI |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Paradigm | Function-based | Object-based |
| Parameter Passing | Pass-by-value | Pass-by-value (primitives) / Pass-by-reference (remote objects) |
| IDL | Sun XDR, DCE IDL | Java interfaces |
| Location Transparency | Partial | Higher (registry-based lookup) |
| Runtime | Platform-independent | JVM-specific |
| Garbage Collection | None | Distributed garbage collection via reference counting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** RPC is **language-agnostic** and uses external IDL (XDR/IDL), whereas RMI is **language-specific** (typically Java) and uses interfaces and object references. RPC treats remote calls like procedure calls; RMI treats them like object method invocations with state.

### 2.5 External Data Representation and Marshalling

To send structured data across heterogeneous machines, in-memory representations must be converted to a neutral **external data representation (XDR)**. Common standards include:

- **Sun XDR** (External Data Representation) — used in classic RPC
- **ASN.1 (BER/PER)** — used in SNMP and telecom
- **CORBA CDR** (Common Data Representation) — for CORBA middleware
- **JSON / XML** — modern, human-readable, web-friendly
- **Protocol Buffers (protobuf)** — Google's binary, schema-driven format
- **Apache Avro** — schema evolution-friendly
- **MessagePack** — compact binary JSON alternative

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** In modern microservices, **gRPC + Protocol Buffers** is the de facto standard (used by Netflix, Google, Kubernetes) because protobuf offers **~3-10× smaller payloads** and **~20-100× faster serialization** compared to JSON/XML.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End RPC Latency

**Problem Setup:** A client in Bangalore (India) invokes an RPC to a server in Frankfurt (Germany). Compute the total RPC call latency for a payload of L = 1 KB over a link of R = 10 Mbps bandwidth, with d = 7,500 km fiber distance, RTT = 120 ms, and stub marshalling cost = 0.5 ms each.

**Step 1 — Transmission Time (Request):**
The time to push 1 KB onto the wire.

$$
T_{tx,req} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1024 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}} = \frac{8192}{10^{7}} \text{ s}
$$

Converting to microseconds:

$$
T_{tx,req} = 0.0008192 \text{ s} = 819.2 \, \mu s \approx 0.819 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Network Round Trip (Propagation + Queue + Server Processing):**
The client must wait for the request to reach the server, the server to process, and the response to come back.

$$
T_{network} = RTT = 120 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Marshalling Overhead (Twice: Client-side and Server-side):**
Both the client stub (encoding request) and the server stub (encoding response) consume CPU.

$$
T_{marshal,total} = 2 \times T_{marshal} = 2 \times 0.5 \text{ ms} = 1.0 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Transmission Time (Response):**
The server returns another 1 KB payload, incurring the same transmission cost.

$$
T_{tx,resp} = 0.819 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Total RPC Latency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{RPC} &= T_{marshal,client} + T_{tx,req} + T_{network} + T_{execute} + T_{marshal,server} + T_{tx,resp} \\
&= 0.5 + 0.819 + 120 + 0 + 0.5 + 0.819 \\
&= 122.638 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Network propagation (RTT) **dominates** the total latency. For cross-continental RPCs, reducing marshalling cost from 0.5 ms to 0.05 ms only saves **~0.9 ms (< 1%)** of the total. The lesson is that engineers should focus on **batching requests, compressing payloads, or moving servers closer** to clients, not on micro-optimizing serialization.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

**Problem Setup:** A high-speed link has bandwidth R = 1 Gbps and round-trip time RTT = 200 ms. How much unacknowledged data can the sender push onto the network before it must stop and wait for acknowledgments?

**Step 1 — Identify the variables:**
- $R = 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^{9} \text{ bits/s}$
- $RTT = 200 \text{ ms} = 0.2 \text{ s}$

**Step 2 — Apply the BDP formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BDP &= R \times RTT \\
&= 10^{9} \text{ bits/s} \times 0.2 \text{ s} \\
&= 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to megabytes:**

$$
BDP = \frac{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits/byte} \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes/MB}} = 25 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** If the TCP receive window is smaller than 25 MB, the link will be **idle** while waiting for ACKs. This is why high-performance systems (e.g., data center fabrics, wide-area replication) use **TCP window scaling**, **RDMA**, or **custom protocols like QUIC**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Communication Paradigm Selection

The following decision matrix helps select the appropriate communication mechanism for a given distributed system requirement:

| Requirement | Best Choice | Justification |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| Need strong typing + objects | **RMI** | Object semantics preserved |
| Cross-language, function-style | **RPC (gRPC)** | IDL-based, language-agnostic |
| Decoupled, fire-and-forget | **Message Queue (RabbitMQ, Kafka)** | Persistent + asynchronous |
| Real-time, low-latency | **UDP / WebSockets** | Low overhead, no handshake |
| Many-to-many pub/sub | **Topic-based MOM / Multicast** | Decouples publishers and subscribers |
| Continuous media (audio/video) | **Stream protocols (RTP/RTSP)** | Time-sensitive, tolerant of loss |
| Group coordination | **Multicast (IP Multicast / gossip)** | Efficient 1-to-N delivery |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping (CO1):** This analytical matrix directly maps to **CO1: "Apply the concepts of communication in distributed systems"** by demonstrating the engineer's ability to make design trade-offs based on system requirements.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OSI vs. TCP/IP Layered Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the OSI 7-layer model to the TCP/IP 4-layer model, highlighting how distributed system communication flows through these layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["OSI L7 APPLICATION HTTP FTP SMTP DNS"]
    B["OSI L6 PRESENTATION TLS SSL XDR ASN1"]
    C["OSI L5 SESSION RPC SESSIONS"]
    D["OSI L4 TRANSPORT TCP UDP SCTP"]
    E["OSI L3 NETWORK IPV4 IPV6 ICMP"]
    F["OSI L2 DATA LINK ETHERNET PPP WIFI"]
    G["OSI L1 PHYSICAL FIBER COPPER RADIO"]
    H["TCP IP APPLICATION LAYER HTTP DNS MQTT"]
    I["TCP IP TRANSPORT LAYER TCP UDP"]
    J["TCP IP INTERNET LAYER IP ICMP"]
    K["TCP IP LINK LAYER ETHERNET ARP"]

    A --> H
    B --> H
    C --> H
    D --> I
    E --> J
    F --> K
    G --> K
```

### 4.2 RPC Operational Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

The following diagram illustrates the **end-to-end flow of an RPC call**, including marshalling, stub generation, and parameter transmission.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["CLIENT APPLICATION calls function foo"]
    C2["CLIENT STUB MARSHALL parameters into bytes"]
    C3["CLIENT RUNTIME invokes TCP SEND"]
    C4["NETWORK TRANSIT request message"]
    C5["SERVER RUNTIME receives via TCP"]
    C6["SERVER STUB UNMARSHALL bytes to structs"]
    C7["SERVER PROCEDURE executes foo"]
    C8["SERVER STUB MARSHALL return value"]
    C9["SERVER RUNTIME sends response"]
    C10["CLIENT RUNTIME receives response"]
    C11["CLIENT STUB UNMARSHALL return value"]
    C12["CLIENT APPLICATION receives result"]

    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6 --> C7
    C7 --> C8 --> C9 --> C4
    C4 -.-> C10 --> C11 --> C12
```

### 4.3 Communication Paradigm Hierarchy

The following diagram classifies the three core paradigms of communication in distributed systems as defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R["COMMUNICATION IN DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS"]
    P1["IPC MESSAGE PASSING"]
    P2["REMOTE INVOCATION"]
    P3["INDIRECT COMMUNICATION"]

    P1 --> P1A["SOCKETS UDP TCP"]
    P1 --> P1B["MULTICAST IP MULTICAST"]

    P2 --> P2A["RPC STRUCTURAL PROCEDURE BASED"]
    P2 --> P2B["RMI OBJECT ORIENTED JAVA"]

    P3 --> P3A["MOM MESSAGE QUEUES RABBITMQ KAFKA"]
    P3 --> P3B["PUB SUB TOPIC BASED"]
    P3 --> P3C["TUPLE SPACES LINDA"]
    P3 --> P3D["STREAM ORIENTED RTP WEBRTC"]
    P3 --> P3E["SHARED MEMORY DSM"]

    R --> P1
    R --> P2
    R --> P3
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: gRPC Communication Pipeline

For modern distributed microservices, the gRPC + Protocol Buffers stack represents the most widely deployed RPC variant. The following Mermaid block diagram details the functional flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["PROTO SCHEMA FILE service proto"]
    S2["PROTOC COMPILER generates stubs"]
    S3["CLIENT STUB IN TARGET LANGUAGE"]
    S4["PROTOBUF SERIALIZATION to binary"]
    S5["HTTP 2 TRANSPORT LAYER TLS ENCRYPTED"]
    S6["NETWORK TRANSIT"]
    S7["SERVER STUB UNMARSHALL"]
    S8["PROTOBUF DESERIALIZATION"]
    S9["SERVER HANDLER EXECUTES BUSINESS LOGIC"]
    S10["RESPONSE PROTOBUF SERIALIZATION"]
    S11["HTTP 2 RESPONSE BACK TO CLIENT"]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10 --> S11 --> S3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
*Distinguish between transient and persistent communication in distributed systems. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Transient Communication (1½ marks):** The message is kept in the communication subsystem's memory only as long as the sender and receiver are both executing. If the receiver is not running, the message is **discarded**. Example: Standard UDP socket communication; classic RPC calls.
- **Persistent Communication (1½ marks):** The message is stored by the middleware in a queue or buffer **until it is delivered**, even if the sender and receiver are not online simultaneously. This provides delivery guarantees. Example: Email (SMTP); Message Queues (RabbitMQ, Kafka).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
*Compare Remote Procedure Call (RPC) and Remote Method Invocation (RMI) in three points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

| Aspect | RPC | RMI |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Paradigm** | Function/procedure-based, language-agnostic | Object-oriented, typically Java-specific |
| **Parameter Passing** | Pass-by-value only | Pass-by-value (primitives) and pass-by-reference (remote objects) |
| **IDL/Definition** | External IDL (XDR, DCE IDL, .proto) | Java interfaces with `extends Remote` |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam Style**

**Q3a. [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
*Explain the layered architecture of communication in distributed systems. Compare the OSI 7-layer model with the TCP/IP 4-layer model with a neat diagram.*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Communication in distributed systems requires a **protocol stack** to enable heterogeneous machines to interoperate. The two reference models are **OSI** (theoretical, ISO-standard) and **TCP/IP** (practical, internet-defining).

**OSI 7 Layers (3 marks — ½ mark per layer with example):**
1. **Application (L7):** End-user services. *Example:* HTTP, SMTP, FTP.
2. **Presentation (L6):** Data format translation, encryption, compression. *Example:* TLS, XDR, ASN.1.
3. **Session (L5):** Dialog management and checkpointing. *Example:* NetBIOS, RPC sessions.
4. **Transport (L4):** End-to-end process-to-process delivery. *Example:* TCP (reliable), UDP (unreliable).
5. **Network (L3):** Logical addressing and routing. *Example:* IPv4, IPv6, OSPF.
6. **Data Link (L2):** Framing and MAC addressing. *Example:* Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11).
7. **Physical (L1):** Bit-level electrical/optical signaling. *Example:* Fiber, copper, radio waves.

**TCP/IP 4 Layers (2 marks):**
1. **Application Layer** — Maps to OSI L5/6/7 (HTTP, DNS, SSH).
2. **Transport Layer** — Maps to OSI L4 (TCP, UDP).
3. **Internet Layer** — Maps to OSI L3 (IP, ICMP, ARP).
4. **Link / Network Access Layer** — Maps to OSI L1/2 (Ethernet, Wi-Fi).

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | OSI | TCP/IP |
|---------|-----|--------|
| Layers | 7 | 4 |
| Origin | ISO standard (1984) | ARPANET / DARPA (1970s) |
| Adoption | Reference model | Practical implementation |
| Top-down design | Yes | Bottom-up design |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students merely *list* the OSI layers without providing **examples or protocols** for each. This loses 1-2 marks. Always pair each layer with **at least one real protocol** (e.g., L4 → TCP/UDP).

---

**Q3b. [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
*With a neat block diagram, explain the working of Remote Procedure Call (RPC). Discuss the role of stubs and marshalling.*

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2 (RPC Operational Flow)** of these notes for the diagram.

**Step-by-Step Working (4 marks):**
1. **Client invokes** the procedure `foo(x, y)` as if it were local.
2. **Client stub (proxy)** intercepts the call. It **marshals** the parameters `x` and `y` into a standardized external data representation (XDR/protobuf).
3. The **client runtime** opens a TCP connection to the server and transmits the message.
4. The **server runtime** receives the message and dispatches it to the **server stub (skeleton)**.
5. The **server stub unmarshals** the byte stream into local data structures, then calls the **actual server procedure** `foo(x, y)`.
6. The server executes the procedure and returns the result, which is **marshalled** by the server stub and sent back to the client.
7. The **client stub unmarshals** the reply and returns it to the calling program.

**Role of Stubs (½ mark):**
- Stubs provide **location transparency** — the client and server are unaware of each other's network location, OS, or hardware.
- Stubs handle **marshalling, parameter passing, and call dispatching**.

**Role of Marshalling (½ mark):**
- Marshalling converts **in-memory data structures** (which may differ in byte order, alignment, pointer structure between machines) into a **flat, network-ordered byte stream** that can be transmitted and reconstructed at the other end.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss:** Students frequently **omit the IDL compiler step**, where stub code is generated from an interface definition. The KTU examiner expects you to mention: **IDL → Interface Definition Language → IDL Compiler → Generates Stubs**. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4a. [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
*Describe the architecture and working of Remote Method Invocation (RMI) in Java. Highlight the differences between RMI and RPC.*

**Model Answer:**

**RMI Architecture (3 marks):**
RMI has three layers:
1. **Stub/Skeleton Layer:** The **stub** (client-side proxy) and **skeleton** (server-side dispatcher) handle marshalling and method dispatching.
2. **Remote Reference Layer:** Manages the lifecycle of remote object references. Translates local references to remote object identifiers and handles **distributed garbage collection** via reference counting.
3. **Transport Layer:** Built on top of TCP/IP. Manages connection setup, message transmission, and connection multiplexing.

**Working of RMI (3 marks):**
1. The server creates a remote object implementing a `Remote` interface (e.g., `extends UnicastRemoteObject`).
2. The server **binds** the object to a name in the **RMI Registry** (a naming service on a well-known port, default 1099).
3. The client looks up the remote object via `Naming.lookup("rmi://server/ObjectName")`.
4. The client invokes methods on the returned stub. The stub marshals arguments, sends the call over TCP, and the server skeleton unmarshals and invokes the actual method.
5. Results are returned via the same path, and reference counts are updated for distributed GC.

**Differences from RPC (1 mark):**

| Aspect | RMI | RPC |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Type System** | Objects, classes, inheritance | Plain functions, structs |
| **Parameter Passing** | By value (primitives) and by reference (remote objects) | By value only |
| **Lookup** | Registry-based naming service | Static or port-mapped |

---

**Q4b. [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
*Explain Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM) and stream-oriented communication. Discuss their relevance in modern distributed applications.*

**Model Answer:**

**Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM) (3½ marks):**
MOM is a category of middleware that provides **asynchronous, persistent, decoupled** communication between distributed applications. Components:

- **Message Queues (Point-to-Point):** Messages are placed in a queue; one consumer retrieves and processes each message. *Example:* RabbitMQ, Amazon SQS.
- **Publish/Subscribe (Topic-based):** Publishers send messages to a topic; all subscribers to that topic receive a copy. *Example:* Apache Kafka, Google Pub/Sub.
- **Message Brokers:** Centralized servers (e.g., ActiveMQ, Kafka brokers) that route, store, and filter messages.

**Key Properties (1 mark):**
- **Persistence:** Messages survive sender/receiver crashes.
- **Asynchrony:** Sender and receiver do not need to be online simultaneously.
- **Decoupling:** Sender does not know the identity or number of receivers.

**Stream-Oriented Communication (2 marks):**
Stream-oriented communication is used for **continuous, time-sensitive data** such as audio, video, and sensor streams. Key protocols:
- **RTP (Real-time Transport Protocol):** Carries real-time audio/video over UDP.
- **RTSP (Real-Time Streaming Protocol):** Controls playback of streams (VCR-like operations).
- **WebRTC:** Browser-based peer-to-peer real-time communication.
- **Apache Kafka Streams / Apache Flink:** Continuous data processing pipelines.

**Properties:** Loss-tolerant, delay-sensitive, ordered, often one-way.

**Relevance in Modern Applications (½ mark):**
- **MOM** powers event-driven microservices, IoT pipelines, financial trading systems, and asynchronous workflows.
- **Streams** enable video conferencing (Zoom), live broadcasting (YouTube Live), and real-time analytics (fraud detection, monitoring).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students confuse **MOM with message passing**. Key distinction: **MOM uses a third-party broker** (e.g., Kafka broker, RabbitMQ server), while **raw message passing** uses direct socket-to-socket communication. Without mentioning the **broker**, the answer is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Preparation:**

- [x] **Three Paradigms:** IPC, Remote Invocation (RPC/RMI), Indirect Communication (MOM, streams, DSM).
- [x] **OSI 7 layers vs. TCP/IP 4 layers** — always provide protocol examples.
- [x] **Persistent vs. Transient** — message survives or dies with the receiver.
- [x] **Synchronous vs. Asynchronous** — sender blocks or proceeds.
- [x] **RPC = Function-based**, language-agnostic, uses IDL (XDR, .proto).
- [x] **RMI = Object-based**, Java-specific, uses registry for lookup, supports remote references and distributed GC.
- [x] **Marshalling** converts in-memory objects ↔ flat byte streams (XDR, CDR, protobuf, JSON, XML).
- [x] **Stubs** = client-side proxies that handle marshalling and provide location transparency.
- [x] **MOM** = asynchronous, persistent, broker-based (Kafka, RabbitMQ).
- [x] **Stream protocols** = RTP, RTSP, WebRTC for real-time media.
- [x] **End-to-end delay formula:** $D_{total} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{queue} + T_{process}$.
- [x] **Bandwidth-Delay Product:** $BDP = R \times RTT$ determines optimal TCP window size.
- [x] **For cross-continental RPCs, network RTT dominates** total latency; optimize for **batching, compression, and edge placement** rather than serialization speed.
- [x] **gRPC + Protocol Buffers** is the modern industry standard (vs. XML-RPC / JSON-RPC).
- [x] **Multicast** enables efficient 1-to-N communication (IP multicast, application-level gossip).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** When answering 14-mark questions, always combine **a diagram (2 marks)** + **step-by-step explanation (4-5 marks)** + **comparison table (2-3 marks)** + **real-world example (1 mark)**. This structure consistently scores full marks in KTU board evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interprocess Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interprocess Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Interprocess Communication (IPC)** is the fundamental mechanism by which cooperating processes within a distributed system exchange data, synchronize their execution, and coordinate their activities across heterogeneous network boundaries. In the context of the KTU PECPT742 syllabus, IPC encompasses the complete set of *software-level protocols, middleware abstractions, and operating-system primitives* that enable two or more processes—residing on the same host or on different hosts connected through a network—to communicate reliably and meaningfully.

According to the standard KTU reference framework (Tanenbaum & Van Steen, *Distributed Systems: Principles and Paradigms*—used as the canonical reference for PECPT742), IPC is structured around three foundational abstractions:

1. **Data Transfer** – the movement of discrete or continuous data units (messages, byte streams) between a sender and one or more receivers.
2. **Synchronization** – the coordination of relative timing between processes so that race conditions, deadlocks, and inconsistent states are avoided.
3. **Logical Organization** – the structuring of communication into layers, protocols, and higher-level abstractions (sockets, RPC, message queues).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Definition:** IPC is *NOT* just about sending bytes from Point A to Point B. It is a *layered, protocol-driven, middleware-facilitated* discipline that guarantees **correctness**, **ordering**, **fault-tolerance**, and **performance** in a distributed environment.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal System

Imagine a multinational organization where branch offices in **Kochi**, **Bengaluru**, and **Chennai** must collaborate on a single project. They cannot simply shout across continents; they need a structured, rule-based communication system.

| Real-World Analogy | Distributed System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Letter sealed in an envelope | Message with header + payload |
| Postal address (PIN code) | IP address + Port number |
| Registered post vs ordinary post | Reliable (TCP) vs Unreliable (UDP) communication |
| Speed post vs local courier | High-bandwidth vs low-bandwidth channels |
| Notice board pinned to a wall | Shared memory (in distributed context) |
| Telephone call (real-time) | Synchronous communication |
| Email (fire-and-forget) | Asynchronous communication |
| Conference call (many-to-many) | Multicast / group communication |
| The Postal Department itself | Middleware (the layer that abstracts complexity) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Just as a postal system has rules for *envelope format*, *addressing*, *priority*, *tracking*, and *return-to-sender*, distributed IPC has rules (protocols) for *message formatting*, *routing*, *priority*, *acknowledgement*, and *error recovery*. The *Middleware* sits between the application process and the network, much like the post office sits between you and the recipient.

---

## 1.3 Why IPC is Indispensable in Distributed Systems

A *distributed system* is a collection of independent processes communicating over a network to appear as a single coherent system to the user (the classical *transparency* goal of KTU Module 1). Without IPC, this is impossible.

The three core imperatives for IPC are:

- **Data Exchange** – moving computed results, queries, or sensor readings between processes.
- **Coordination** – enforcing *mutual exclusion*, electing leaders, achieving consensus.
- **Resource Sharing** – accessing remote files, databases, printers, and services.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** Every other concept in PECPT742—Remote Procedure Calls (RPC), Remote Method Invocation (RMI), Publish/Subscribe systems, Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM), and Distributed Objects—is *built on top of* a foundational IPC layer. Master IPC and the rest of the course becomes intuitive.

---

## 1.4 The OSI-Aligned Communication Stack (High-Level View)

IPC in distributed systems maps onto (and extends) the classical **OSI/TCP-IP layered model**:

$$ \text{Application} \rightarrow \text{Presentation} \rightarrow \text{Session} \rightarrow \text{Transport} \rightarrow \text{Network} \rightarrow \text{Data Link} \rightarrow \text{Physical} $$

The KTU 2024 scheme places particular emphasis on the **Transport Layer** (TCP/UDP) and the **Middleware Layer** (sockets, RPC, message queues) because these are the layers where the *distributed* character of the system is defined.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Latency** — the time delay between initiating a request and receiving the first response, typically measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Bandwidth** — the maximum rate at which data can be transmitted, typically measured in **bits per second (bps)** or **Megabits per second (Mbps)**.
> - **Jitter** — the variation in latency over time, measured in **ms**.
> - **Throughput** — the actual achieved data rate, measured in **bytes/second**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Communication Models

KTU Module 1 categorizes IPC into three principal models, each with distinct semantics:

### 2.1.1 Message Passing
- Processes send and receive discrete messages through *send()* and *receive()* primitives.
- Each message is a self-contained, addressable unit of data.
- Used in nearly all modern distributed systems (HTTP, MQTT, gRPC, Kafka).
- **Variants:**
  - *Datagram-style* (UDP-like) — connectionless, best-effort, low overhead.
  - *Stream-style* (TCP-like) — connection-oriented, ordered, reliable, higher overhead.

### 2.1.2 Shared Memory
- A common address space is mapped into the address space of multiple processes.
- Communication is performed via *read* and *write* operations on shared variables.
- The KTU syllabus notes that *true* shared memory is *non-existent* across machines; what is offered instead is **Distributed Shared Memory (DSM)**, which simulates it through message passing under the hood.
- **Synchronization Primitives Required:** semaphores, monitors, condition variables.

### 2.1.3 Middleware-Based / Indirect Communication
- Communication occurs *through an intermediary* — a message broker, queue, or event bus.
- Examples: RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka, MQTT brokers, ZeroMQ.
- Decouples sender and receiver in *space* (no need to know each other) and *time* (no need to be online simultaneously).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU 2024 question paper often asks students to *compare* these three models. The key discriminating factors are: **coupling**, **performance**, **fault-tolerance**, and **scalability**.

---

## 2.2 Classification Axes for IPC (KTU's "Five-Dimensional" Model)

KTU Module 1 frames IPC design decisions along **five orthogonal axes**. Mastering these will allow you to answer almost any Part-A (3-mark) definition question:

| # | Axis | Two Extreme Choices | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Persistence** | Transient vs Persistent | Speed vs Durability |
| 2 | **Synchronicity** | Asynchronous vs Synchronous | Responsiveness vs Coordination |
| 3 | **Addressing** | Direct vs Indirect | Tight coupling vs Loose coupling |
| 4 | **Topology** | Unicast vs Multicast/Broadcast | 1-to-1 vs 1-to-many |
| 5 | **Delivery Guarantee** | Best-effort vs Reliable (with ordering) | Performance vs Correctness |

### 2.2.1 Persistence
- **Transient Communication:** A message exists *only* while both sender and receiver are executing. If the receiver is offline, the message is *lost*.
- **Persistent Communication:** A message is *stored* by the middleware until the receiver is ready to consume it (e.g., a message queue).

### 2.2.2 Synchronicity
- **Asynchronous Send:** Sender proceeds immediately after handing the message to the OS/middleware.
- **Synchronous Send:** Sender *blocks* until the message is *received* (or at least *delivered to the receiver's buffer*).
- **Synchronous Receive:** Receiver blocks until a message arrives.

### 2.2.3 Addressing
- **Direct:** Sender explicitly names the receiver (e.g., `send(process_id_P, message)`).
- **Indirect:** Sender deposits the message in a *channel, queue, or topic*; the receiver subscribes (e.g., publish-subscribe).

### 2.2.4 Topology
- **Unicast (1-to-1):** Point-to-point delivery.
- **Broadcast (1-to-all):** Delivered to every process in the network.
- **Multicast (1-to-m):** Delivered to a *group* of processes that have joined a multicast group.

### 2.2.5 Delivery Guarantees
- **Best-effort:** No acknowledgement, no retransmission, no ordering.
- **Reliable with FIFO Ordering:** Messages from a single sender are delivered in send order.
- **Causal Ordering:** If event A causally precedes event B, A is delivered first.
- **Total Ordering:** All receivers see messages in the *same global order*.

---

## 2.3 The Core Performance Formulas (KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET — IPC PERFORMANCE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Message Transmission Time** | $T_{\text{msg}} = L + \dfrac{S}{B}$ | seconds (s) | $L$ = latency, $S$ = size, $B$ = bandwidth |
| 2 | **Total Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $T_{\text{RTT}} = 2L + \dfrac{S}{B_{\text{up}}} + \dfrac{S}{B_{\text{down}}} + T_{\text{proc}}$ | seconds (s) | Includes processing time at receiver |
| 3 | **Effective Throughput** | $B_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{S}{T_{\text{RTT}}}$ | bytes/second (B/s) | Measures real achievable bandwidth |
| 4 | **Pipeline Speedup (n messages)** | $T_{\text{pipeline}} = L + \dfrac{n \cdot S}{B}$ | seconds (s) | One-way pipelined, $L$ is initial only |
| 5 | **Amdahl's IPC bound** | $S_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - p}$ | dimensionless | $p$ = parallel fraction; caps distributed speedup |
| 6 | **Stop-and-Wait Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{S/B}{L + S/B} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | fraction ($0 \le U \le 1$) | $a = \dfrac{L \cdot B}{S}$ is the bandwidth-delay product ratio |
| 7 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** | $\text{BDP} = L \cdot B$ | bits | Minimum bytes "in flight" to fill the pipe |
| 8 | **Maximum Achievable Throughput** | $B_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{B}{1 + 2a}$ | bits/second (bps) | Stop-and-wait protocol upper bound |

> **Pipe-Character Avoidance Notice:** All absolute-value and conditional expressions in this table use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation written as `\vert` to preserve the markdown table structure.

### 2.3.1 Derived Nomenclature
- **Latency ($L$):** Time for the *first bit* to travel from sender to receiver.
- **Bandwidth ($B$):** Number of bits per second the link can sustain *after* the first bit arrives.
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP):** The number of bits that can "fill" the network pipe; determines the optimal TCP window size.
- **Stop-and-Wait:** A naive protocol where the sender waits for ACK before sending the next message; wastes bandwidth when $a = L \cdot B / S \gg 1$.

---

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Walkthrough — Why Protocol Design Matters

Let us walk through the *why* of a fundamental design choice: **Why use a sliding-window protocol instead of stop-and-wait?**

1. **Step 1 — The Problem:** In a WAN with $L = 80$ ms RTT and $B = 100$ Mbps, sending a 1 KB message via stop-and-wait yields:
2. **Step 2 — Compute the ratio:**
   $a = \dfrac{L \cdot B}{S} = \dfrac{0.08 \times 100 \times 10^{6}}{1024 \times 8} \approx 977$

3. **Step 3 — Compute utilization:**
   $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 1954} \approx 0.000512$

   This means only **0.05 % of the link is utilized**. The link is *idle* 99.95 % of the time.
4. **Step 4 — The Fix:** Use a **sliding-window** protocol with window size $W \ge 2a + 1$ to keep the pipe full. This raises $U \to 1$.
5. **Step 5 — The Real-World Mapping:** TCP's flow control uses a sliding window whose size dynamically tracks the BDP, achieving near-optimal utilization.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The same formulas explain *why* HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 use *multiplexing* and *stream pipelining* — to overcome the head-of-line blocking inherent in older protocols. They also justify the design of **QUIC**, which eliminates the handshakes and uses UDP-based multiplexing.

---

## 2.5 IPC Protocol Layering in Distributed Systems

| Layer | Function | Real-World Examples | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | Business logic, API calls | HTTP, gRPC, MQTT | Tanenbaum §2.x |
| **Middleware / Marshalling** | Encoding, stub generation | Protobuf, Avro, CORBA | Module 1 emphasis |
| **Transport Layer** | Reliable byte streams / datagrams | TCP, UDP, QUIC | Module 1 core |
| **Network Layer** | Routing, addressing | IPv4, IPv6, IPsec | Foundational |
| **Link / Physical Layer** | Electrical / optical signaling | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, fiber | Background |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Mapping:** The KTU PECPT742 Module 1 focuses primarily on the **Application**, **Middleware**, and **Transport** layers. Lower layers are assumed to be functional abstractions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Message Transmission Time

We will derive the *total elapsed wall-clock time* for transmitting a message of size $S$ bytes over a link of bandwidth $B$ bits/second with one-way latency $L$ seconds.

**Given:**
- $S$ = message size in bytes
- $B$ = link bandwidth in bits/second
- $L$ = one-way propagation + serialization latency in seconds

**Find:** $T_{\text{msg}}$, the time from the moment `send()` is called to the moment the last bit arrives at the receiver.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Convert size from bytes to bits.

$$ S_{\text{bits}} = 8 \cdot S $$

Explanation: There are **8 bits per byte**, so multiplying by 8 converts the byte count to a bit count.

Step 2: Compute the *serialization delay* — the time it takes to push all bits onto the wire.

$$ T_{\text{ser}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{bits}}}{B} = \dfrac{8 \cdot S}{B} $$

Explanation: Bandwidth $B$ is the rate at which bits leave the sender; dividing total bits by rate gives the time required.

Step 3: Combine with propagation latency. The message is *fully transmitted* only after the last bit has both *been serialized* and *propagated* across the link. For one-way transmission, the last bit's arrival time is the sum of the *initial* propagation delay (when the first bit starts traveling) plus the serialization of the rest.

$$ T_{\text{msg}} = L + T_{\text{ser}} = L + \dfrac{8 \cdot S}{B} $$

Step 4: Sanity check. As $S \to 0$, $T_{\text{msg}} \to L$ — the message arrives after pure propagation delay, as expected for an infinitely small packet. As $L \to 0$, $T_{\text{msg}} \to 8S/B$ — pure serialization, as expected for a zero-latency link.

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{T_{\text{msg}} = L + \dfrac{8 S}{B}} $$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Stop-and-Wait Protocol Utilization

We derive the maximum link utilization $U$ of the *Stop-and-Wait* ARQ protocol.

**Given:**
- $L$ = one-way latency
- $B$ = bandwidth
- $S$ = message size in bits

**Assumption:** The ACK is negligibly small (classical assumption).

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Total cycle time. The sender sends the message, waits for the ACK, and then can send the next. The round-trip is $2L$ (one-way there, one-way back). The sender's "data transmission" portion takes $S/B$ seconds.

$$ T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{S}{B} + 2L $$

Step 2: Define the *useful data transmission fraction* (utilization).

$$ U = \dfrac{\text{Time spent sending data}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \dfrac{S/B}{S/B + 2L} $$

Step 3: Multiply numerator and denominator by $B/S$ to normalize.

$$ U = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2L \cdot B}{S}} $$

Step 4: Define the **bandwidth-delay ratio** $a$ as a compact notation.

$$ a = \dfrac{L \cdot B}{S} $$

Step 5: Substitute $a$ into the expression.

$$ U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} $$

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}} $$

Step 6: Threshold analysis. When $a \ll 1$ (small bandwidth-delay product, e.g., LAN), $U \to 1$ — stop-and-wait is fine. When $a \gg 1$ (WAN/satellite), $U \to 0$ — the protocol is catastrophically inefficient. The *minimum* sliding-window size to achieve $U = 1$ is:

$$ W_{\min} = 2a + 1 = \dfrac{2 L B}{S} + 1 $$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Total-Order Broadcast Cost

A common KTU problem: compute the *message complexity* of achieving **total-order broadcast** in a group of $N$ processes using a sequencer-based approach.

**Setup:** One designated process (the *sequencer*) assigns a sequence number to every broadcast message. Receivers deliver in sequence-number order.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: For each broadcast, the original sender transmits to the sequencer. This is **1** message.

Step 2: The sequencer assigns a sequence number and multicasts to all $N-1$ other group members. This is **1** multicast = conceptually $N-1$ point-to-point messages, but counted as **1** in *multicast complexity*.

Step 3: Each of the $N-1$ receivers sends an ACK back to the sequencer. This is $N-1$ unicast ACKs.

Step 4: Total message count per broadcast.

$$ M_{\text{total}} = 1 + 1 + (N-1) = N + 1 $$

Step 5: In the multicast-notation convention (counting one multicast as 1), this simplifies to $O(N)$ messages and $O(\log N)$ with hierarchical sequencers.

$$ \boxed{M_{\text{total-broadcast}} = N + 1 \text{ messages (unicast view)},\quad O(1) \text{ multicasts}} $$

Step 6: Compare with the naive approach — sender unicasts to all $N-1$ receivers — which also costs $N-1$ unicast messages but provides *no* global ordering.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Trade-offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 EXAMINATION COMPARISON MATRIX** — Print this out. It is worth a 14-mark question almost every semester.

| Property | Transient + Sync | Transient + Async | Persistent + Async | Persistent + Sync |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** | Tight (temporal) | Loose (temporal) | Very loose (space + time) | Tight (space) |
| **Failure Handling** | Poor — message lost on receiver crash | Poor — same | Excellent — message stored | Good — message stored |
| **Performance** | High (small msgs) | High (overlap) | Medium (broker overhead) | Medium |
| **Scalability** | Low | Medium | High | Low |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | Low | High (need broker) | Medium |
| **Example** | TCP `send/recv` with blocking | TCP non-blocking | Kafka, RabbitMQ | RPC over a queue |
| **When to Use** | Tight client-server RPC | Fire-and-forget telemetry | Audit logs, e-commerce orders | Critical transactions |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style)

**Problem:** A process $P_1$ in **Kerala** sends a 64 KB message to process $P_2$ in **Bengaluru**. The one-way latency is $L = 40$ ms and the link bandwidth is $B = 10$ Mbps. The receiver sends back a 64-byte ACK. Compute:
1. The one-way message transmission time.
2. The total RTT (including ACK).
3. The effective throughput.
4. The bandwidth-delay product (BDP).

**Solution:**

Step 1: Convert units.

$$ S = 64 \text{ KB} = 64 \times 1024 \times 8 = 524288 \text{ bits} $$

$$ S_{\text{ACK}} = 64 \times 8 = 512 \text{ bits} $$

$$ B = 10 \text{ Mbps} = 10 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s} $$

$$ L = 40 \text{ ms} = 0.04 \text{ s} $$

Step 2: One-way transmission time (ignoring ACK).

$$ T_{\text{msg}} = L + \dfrac{S}{B} = 0.04 + \dfrac{524288}{10^{7}} $$

$$ T_{\text{msg}} = 0.04 + 0.0524 \approx 0.0924 \text{ s} = 92.4 \text{ ms} $$

Step 3: Total RTT (forward path + ACK return + processing time $T_{\text{proc}} \approx 0$).

$$ T_{\text{RTT}} = 2L + \dfrac{S}{B} + \dfrac{S_{\text{ACK}}}{B} $$

$$ T_{\text{RTT}} = 0.08 + 0.0524 + 0.0000512 \approx 0.1324 \text{ s} = 132.4 \text{ ms} $$

Step 4: Effective throughput.

$$ B_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{S}{T_{\text{RTT}}} = \dfrac{524288 \text{ bits}}{0.1324 \text{ s}} $$

$$ B_{\text{eff}} \approx 3.96 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} \approx 3.96 \text{ Mbps} $$

Step 5: Bandwidth-delay product.

$$ \text{BDP} = L \cdot B = 0.04 \times 10^{7} = 4 \times 10^{5} \text{ bits} = 50 \text{ KB} $$

**Interpretation:** The pipe can hold 50 KB "in flight." Since our message is 64 KB, the link is *almost* full with a single message. If we were to send 10 such messages, a sliding window of $W = 2$ would be sufficient.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IPC Architecture — The Layered Block Diagram

This is the **canonical IPC architecture** that the KTU board examiners expect students to draw (or describe) in a 7-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef appStyle fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef midStyle fill:#A0D8F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef transStyle fill:#B6E2A1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef netStyle fill:#F0A0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    APP_A["APPLICATION PROCESS A"]:::appStyle
    APP_B["APPLICATION PROCESS B"]:::appStyle
    MID_A["MIDDLEWARE A  Stub Marshaller Transport Adapter"]:::midStyle
    MID_B["MIDDLEWARE B  Stub Marshaller Transport Adapter"]:::midStyle
    TRANS_A["TRANSPORT LAYER  TCP OR UDP"]:::transStyle
    TRANS_B["TRANSPORT LAYER  TCP OR UDP"]:::transStyle
    NET_A["NETWORK LAYER  IP ROUTING"]:::netStyle
    NET_B["NETWORK LAYER  IP ROUTING"]:::netStyle

    APP_A --> MID_A
    MID_A --> TRANS_A
    TRANS_A --> NET_A
    NET_A -- "PHYSICAL LINK FIBER OR WIRELESS" --> NET_B
    NET_B --> TRANS_B
    TRANS_B --> MID_B
    MID_B --> APP_B
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Application process A invokes a *send* call. The data is wrapped by the *Middleware A* (stub generated by an IDL compiler, marshalled into a wire format such as Protobuf, then handed to the transport adapter). The *Transport Layer* (TCP) adds headers, sequences, and reliability. The *Network Layer* (IP) routes the packet across the physical link to process B, where the stack is *unwound in reverse*. The middleware decodes the message and the application process B receives a faithful copy of the original data.

---

## 4.2 The Five-Axis IPC Decision Flow

This diagram captures the *decision tree* a system designer follows when choosing an IPC mechanism — a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef qStyle fill:#FFF4B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef aStyle fill:#C0EBC0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    START(["CHOOSE AN IPC MECHANISM"]):::qStyle
    Q1{"MUST MESSAGE SURVIVE RECEIVER CRASH"}:::qStyle
    PERS["PERSISTENT  MESSAGE QUEUE OR KAFKA"]:::aStyle
    Q2{"DOES SENDER NEED REPLY"}:::qStyle
    SYNC["SYNCHRONOUS  RPC OR RMI"]:::aStyle
    ASYNC["ASYNCHRONOUS  FIRE AND FORGET"]:::aStyle
    Q3{"HOW MANY RECEIVERS"}:::qStyle
    UNI["UNICAST  SOCKETS OR PIPES"]:::aStyle
    MULTI["MULTICAST  IP MULTICAST OR GROUPS"]:::aStyle
    Q4{"MUST ALL RECEIVERS SEE SAME ORDER"}:::qStyle
    TOT["TOTAL ORDER BROADCAST  SEQUENCER"]:::aStyle
    BEST["BEST EFFORT  UDP DATAGRAM"]:::aStyle

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- "YES" --> PERS
    Q1 -- "NO" --> Q2
    Q2 -- "YES" --> SYNC
    Q2 -- "NO" --> ASYNC
    ASYNC --> Q3
    SYNC --> Q3
    PERS --> Q3
    Q3 -- "ONE" --> UNI
    Q3 -- "MANY" --> MULTI
    MULTI --> Q4
    Q4 -- "YES" --> TOT
    Q4 -- "NO" --> BEST
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Each yellow diamond is a design question; each green rectangle is the resulting architectural choice. A student who can reproduce this tree in an exam demonstrates complete mastery of the syllabus's classification axes.

---

## 4.3 Message Flow — Synchronous RPC Sequence

This block diagram shows the *temporal sequence* of messages in a synchronous Remote Procedure Call — a critical concept for Module 1's RPC sub-topic.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CL as CLIENT PROCESS
    participant CMID as CLIENT MIDDLEWARE
    participant SRV as SERVER PROCESS
    participant SMID as SERVER MIDDLEWARE

    CL->>CMID: 1 INVOKE procedure args
    CMID->>CMID: 2 MARSHALL parameters to bytes
    CMID->>SRV: 3 SEND request message
    SMID->>SMID: 4 UNMARSHALL parameters
    SMID->>SRV: 5 DISPATCH to local procedure
    SRV->>SMID: 6 RETURN result
    SMID->>CMID: 7 SEND reply message
    CMID->>CMID: 8 UNMARSHALL result
    CMID->>CL: 9 DELIVER result to caller
```

**Reading the Sequence:** Notice that the *caller blocks* from Step 1 to Step 9 — the synchronous nature is explicit. The two expensive operations are *marshalling* (Steps 2, 8) and *network round-trip* (Steps 3, 7). KTU examiners love asking students to identify which step is the *bottleneck* in high-latency scenarios (the answer: the network round-trip, which is why pipelining and batching matter).

---

## 4.4 Group Communication Topologies

This block-level functional diagram contrasts the three principal group-communication patterns.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef srcStyle fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef destStyle fill:#A0D8F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    S["SENDER PROCESS"]:::srcStyle
    D1["RECEIVER 1"]:::destStyle
    D2["RECEIVER 2"]:::destStyle
    D3["RECEIVER 3"]:::destStyle
    D4["RECEIVER 4"]:::destStyle
    D5["RECEIVER 5"]:::destStyle

    S -- "UNICAST" --> D1
    S -- "BROADCAST TO ALL" --> D2
    S -- "BROADCAST TO ALL" --> D3
    S -- "MULTICAST TO GROUP G" --> D4
    S -- "MULTICAST TO GROUP G" --> D5
```

**Engineering Insight:** *Unicast* to $N$ receivers requires $N$ messages. *Broadcast* to all uses one network-level broadcast, but in practice IP-routed networks often emulate it via $N$ unicasts. *Multicast* uses one packet and the network replicates it efficiently at routers. This is why video-streaming services and stock-ticker systems prefer IP multicast.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A.1 — Definition Type `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Interprocess Communication. List and briefly explain any two classification axes for IPC in distributed systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Interprocess Communication (IPC) refers to the set of mechanisms and protocols that allow cooperating processes, executing on the same or different machines, to exchange data, synchronize their execution, and coordinate their actions over a network.

**Axis 1 — Persistence (1 mark):** Communication is classified as *transient* if the message exists only while both sender and receiver are running, and *persistent* if the message is stored by the middleware until the receiver is ready to consume it (e.g., message queues).

**Axis 2 — Synchronicity (1 mark):** Communication is *synchronous* if the sender blocks until the message is received, and *asynchronous* if the sender continues immediately after handing the message to the OS.

> [!TIP]
> **Marker Logic:** Award the definition 1 mark, then 1 mark each for the two axes with a valid explanation. A 2-line answer for each axis is sufficient.

---

### Question A.2 — Conceptual `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between transient and persistent communication. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Transient | Persistent |
|---|---|---|
| Message Lifetime | Exists only while sender and receiver are running | Stored in middleware until consumed |
| Failure Recovery | Lost on receiver failure | Survives receiver failure |
| Coupling | Tight (temporal) | Loose (space + time) |
| Example (1 mark) | TCP socket communication | Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, email |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "TCP is persistent" — TCP is reliable, but the message is *not* stored beyond the lifetime of the connection; it is transient.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the protocol layer:** Students often describe IPC as *just* the application layer, ignoring the transport and middleware layers. Always mention the *stack* (Application → Middleware → Transport → Network).
> 2. **Confusing "reliable" with "persistent":** A protocol can be reliable (no loss) yet transient (no storage). Distinguish these.
> 3. **No diagram:** The KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly allocates *1 to 2 marks* for a labeled diagram. Always include a flowchart or sequence diagram.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical problems:** Writing $L = 40$ instead of $L = 40 \text{ ms}$. Markers routinely deduct ½ mark for missing units.
> 5. **No example:** Every IPC concept must be backed by a real-world example (TCP, HTTP, Kafka, etc.). Abstract answers lose marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full KTU Board Pattern `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks] Explain in detail the different communication models used in distributed systems. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.**

**(b) [7 marks] With a suitable block diagram, describe the layered architecture of IPC in a distributed system. Explain the role of middleware in this architecture.**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Communication Models (7 marks)

There are three principal communication models in distributed systems:

**1. Message Passing (2 marks)**
- Processes communicate by sending and receiving discrete messages through `send()` and `receive()` primitives.
- Two sub-variants: **datagram** (connectionless, unreliable, low overhead — e.g., UDP) and **stream** (connection-oriented, reliable, ordered — e.g., TCP).
- **Advantages:** Simple, scalable, works across heterogeneous networks.
- **Disadvantages:** Marshalling/unmarshalling overhead; no implicit data sharing.

**2. Shared Memory (2 marks)**
- A common address space is mapped into the address space of multiple processes, who communicate via reads and writes.
- True shared memory is impossible across machines; **Distributed Shared Memory (DSM)** simulates it via message passing.
- **Advantages:** Familiar programming model, fast on a single node.
- **Disadvantages:** Synchronization complexity (semaphores, monitors), coherence overhead in DSM.

**3. Indirect Communication (3 marks — emphasize this)**
- Communication occurs *through an intermediary* — message broker, queue, tuple space, or event bus.
- Sub-variants: *message queues* (point-to-point), *publish-subscribe* (topic-based), *tuple spaces* (associative).
- **Advantages:** Loose coupling in time and space; high scalability; built-in fault tolerance.
- **Disadvantages:** Broker is a single point of failure (unless replicated); higher latency; broker software cost.

**Summary Comparison Table (built into the answer):**

| Criterion | Message Passing | Shared Memory | Indirect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coupling | Medium | Tight | Loose |
| Scalability | High | Low | Very High |
| Performance | High | Highest (local) | Medium |
| Fault tolerance | Low (transient) | Low | High (persistent) |

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — IPC Layered Architecture (7 marks)

**Diagram (2 marks):** The student must draw a labeled layered diagram similar to the one in Section 4.1 of these notes. The expected layers are:

1. **Application Layer** — the user's process (e.g., a Java program calling `requestService()`).
2. **Middleware / Stub Layer** — the *interface definition language* (IDL) compiler-generated stub and skeleton; the marshaller that converts data to wire format (Protobuf, Avro, CORBA CDR).
3. **Transport Layer** — TCP (reliable stream) or UDP (datagram) — provides end-to-end delivery.
4. **Network Layer** — IP routing across the internetwork.
5. **Link / Physical Layer** — the actual medium (Ethernet, fiber, Wi-Fi).

**Role of Middleware (3 marks):**

The middleware is the *glue* that hides the heterogeneity of networks, hardware, and operating systems. Specifically, it provides:

- **Location Transparency** — the caller does not need to know the server's IP/port.
- **Data Marshalling** — converts in-memory objects to a network byte-order wire format.
- **Protocol Abstraction** — provides higher-level abstractions (RPC, RMI, pub/sub) on top of raw sockets.
- **Security** — authentication, encryption, and access control.
- **Reliability** — retries, timeouts, and acknowledgements.

**Two real-world examples (2 marks):**
- *CORBA* uses IDL to define interfaces and generates stubs/skeletons.
- *gRPC* uses Protobuf for marshalling and HTTP/2 for transport.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks] Explain the TCP and UDP transport protocols as they relate to IPC. Compare them on at least five parameters.**

**(b) [7 marks] What is group communication? Explain the various message-ordering semantics used in group communication with examples.**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — TCP vs UDP (7 marks)

**TCP — Transmission Control Protocol (3 marks):**
- *Connection-oriented:* A 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) establishes a virtual connection.
- *Reliable:* Sequence numbers, acknowledgements, retransmissions, and checksums guarantee no-loss, in-order delivery.
- *Byte-stream oriented:* The application sees a continuous stream of bytes with no message boundaries.
- *Flow and congestion control:* Sliding window protocol (Section 2.3 formulas apply) prevents overwhelming the receiver or the network.
- *Higher overhead:* 20-byte header, state tables at endpoints.
- *Use cases:* Web (HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2), email (SMTP), file transfer (FTP).

**UDP — User Datagram Protocol (3 marks):**
- *Connectionless:* No handshake; each datagram is independent.
- *Unreliable:* No acknowledgements, no retransmissions, no ordering.
- *Message-oriented:* Each `sendto()` call produces one datagram; boundaries preserved.
- *Low overhead:* 8-byte header, no per-connection state.
- *Use cases:* DNS, video streaming, VoIP, online gaming, multicast.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Reliable (ACK + retransmit) | Unreliable (best-effort) |
| Ordering | In-order | No ordering guarantee |
| Speed | Slower (overhead) | Faster |
| Header size | 20–60 bytes | 8 bytes |
| Use case | HTTP, FTP, SMTP | DNS, video, gaming |

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Group Communication & Ordering (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Group communication is the dissemination of messages from one process to a *set* of processes (the *group*) that have subscribed to receive such messages.

**Message-Ordering Semantics (5 marks):**

1. **No-Order / Best-Effort Multicast (1 mark):**
   - No delivery guarantees; the cheapest and fastest.
   - Example: standard IP multicast.

2. **FIFO Ordering (1 mark):**
   - Messages from a single sender are delivered in the order they were sent.
   - Different senders' messages may interleave arbitrarily.
   - Example: a chat application guarantees that messages from user A arrive in A's send order, but A's and B's messages may be interleaved.

3. **Causal Ordering (1 mark):**
   - If event A *causally precedes* event B (A happened before B in the *Lamport* sense), then every receiver delivers A before B.
   - Example: in a collaborative editor, a "create document" event is causally before "edit document"; the edit must be delivered only after the create is delivered.

4. **Total Ordering (2 marks):**
   - All receivers see all messages in the *same global order*, regardless of the sender.
   - Implementation typically uses a *sequencer* or *Paxos-based* agreement.
   - Example: a distributed database replicating state-machine transitions — every replica must apply transactions in the identical order to remain consistent.

**Diagram or Example (1 mark):** A simple timeline showing three processes and three messages, with FIFO vs total-order differences, is sufficient.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION — IPC MODULE 1**
> - **IPC =** Data transfer + Synchronization + Logical organization.
> - **3 communication models:** Message Passing, Shared Memory (incl. DSM), Indirect (queue/pub-sub).
> - **5 design axes:** Persistence, Synchronicity, Addressing, Topology, Delivery Guarantee.
> - **TCP vs UDP:** Connection+reliable vs connectionless+best-effort.
> - **Unicast = 1-to-1, Multicast = 1-to-m, Broadcast = 1-to-all.**
> - **Message time formula:** $T_{\text{msg}} = L + 8S/B$ — memorize this.
> - **Stop-and-wait utilization:** $U = 1/(1+2a)$ — when $a \gg 1$ the protocol is useless.
> - **Sliding window size needed:** $W = 2a + 1$ to keep the pipe full.
> - **BDP** = $L \times B$ bits — defines the *in-flight* capacity of the network.
> - **Group ordering hierarchy:** Best-effort $<$ FIFO $<$ Causal $<$ Total.
> - **Middleware =** stub + marshaller + transport adapter; provides *transparency*.
> - **Reliable ≠ Persistent** — TCP is reliable but transient; Kafka is reliable *and* persistent.
> - **Always draw a labeled diagram** in Part-B answers; the KTU key allots 1–2 marks for it.
> - **Always quote units** in numerical problems; missing units cost marks.
> - **Always give a real-world example** for every conceptual answer; abstract answers lose marks.
> - **Key text-reference chapter:** Module 1 is fully covered in the *Tanenbaum & Van Steen* "Communication" chapter (chapters 4–5 in the 3rd edition) — although do not fabricate edition specifics in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Remote Procedure Call (RPC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Remote Procedure Call (RPC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remote Procedure Call (RPC)** is a powerful, high-level **communication paradigm** in distributed systems that implements the concept of *procedure-oriented* message passing. It allows a program (the client) to call a procedure (subroutine) located in another address space (typically on a remote machine) in a manner that is **syntactically and semantically identical** to making a local procedure call. RPC achieves this by hiding the underlying network communication, data marshalling, and transport details behind a procedure call abstraction.

Formally, RPC is defined as the **synchronous** one-way transfer of control flow and data between two processes (or threads) residing in different address spaces, mediated by a network transport layer such as **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** or **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Telephone Translator Service"

Imagine you are in **Kerala** and you need a specific government certificate issued only by an office in **Delhi**. You cannot travel, so you hire a **Translator-Proxy (the Client Stub)**. You give your documents and your request to the translator in plain Malayalam. The translator converts everything into Hindi (the **network protocol**), calls the Delhi office, waits patiently for the response, translates the answer back to Malayalam, and hands it to you. To you, it *feels* like you directly spoke to the Delhi officer — but in reality, the entire heavy lifting was hidden.

In this analogy:
- **You (Client Process)** → call a normal function
- **The Function Call** → "give me the certificate"
- **Client Stub (Proxy)** → translator, packs and sends
- **Network (TCP/UDP)** → the telephone line
- **Server Stub (Skeleton)** → the Delhi interpreter receiving the call
- **Server Procedure** → the actual Delhi office officer

> [!NOTE]
