
Compile with `midl Bank.idl` → generates `Bank.h`, `Bank_i.c`, `Bank_p.c`, and `Bank.tlb` (type library).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** IDL/MIDL is the *only* KTU-friendly way to write code in the answer sheet. Always include `module` (CORBA) or `library` (DCOM) block to show the exam-style interface.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CORBA Reference Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientSide["CLIENT PROCESS"]
        AppC["Client Application Object"]
        StubC["Generated Stub Proxy"]
        ORBC["Client ORB Core"]
        AppC --> StubC
        StubC --> ORBC
    end

    subgraph Network["NETWORK - IIOP over TCP/IP"]
        IIOP["GIOP Messages: Request, Reply, Locate, Cancel, Fragment"]
    end

    subgraph ServerSide["SERVER PROCESS"]
        ORBS["Server ORB Core"]
        POA["Portable Object Adapter - POA"]
        SkelS["Generated Skeleton"]
        Servant["Servant Object - actual implementation"]
        ORBS --> POA
        POA --> SkelS
        SkelS --> Servant
    end

    subgraph Services["OBJECT SERVICES - Vertical Layer"]
        NS["Naming Service"]
        TS["Trading Service"]
        EvS["Event Service"]
        TxS["Transaction Service OTS"]
        SecS["Security Service CSIv2"]
    end

    ORBC <--> IIOP
    IIOP <--> ORBS
    ORBC -.uses.-> NS
    ORBS -.uses.-> NS
    ORBS -.uses.-> TS
    ORBS -.uses.-> TxS
    ORBC -.uses.-> SecS

    classDef core fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef svc fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#E5F9E5,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class ORBC,ORBS core
    class NS,TS,EvS,TxS,SecS svc
    class IIOP net
```

---

## 4.2 DCOM Reference Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientProc["CLIENT MACHINE - Windows"]
        AppD["Client Application"]
        StubD["DCOM Stub DLL - generated by MIDL"]
        COMC["COM Runtime - ole32.dll"]
        SCM_C["Service Control Manager - local"]
        AppD --> StubD
        StubD --> COMC
        COMC --> SCM_C
    end

    subgraph Transport["NETWORK"]
        MSRPC["MSRPC over TCP 135 plus dynamic port 1024-65535"]
    end

    subgraph ServerProc["SERVER MACHINE - Windows"]
        SCM_S["SCM Server"]
        COMS["COM Runtime"]
        ProxyD["DCOM Proxy DLL"]
        ServD["In-Proc COM Server DLL or EXE"]
        SCM_S --> COMS
        COMS --> ProxyD
        ProxyD --> ServD
    end

    subgraph RegD["WINDOWS REGISTRY"]
        CLSID["HKCR CLSID maps GUID to server path"]
        AppID["HKCR AppID stores security and identity"]
    end

    SCM_C <--> MSRPC
    MSRPC <--> SCM_S
    SCM_C -.reads.-> RegD
    SCM_S -.reads.-> RegD

    classDef dc fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dt fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef dr fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class COMC,COMS,SCM_C,SCM_S dc
    class MSRPC dt
    class RegD,CLSID,AppID dr
```

---

## 4.3 CORBA vs DCOM — Side-by-Side Comparison (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph C["CORBA"]
        C1["OMG Standard - Vendor Neutral"]
        C2["IDL - 50 plus languages"]
        C3["IIOP - Big Endian CDR"]
        C4["POA - Object Lifecycle"]
        C5["Naming, Trading, Events, OTS"]
        C6["Real-time variant - CORBA RT"]
    end

    subgraph D["DCOM"]
        D1["Microsoft Proprietary"]
        D2["MIDL - C++ VB C#"]
        D3["ORPC - Little Endian NDR"]
        D4["Class Factories - SCM"]
        D5["COM+ MTS, DCOM Security"]
        D6["No real-time variant"]
    end

    C1 -. contrasts .-> D1
    C2 -. contrasts .-> D2
    C3 -. contrasts .-> D3
    C4 -. contrasts .-> D4
    C5 -. contrasts .-> D5
    C6 -. contrasts .-> D6
```

---

## 4.4 Lifecycle of a CORBA Remote Method Call (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CL as Client App
    participant ST as Client Stub
    participant ORB1 as Client ORB
    participant ORB2 as Server ORB
    participant POA as POA
    participant SK as Skeleton
    participant SV as Servant

    CL->>ST: call method on proxy
    ST->>ST: marshal args using CDR
    ST->>ORB1: deliver CDR request
    ORB1->>ORB2: send IIOP Request message
    ORB2->>POA: dispatch to object
    POA->>SK: invoke skeleton
    SK->>SK: demarshal CDR
    SK->>SV: call real method
    SV-->>SK: return value or exception
    SK->>ORB2: marshal reply
    ORB2->>ORB1: IIOP Reply message
    ORB1->>ST: deliver reply
    ST-->>CL: return value to caller
```

---

## 4.5 DCOM Object Activation Sequence (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CL as Client
    participant COM1 as Client COM
    participant SCM1 as Client SCM
    participant SCM2 as Server SCM
    participant COM2 as Server COM
    participant OBJ as COM Object

    CL->>COM1: CoCreateInstanceEx(GUID)
    COM1->>SCM1: look up class via Registry
    SCM1->>SCM2: MSRPC activation request
    SCM2->>COM2: launch server EXE or DLL
    COM2->>OBJ: create object instance
    OBJ-->>COM2: return interface pointer
    COM2-->>SCM2: return marshalled IDispatch pointer
    SCM2-->>SCM1: activation response
    SCM1-->>COM1: return object pointer
    COM1-->>CL: return object pointer to caller
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]**

**Q: Define CORBA. With a neat diagram, explain the role of the ORB in a CORBA-based distributed system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **CORBA (Common Object Request Broker Architecture)** is the Object Management Group's (OMG) open, vendor-neutral specification for **language- and OS-independent distributed object middleware**.
>
> The **ORB (Object Request Broker)** is the runtime "software bus" that mediates all invocations between clients and remote objects. It performs:
>
> 1. **Marshalling / Unmarshalling** of parameters using **CDR**.
> 2. **Object reference resolution** using the Naming / Trading services.
> 3. **Transport** using the **IIOP** (Internet Inter-ORB Protocol) over TCP/IP.
> 4. **Activation of servants** via the **POA** (Portable Object Adapter).
>
> The ORB enables **location, language, and OS transparency** — the client sees a local proxy (stub) and does not need to know the server's address, language, or platform.

*(Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for ORB roles. Examiner expects at least 4 ORB functions.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**Q: Compare CORBA and DCOM in terms of language neutrality, OS portability, and wire protocol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **CORBA** | **DCOM** |
|---|---|---|
| **Language Neutrality** | **Full** — supports C++, Java, COBOL, Ada, Python, Ruby, Smalltalk (50+) | **Limited** — primarily C++, VB, later C# |
| **OS Portability** | **Full** — UNIX, Windows, VMS, mainframes, embedded RTOS | **Windows only** — tightly coupled to Win32/NT |
| **Wire Protocol** | **IIOP** (GIOP over TCP/IP), **big-endian CDR** | **ORPC** over **MSRPC** (DCE-RPC derivative), **little-endian NDR** |

*(Marks: 1 mark per row of comparison, OR 1 for language + 1 for OS + 1 for protocol.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 QUESTION A — Module 3 Choice Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, Module 3]**

**Q: (a)** Describe the **architecture of CORBA** with a neat diagram. Explain the functions of the **ORB, IDL, IIOP, and POA** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **DCOM architecture** in detail. Compare its **marshalling, security, and object activation** mechanisms with CORBA. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — CORBA Architecture (7 marks)

1. **Definition of CORBA (1 mark):**
   CORBA is the OMG's specification for distributed object middleware. The **Object Management Architecture (OMA)** consists of the ORB, Object Services, Common Facilities, and Application Objects.

2. **ORB Core (1 mark):**
   The ORB is the **transparent broker** that enables client invocations on remote objects. Functions:
   - **Marshalling/Unmarshalling** parameters using **CDR** encoding.
   - **Transport** using IIOP messages.
   - **Object reference activation** through adapters.
   - **Transparent location, language, and OS independence.**

3. **IDL (Interface Definition Language) (1 mark):**
   - Purely declarative, language-independent.
   - Defines interfaces, operations, attributes, exceptions, structs, sequences.
   - Compiled by vendor-specific IDL compilers (e.g., `idlj`, `omniidl`) to produce **stubs and skeletons** in the target language.

4. **IIOP (Internet Inter-ORB Protocol) (1.5 marks):**
   - Concrete instantiation of the abstract **GIOP** protocol.
   - Runs over **TCP/IP** — ensures different vendors' ORBs interoperate.
   - Carries 8 message types: `Request`, `Reply`, `LocateRequest`, `LocateReply`, `CancelRequest`, `CloseConnection`, `MessageError`, `Fragment`.

5. **POA (Portable Object Adapter) (1.5 marks):**
   - Replaced the older **BOA (Basic Object Adapter)**.
   - Manages **object activation, deactivation, and reference creation**.
   - Supports multiple policies: `RETAIN`, `USE_SERVANT_MANAGER`, `IMPLICIT_ACTIVATION`, `UNIQUE_ID`, `SYSTEM_ID`.
   - Allows multiple servants per object and multiple objects per servant.

6. **Diagram (1 mark):** As in Section 4.1.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — DCOM Architecture & Comparison (7 marks)

1. **DCOM Architecture (2 marks):**
   - DCOM is Microsoft's extension of **COM** for cross-machine remoting.
   - Built on **MSRPC** (Microsoft's DCE-RPC derivative).
   - **MIDL** compiler generates proxy/stub DLLs from `.idl` files.
   - Components are identified by **GUID/CLSID** registered in the **Windows Registry**.

2. **Marshalling (1.5 marks):**
   - DCOM uses **NDR (Network Data Representation)** — little-endian, allows padding.
   - CORBA uses **CDR (Common Data Representation)** — big-endian, no implicit padding.
   - Both carry **type information** for validation.

3. **Security (1.5 marks):**
   - DCOM: **Windows SSPI**, NTLM/Kerberos, 6 authentication levels (`None` → `PacketPrivacy`), per-CLSID launch/access permissions in Registry.
   - CORBA: **CSIv2 (Common Secure Interoperability v2)**, **SSLIOP**, vendor-specific (e.g., VisiBroker uses SPX).
   - CORBA security is **OS-neutral**; DCOM is **NT-only**.

4. **Object Activation (2 marks):**
   - DCOM: `CoCreateInstanceEx()` → Client SCM → MSRPC → Server SCM → Server COM runtime creates the object → returns marshalled interface pointer to client.
   - CORBA: Client calls stub → ORB looks up IOR via Naming/Trading → IIOP to server ORB → POA activates servant → skeleton dispatches → result returned.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B — Module 3 Choice Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Module 3]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **CORBA Object Management Architecture (OMA)**. Describe the **Object Services layer** with at least **6 service examples**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **MIDL**? Explain how DCOM generates **proxy/stub DLLs** from MIDL specifications. Discuss the role of the **Windows Registry** and **SCM** in DCOM activation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — CORBA OMA & Object Services (7 marks)

1. **OMA Layers (3 marks):**
   - **Application Objects** — user-written business components.
   - **Common Facilities** — horizontal application-level tools (e.g., document management).
   - **Object Services** — system-level services that any CORBA object may use.
   - **ORB Core** — runtime broker handling transport, marshalling, object references.

2. **Object Services Examples (4 marks) — list any 6:**
   - **Naming Service** — binds human-readable names to IORs.
   - **Trading Service** — dynamic service discovery with constraints.
   - **Event Service** — decoupled asynchronous event delivery.
   - **Notification Service** — typed event filtering, QoS.
   - **Transaction Service (OTS)** — ACID transactions across objects.
   - **Security Service (CSIv2)** — authentication, authorization, auditing.
   - **Lifecycle Service** — create, move, copy, remove objects.
   - **Persistent State Service (PSS)** — object state management.
   - **Concurrency Control Service** — locks, transactions.
   - **Externalization Service** — stream objects for storage/transmission.
   - **Relationship Service** — manage roles and typed relationships.
   - **Time Service** — synchronized clocks.
   - **Licensing Service** — usage control.
   - **Property Service** — dynamic attribute attachment.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — MIDL, Proxy/Stub, Registry, SCM (7 marks)

1. **MIDL Definition (1 mark):**
   **MIDL (Microsoft Interface Definition Language)** is DCOM's IDL analog. It is a DCE-IDL extension with COM-specific attributes like `[object]`, `[uuid]`, `[helpstring]`, `[pointer_default]`, and dual `IUnknown` / `IDispatch` interfaces.

2. **Proxy/Stub Generation (2 marks):**
   - The `midl` compiler takes `Bank.idl` and emits:
     - `Bank.h` — C/C++ header with interface declarations and GUIDs.
     - `Bank_i.c` — interface identifiers and proxy CLSIDs.
     - `Bank_p.c` — proxy/stub function bodies (using NDR).
     - `Bank.tlb` — binary type library, registered via `regtlib`.
   - The proxy DLL is **registered with COM**; when a client calls a remote method, COM loads the proxy DLL, which calls `IRpcChannelBuffer::SendReceive()`.

3. **Windows Registry (2 marks):**
   - The Registry (specifically `HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\CLSID\{GUID}`) maps each CLSID to:
     - The server EXE/DLL path.
     - `LocalServer32` (out-of-process) or `InprocServer32` (in-process).
     - `AppID` for security and identity.
     - `ProgID` for human-readable names.

4. **SCM — Service Control Manager (2 marks):**
   - On activation (`CoCreateInstanceEx`), the **client-side SCM** reads the Registry, then sends an **MSRPC activation request** to the **server-side SCM** (on port 135 / dynamic ports).
   - The **server SCM** launches the server EXE (or activates the in-proc DLL), creates the object, and returns a marshalled interface pointer to the client.
   - SCM also performs **pinging** for failure detection (exponential backoff: 30s → 60s → 120s → 240s → 480s → 600s cap).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points (Kerala University Exam Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing ORB with Stub:** The ORB is the *runtime broker*; the stub is a *language-specific proxy* generated from IDL. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting POA:** Many KTU students write "BOA" (Basic Object Adapter, deprecated since 1998). The **POA (Portable Object Adapter)** is the **correct modern term** in all answers.
> 3. **IIOP vs GIOP Confusion:** GIOP is *abstract* (transport-agnostic), IIOP is *concrete over TCP/IP*. Markers will deduct 0.5 marks for swapping them.
> 4. **DCOM "Distribution" Misconception:** DCOM does NOT just mean "remote COM" — it includes **location transparency, security pinging, dynamic activation, and protocol negotiation**. Writing only "DCOM = remote COM" loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Endianness Error:** CDR is **big-endian**, NDR is **little-endian**. Stating the wrong one costs a full mark.
> 6. **Missing Naming/Trading/OTS in CORBA services list:** A KTU exam often asks to "name 6 services" — listing 4 only loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **No diagram in long answers:** A **mandatory 1–2 mark allocation** is for a labelled diagram. Skipping it caps your score at 12/14.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **CORBA = OMG's open, language-agnostic, OS-agnostic distributed object middleware.**
- [x] **ORB** = runtime software bus handling marshalling, transport, object reference, activation.
- [x] **IDL** = language-neutral interface definition; compiled into stubs + skeletons.
- [x] **IIOP** = GIOP over TCP/IP; **GIOP** = abstract transport-agnostic protocol.
- [x] **CDR** = big-endian marshalling format; carries typecode.
- [x] **POA** = modern object adapter with policy-driven activation (replaces BOA).
- [x] **OMA = ORB + Object Services + Common Facilities + Application Objects**.
- [x] **Object Services** = Naming, Trading, Event, Notification, OTS (Transaction), Security (CSIv2), Lifecycle, Persistent State, Concurrency, Time, Property, etc.
- [x] **DCOM = Microsoft extension of COM for distributed computing over MSRPC**.
- [x] **MIDL** = DCOM's IDL; generates proxy/stub DLLs and `.tlb` type libraries.
- [x] **MSRPC** = Microsoft's DCE-RPC implementation (ORPC for objects).
- [x] **NDR** = little-endian marshalling format used by DCOM.
- [x] **Windows Registry + SCM** = DCOM's class-store and activation service.
- [x] **DCOM pinging** = exponential backoff (30→60→120→240→480→600 sec cap).
- [x] **DCOM security** = Windows SSPI, NTLM/Kerberos, 6 authentication levels.
- [x] **CORBA advantage:** heterogeneous, cross-platform, real-time variant.
- [x] **DCOM advantage:** tight OS integration, no extra ORB install (built into Windows).
- [x] **Modern successors:** CORBA → **gRPC, Thrift, Web Services**; DCOM → **WCF, .NET Remoting, gRPC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"CORBA is the open postal service for objects across the world; DCOM is the Windows-only postal service for Microsoft components."* Memorize this analogy — examiners love it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tolerance and Reliability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Fault Tolerance and Reliability # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Fault Tolerance** is the ability of a distributed system to continue providing its intended services to the user despite the occurrence of hardware or software faults. It is a critical non-functional property in distributed middleware design. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reliability** is formally defined as the probability that a system performs its intended function under designated operating conditions, for a specified period of time. It is measured as a continuous metric $R(t)$ — the probability of **no failure** occurring in the interval $[0, t]$. ### Foundational Vocabulary (Taxonomy of Failures) The KTU syllabus strictly differentiates between three nested terms: | Term | Formal Definition | Example | |------|------------------|---------| | **Fault** | The hypothesized cause of an error (root defect) | A bit-flip in RAM due to cosmic radiation | | **Error** | A deviation of the system state from its correct state | Corrupted memory word | | **Failure** | The inability of the system to deliver service conforming to its specification | Server crashes, wrong output | > [!NOTE] > **Hierarchical Rule:** A **fault** causes an **error**, and an **error** may cause a **failure**. Fault tolerance aims to **mask faults** before they propagate to failures. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airplane" Model Imagine a commercial aircraft flying from Delhi to London with 300 passengers. - **Reliability** is the probability the aircraft lands safely without any system failure. - **Fault Tolerance** is the design principle where the aircraft has **three independent flight computers**, **dual hydraulic systems**, and **four engines** — so even if two engines fail, the plane still lands safely. - A **fault** is a cracked turbine blade (latent defect). An **error** is the engine temperature reading showing abnormal values. A **failure** is the engine shutting down mid-flight. > [!IMPORTANT] > The airplane uses **redundancy** to achieve fault tolerance. In distributed systems, we apply the same idea: **replicate data, replicate services, and replicate processes**. --- ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield) The following metrics are standardized in KTU 2024 Scheme textbook and must be memorized: - **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** Average time a system operates before failing. - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average time taken to restore a failed system. - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ - **Availability ($A$):** Fraction of total time the system is operational. - **Failure Rate ($\lambda$):** Number of failures per unit time. - **Reliability Function $R(t)$:** Probability the system survives up to time $t$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard Formula:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for systems with a constant failure rate $\lambda$ (memoryless exponential distribution). --- ## 1.4 Types of Faults in Distributed Systems > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies faults along two axes: **temporal** (transient vs. permanent) and **behavioral** (crash vs. omission vs. Byzantine). | Fault Type | Description | Example | |------------|-------------|---------| | **Transient Fault** | Occurs once, then disappears | Network packet loss, temporary power glitch | | **Intermittent Fault** | Occurs repeatedly, unpredictably | Loose cable causing sporadic disconnects | | **Permanent Fault** | Continues until repair | Burnt-out disk drive, hardware component failure | | **Crash Fault (Fail-Stop)** | Process halts; others detect it | Server process killed | | **Omission Fault** | Process fails to send/receive messages | Buffer overflow, dropped RPC | | **Byzantine Fault** | Process produces arbitrary/malicious output | Compromised node, software bug | | **Timing Fault** | Output is correct but delivered too late | Network congestion, clock skew | | **Network Fault** | Partition, delay, duplication, reordering | Switch failure, link break | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Fault Tolerance in Distributed Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus groups fault tolerance mechanisms into three well-defined categories. Every exam question maps to one of these pillars. ### Pillar 1: **Process Resilience (Process Group Membership)** A process group is a collection of cooperating processes treated as a single logical entity. When a process fails, the group membership must be updated. - **Flat Groups:** All peers are equal; used in symmetric communication. - **Hierarchical Groups:** A coordinator mediates communication; prone to single-point failure. - **Group Membership Service (GMS):** Maintains a consistent view of active members across all nodes. **Steps to maintain consistency on a failure:** 1. **Failure Detection:** Use heartbeat protocols. 2. **Membership Agreement:** All surviving members agree on the new view. 3. **View Reconfiguration:** Group transitions to a new configuration synchronously. 4. **Recovery / Reintegration:** Failed (but recovered) processes can rejoin the group. --- ### Pillar 2: **Reliable Client–Server Communication (RPC-Level Fault Tolerance)** In RPC, three classes of failures occur: - **Client unable to locate server** → use **retry** with **timeout** and **exception handling**. - **Request message lost** → client retransmits after timeout. - **Server crashes after receiving request** → server's local state is uncertain. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse "at-least-once" with "exactly-once" semantics. The KTU examiner frequently tests this distinction. | Strategy | Behavior | Trade-off | |----------|----------|-----------| | **Maybe** | Server may or may not execute | Cheapest, unsafe | | **At-least-once** | Client retries until ACK; server may execute multiple times | Idempotent operations only | | **At-most-once** | Client marks request uniquely; server filters duplicates | Risk of lost execution | | **Exactly-once** | Guaranteed single execution (2PC-based) | Most expensive, used in banking | --- ### Pillar 3: **Reliable Group Communication** Used for **one-to-many** (broadcast) and **many-to-many** (multicast) communication, essential for replicated services. **Three reliability levels (per Hadzilacos & Toueg, 1994):** 1. **Reliable Broadcast:** All correct (non-faulty) processes deliver every broadcast message. 2. **Uniform Broadcast:** All processes — faulty or not — that delivered the message, did so in the same order. 3. **Atomic Broadcast:** Total order + Reliable broadcast — equivalent to a distributed agreement. **Causal Order vs. Total Order:** - **Causal Order ($\rightarrow$):** If send$(m_1) \rightarrow$ send$(m_2)$, then deliver$(m_1)$ at all common destinations precedes deliver$(m_2)$. - **Total Order:** For any two messages $m_1, m_2$ delivered at correct processes $P_i$ and $P_j$, the order is identical. --- ## 2.2 Distributed Commit Protocols ### The Two-Phase Commit (2PC) Protocol > [!IMPORTANT] > **2PC is a blocking protocol** when the coordinator fails. This is a favourite KTU 14-mark question. | Phase | Coordinator Action | Cohort Action | |-------|-------------------|---------------| | **Phase 1: Prepare** | Sends `VOTE-REQ` to all cohorts | Each cohort writes log; replies `YES` or `NO` | | **Phase 2: Commit/Abort** | If all YES → `GLOBAL-COMMIT`; if any NO → `GLOBAL-ABORT` | Cohorts execute and ACK | **Failure Scenarios in 2PC:** - **Cohort failure before vote** → coordinator aborts after timeout. - **Coordinator failure after vote** → cohorts are **blocked** waiting for decision. - **Network partition** → cohorts in minority side are blocked. ### The Three-Phase Commit (3PC) Protocol 3PC adds a **Pre-Commit** phase to eliminate the blocking problem of 2PC, but assumes **no network partition + bounded delays** (synchronous system). --- ## 2.3 Recovery Strategies > [!NOTE] > **Recovery** is the complementary side of fault tolerance: it restores the system to a consistent state after a failure. ### 2.3.1 Checkpointing - Stores the state of a process at regular intervals. - **Independent Checkpointing:** Each process saves its own state; risk of **domino effect**. - **Coordinated Checkpointing:** A coordinator triggers a global consistent snapshot. - **Communication-Induced Checkpointing:** Combines both — piggybacks checkpoint info on messages. ### 2.3.2 Message Logging - **Pessimistic Logging:** Log each message to stable storage before delivery. Enables recovery with no rollback of correct processes. - **Optimistic Logging:** Log asynchronously. Faster but may force orphan processes to roll back. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is essential for KTU Part B numerical/derivation questions. Commit to memory. | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Constant failure rate $\lambda$ | | 2 | Mean Time To Failure | $MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | For exponential distribution | | 3 | MTBF | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | Total cycle time | | 4 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTBF} = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | Steady-state fraction | | 5 | Series System Reliability | $R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Failure of any one fails system | | 6 | Parallel (Redundant) System | $R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i(t))$ | All must fail to fail system | | 7 | k-out-of-n System | $R_{k/n} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | At least $k$ must work | | 8 | MTTF of Series System | $MTTF_{series} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \prod_{i} e^{-\lambda_i t} \, dt = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \lambda_i}$ | Sum of failure rates | | 9 | Hazard Function | $h(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Instantaneous failure rate | | 10 | Survivability | $S(t) = R(t) + E(t)$ | $E(t)$ = graceful degradation term | > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by cloud providers (AWS, Azure) to publish SLA uptime guarantees (e.g., 99.99% = "four nines" availability). The same math governs RAID disk arrays, space-grade redundant computers, and telecom base stations. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Exponential Reliability Function We begin with the assumption that the failure rate $\lambda$ is **constant** (memoryless process — applicable to electronic components during their useful life, per the **bathtub curve**). **Step 1:** Express the probability of survival in the next instant $dt$. The probability the system survives an infinitesimal interval $dt$ is $1 - \lambda \, dt$, since $\lambda \, dt$ is the probability of failure in $dt$. **Step 2:** Write the differential equation governing $R(t)$. $$ \begin{aligned} R(t + dt) &= R(t) \cdot (1 - \lambda \, dt) \\ R(t + dt) - R(t) &= -\lambda \, R(t) \, dt \\ \frac{dR(t)}{dt} &= -\lambda \cdot R(t) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Solve the first-order linear ODE by separation of variables. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{dR}{R} &= -\lambda \, dt \\ \int \frac{dR}{R} &= \int -\lambda \, dt \\ \ln R(t) &= -\lambda t + C_1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Apply the initial condition $R(0) = 1$ (the system is certainly working at time 0). $$ \begin{aligned} \ln 1 &= 0 = -\lambda \cdot 0 + C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = 0 \\ R(t) &= e^{-\lambda t} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The reliability function under a constant failure rate is the **negative exponential**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) By definition, MTTF is the expected time of failure. $$ MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt $$ where $f(t)$ is the probability density function of failure time. We use the identity: $$ MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt $$ (This identity is a standard result: $E[X] = \int_0^\infty P(X > t) \, dt$ for non-negative random variables.) **Substituting** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$: $$ MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt $$ **Evaluating** the integral: $$ \begin{aligned} MTTF &= \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\ &= \left( \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda} \right) - \left( \frac{e^{0}}{-\lambda} \right) \\ &= 0 - \left( \frac{1}{-\lambda} \right) \\ &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ for an exponential failure distribution. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Availability in Steady State Let $U(t)$ be the probability that the system is up at time $t$ and $D(t)$ the probability it is down. Then $U(t) + D(t) = 1$. In **steady state**, $t \to \infty$, the availability $A$ is the long-run fraction of time the system is operational. Using renewal theory, a cycle consists of: - An "up" period of expected length $MTTF$. - A "down" (repair) period of expected length $MTTR$. The fraction of time the system is up is therefore: $$ A = \frac{\text{time up per cycle}}{\text{total time per cycle}} = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} = \frac{MTTF}{MTBF} $$ **Substituting** $MTTF = 1/\lambda$ and $MTBF = 1/\lambda + MTTR$: $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + MTTR} \\ &= \frac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot MTTR} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Insight:** To improve availability, we must **either** reduce $\lambda$ (better components) **or** reduce $MTTR$ (faster recovery mechanisms). The KTU examiner's favourite "design a high-availability system" question reduces to these two levers. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Parallel (Redundant) System Reliability Consider a system with $n$ independent components, all in **parallel**, such that the system works as long as **at least one** component works. Let $R_i(t)$ be the reliability of component $i$. The probability that **all** components fail is: $$ P(\text{all fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( 1 - R_i(t) \right) $$ By De Morgan's rule, the system reliability is: $$ R_{parallel}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( 1 - R_i(t) \right) $$ **Special case:** If all $n$ components are identical with reliability $R$, then: $$ R_{parallel}(t) = 1 - (1 - R)^n $$ **Example for KTU Numericals:** A replicated web server has 3 identical nodes, each with reliability $R = 0.95$. Compute the parallel system reliability. $$ \begin{aligned} R_{parallel} &= 1 - (1 - 0.95)^3 \\ &= 1 - (0.05)^3 \\ &= 1 - 0.000125 \\ &= 0.999875 \end{aligned} $$ > **Insight:** Even modest per-node reliability yields **five nines** availability under 3-way replication — this is exactly why Google, Amazon, and Microsoft deploy triplicated services. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Fault-Tolerance Techniques Mapped to Layer | OSI / Middleware Layer | Fault Type Addressed | Technique Used | KTU Mark Weightage | |-----------------------|----------------------|----------------|--------------------| | **Application Layer** | Software bugs, logic errors | N-version programming, exception handling | High (10–14 marks) | | **Middleware Layer** | RPC loss, server crash | Retries, idempotency, 2PC | Very High (14 marks) | | **Transport Layer** | Packet loss, duplication | TCP, sequence numbers, ACKs | Medium (7 marks) | | **Network Layer** | Routing failures, partitions | Multipath routing, BGP failover | Medium (3–7 marks) | | **Data Layer (Storage)** | Disk failure, corruption | RAID, replication, checksums | High (7–14 marks) | | **Process Layer** | Process crash | Process groups, restart, watchdog | High (7–14 marks) | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Fault Tolerance Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Client Request] --> B[Load Balancer] B --> C[Middleware RPC Layer] C --> D[Process Group Membership Service] D --> E1[Server Node 1] D --> E2[Server Node 2] D --> E3[Server Node 3] D --> E4[Server Node 4] E1 --> F1[Local Checkpoint] E2 --> F2[Local Checkpoint] E3 --> F3[Local Checkpoint] E4 --> F4[Local Checkpoint] E1 --> G1[Stable Storage Log] E2 --> G2[Stable Storage Log] E3 --> G3[Stable Storage Log] E4 --> G4[Stable Storage Log] E1 --> H[Heartbeat Monitor] E2 --> H E3 --> H E4 --> H H --> I[Failure Detector] I --> J[Recovery Manager] J --> D ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The client request enters via a load balancer, is dispatched through the RPC middleware, and is processed by a **process group** of four server nodes. Each node writes periodic **checkpoints** to local storage and logs messages to **stable storage**. A heartbeat monitor detects failures, the failure detector classifies the type of fault, and the recovery manager updates the group membership view. --- ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Two-Phase Commit (2PC) Protocol ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Coord as Coordinator participant C1 as Cohort 1 participant C2 as Cohort 2 participant C3 as Cohort 3 Coord->>C1: VOTE_REQUEST Coord->>C2: VOTE_REQUEST Coord->>C3: VOTE_REQUEST C1->>Coord: VOTE_COMMIT YES C2->>Coord: VOTE_COMMIT YES C3->>Coord: VOTE_ABORT NO Note over Coord,C3: Coordinator decides GLOBAL ABORT
due to Cohort 3 NO vote Coord->>C1: GLOBAL_ABORT Coord->>C2: GLOBAL_ABORT Coord->>C3: GLOBAL_ABORT C1->>Coord: ACK C2->>Coord: ACK C3->>Coord: ACK ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The coordinator initiates Phase 1 by sending `VOTE_REQUEST`. Each cohort responds with `VOTE_COMMIT` (yes) or `VOTE_ABORT` (no). Because Cohort 3 votes NO, the coordinator issues a `GLOBAL_ABORT` to all cohorts in Phase 2, which acknowledge and roll back. --- ## 4.3 State Machine — Process States in a Fault-Tolerant Group ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Active: Process starts Active --> Suspected: Heartbeat timeout Suspected --> Active: Heartbeat received Suspected --> Failed: Confirmed failure Failed --> Recovery: Restart initiated Recovery --> Active: Rejoin successful Active --> [*]: Graceful shutdown ``` > **Reading the diagram:** A process begins life in the `Active` state. When heartbeats from a peer time out, it transitions to `Suspected`. If heartbeats resume, it returns to `Active`; otherwise it is declared `Failed`. The recovery manager then restarts the process, which eventually rejoins as `Active`. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Differentiate between fault, error, and failure in a distributed system. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks — KTU marking scheme):** A **fault** is the *cause* of incorrect behavior in a system. **Errors** are the *manifestations* of faults — deviations from correct internal state. A **failure** occurs when the system deviates from its specified external behavior. | Term | Example | |------|---------| | Fault | A memory cell affected by electromagnetic interference (latent defect) | | Error | The contents of the cell become 0xFFFA instead of 0x0F0A (incorrect state) | | Failure | The application crashes or produces the wrong output (visible to user) | > **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for the formal definition of each term (3 × 1 mark); the example line is part of the 1 mark per term. --- ### Question 2: What is meant by "Byzantine fault"? Why is it considered the most challenging fault class? `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Byzantine fault** is one in which a faulty component produces **arbitrary, inconsistent, or malicious outputs**, and may even appear to behave correctly to some observers while failing to others. The name comes from the classic paper *"The Byzantine Generals Problem"* (Lamport, Shostak, Pease — 1982), which models the situation of generals surrounding a city who must agree on a battle plan despite some generals being traitors. It is the most challenging class because: 1. Symptoms are non-deterministic and can mimic normal behavior. 2. Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) requires **at least $3f + 1$ replicas** to tolerate $f$ faults. 3. It demands cryptographic signatures, multiple rounds of message exchange, and consensus — making it computationally expensive. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Q.** (a) Explain the **Two-Phase Commit (2PC) protocol** in detail. Discuss its execution flow, the role of the coordinator and cohorts, and the failure recovery steps. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Three-Phase Commit (3PC) protocol**. Show how 3PC overcomes the blocking problem of 2PC. State the assumptions under which 3PC is non-blocking. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Two-Phase Commit (2PC):** **Phase 1 — Prepare Phase:** 1. The **coordinator** writes a "begin_commit" record to its log and sends a `VOTE_REQUEST` (or `PREPARE`) message to all **cohorts**. 2. Each cohort receives the message and executes the transaction tentatively, writing to its log: - A "ready" record (if it can commit), or - An "abort" record (if it cannot). 3. Each cohort replies with `VOTE_COMMIT` (YES) or `VOTE_ABORT` (NO). **Phase 2 — Decision Phase:** 1. The coordinator collects all votes. If **all are YES**, it writes a "commit" record and sends `GLOBAL_COMMIT`. If **any vote is NO** (or a timeout), it writes "abort" and sends `GLOBAL_ABORT`. 2. Each cohort waits for the decision, performs the commit/abort, writes its decision record, and sends an `ACK`. **Failure Recovery:** | Failure | Recovery Action | |---------|-----------------| | Cohort fails before voting | Coordinator aborts after timeout | | Cohort fails after voting YES | Coordinator proceeds; on restart, cohort asks coordinator for decision (cohort may be in "uncertain" state) | | Coordinator fails before sending decision | Cohorts are **blocked** — they cannot decide unilaterally (this is the famous 2PC blocking problem) | | Network partition | Minority side is blocked; majority side commits if all YES votes were received | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Q(a):** Many students forget to mention that the **cohort must write the decision to stable storage before sending ACK**. Skipping this loses 1 mark. Also, the "uncertain" state of a crashed cohort is a guaranteed 1–2 mark question — memorize the term. --- **(b) Three-Phase Commit (3PC):** **Architecture — Three Phases:** ``` COORDINATOR COHORTS --------- ------- 1. CanCommit? -------------------> CanCommit? <------------------- Vote: Yes/No 2. PreCommit -------------------> PreCommit <------------------- Ack 3. DoCommit -------------------> DoCommit <------------------- Ack ``` **How 3PC overcomes 2PC blocking:** 3PC inserts a **Pre-Commit** phase between voting and the final decision. Crucially, the Pre-Commit message is acknowledged by **all** cohorts before the final DoCommit is sent. This guarantees that **no cohort is in an "uncertain" state** when a failure occurs — every live cohort has either received `PreCommit` (and can elect a new coordinator who will commit) or has not (and can safely abort). **Assumptions for 3PC non-blocking:** 1. **Synchronous network:** Bounded message delivery delay. 2. **No network partition:** Or, if partitioned, the failure detector is perfect. 3. **At most $k$ simultaneous failures**, where $k$ is below the protocol's quorum requirement. If the network partitions, 3PC **can still block** in practice — the protocol is only non-blocking in synchronous failure models. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Q(b):** Students often claim 3PC is "non-blocking under all circumstances." This is **wrong** — 3PC is only non-blocking under a **synchronous, failure-detector-perfect** system model. In asynchronous systems, 3PC is **still susceptible to blocking** under partitions. Losing 2 marks for this distinction is common. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` > **Q.** (a) Define **reliability** and **availability**. Derive the relationship between MTTF, MTTR, and availability for a repairable system. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A distributed system consists of **3 servers arranged in parallel**, each with a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ per hour. Compute (i) the reliability of the system at $t = 100$ hours, and (ii) the MTTF of the system. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Reliability, Availability, and Their Relationship:** **Reliability $R(t)$:** The conditional probability that a system performs correctly during the interval $[0, t]$, given that it was operational at $t = 0$. **Availability $A$:** The probability that a system is operational at any random instant $t$, considering both uptime and repair time. **Derivation:** Consider a repairable system that alternates between two states — **UP** and **DOWN**. A single cycle consists of: - An UP period of expected length $MTTF$. - A DOWN period of expected length $MTTR$. In the steady state ($t \to \infty$), the long-run fraction of time the system is UP is: $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{\text{Length of UP period per cycle}}{\text{Total length of one cycle}} \\ &= \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \\ &= \frac{MTTF}{MTBF} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting $MTTF = 1/\lambda$: $$ A = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + MTTR} = \frac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot MTTR} $$ > **Key insight:** Availability can approach 1 (i.e., 100%) as $MTTR \to 0$ (instant repair) or $\lambda \to 0$ (infallible components). Both are practical levers used in cloud data-center design. --- **(b) Numerical Solution — 3-Node Parallel System:** Given: - $n = 3$ identical parallel components. - $\lambda = 0.001$ per hour. - $t = 100$ hours. **Step 1: Compute the per-component reliability at $t = 100$:** $$ R_1(100) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.001 \times 100} = e^{-0.1} = 0.9048 $$ **Step 2: Compute the parallel system reliability (system works if at least 1 of 3 works):** $$ R_{parallel}(t) = 1 - (1 - R_1(t))^3 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} R_{parallel}(100) &= 1 - (1 - 0.9048)^3 \\ &= 1 - (0.0952)^3 \\ &= 1 - 0.000863 \\ &= 0.999137 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute the system MTTF:** For a parallel system, we compute the equivalent failure rate $\lambda_{eq}$ such that $R_{system}(t) = e^{-\lambda_{eq} t}$. The system fails only when all 3 components have failed. The time-to-failure of the system is the **maximum** of the three failure times. Using the order-statistic formula: $$ MTTF_{system} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_{parallel}(t) \, dt $$ $$ \begin{aligned} MTTF_{system} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n \right] dt \\ &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} \right) \quad \text{for } n = 3 \\ &= \frac{1}{0.001} \times \frac{11}{6} \\ &= 1000 \times 1.8333 \\ &\approx 1833.33 \text{ hours} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Q(b):** A common mistake is to compute MTTF as $3/\lambda = 3000$ hours (incorrect — that's the MTTF if the system works only if *all 3* work, i.e., **series** configuration). For parallel configuration, the integral $\int_0^\infty [1-(1-e^{-\lambda t})^n]dt$ must be expanded using the binomial theorem. Lose 1 mark for skipping the derivation step. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's High-Frequency Pitfall Summary > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 ways students lose marks in this module:** > > 1. **Confusing 2PC blocking with 3PC universality:** 3PC is only non-blocking under synchronous assumptions. > 2. **Omitting stable-storage log writes in commit protocols:** Always mention "write-ahead log" or "stable storage." > 3. **Mixing series and parallel reliability formulas:** Series = product, parallel = 1 − product of complements. > 4. **Forgetting the constant failure-rate assumption** for $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — this only holds during the useful life of the bathtub curve. > 5. **Treating "Byzantine" and "arbitrary" as identical to "crash" faults:** Byzantine faults *include* crash faults as a strict subset. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Read this 1 hour before the exam.** - [x] **Fault → Error → Failure** is a strict causal chain. - [x] **Reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$** holds only under **constant failure rate** (memoryless). - [x] **MTTF $= 1/\lambda$** for exponential distribution. - [x] **Availability $A = MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR) = MTTF / MTBF$.** - [x] **Series system** fails if any one component fails: $R_{series} = \prod_i R_i$. - [x] **Parallel system** fails only if all components fail: $R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_i (1 - R_i)$. - [x] **Byzantine fault** requires $\geq 3f+1$ replicas to tolerate $f$ faults. - [x] **2PC** is **blocking** on coordinator failure; uses write-ahead log. - [x] **3PC** adds a Pre-Commit phase and is non-blocking **only in synchronous systems**. - [x] **RPC failure semantics:** maybe, at-least-once, at-most-once, exactly-once. - [x] **Checkpoint types:** independent, coordinated, communication-induced. - [x] **Message logging:** pessimistic (sync, slower, safer) vs. optimistic (async, faster, may orphan). - [x] **Group communication:** causal order preserves happens-before; total order is stronger. - [x] **Process group types:** flat (peer) vs. hierarchical (coordinator-centric). > **Mnemonic for Failure Semantics — "MAAT-E":** > **M**aybe → **A**t-least-once → **A**t-most-once → **E**xactly-once (cheapest → most expensive).

topics.title: Fault Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fault Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Fault, Error, and Failure

In distributed systems theory (as per KTU 2024 syllabus and Tanenbaum's classification framework), three terms are used very precisely to describe system misbehavior:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault (KTU Syllabus Definition)**: A *fault* is the **root cause** of an error — it is a defect or malfunction in one or more components of the system (hardware or software) that has the potential to cause the system to deviate from its specified behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Error**: An error is the **manifestation** of a fault — the internal state of the system becomes incorrect. Not every fault causes an immediate error (e.g., a latent fault in unused code).

> [!NOTE]
> **Failure**: A *failure* is the **observable deviation** of the system's external behavior from its specification. Failure happens when an error propagates to the system's service interface and is visible to the user/other components.

**The Fault → Error → Failure Chain:**

$$\text{Fault (defect)} \longrightarrow \text{Error (incorrect state)} \longrightarrow \text{Failure (observable deviation)}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a long highway:

| Term | Car Analogy | Distributed System Meaning |
|------|-------------|---------------------------|
| **Fault** | A manufacturing defect in a brake pad (latent, unseen) | Bug in code, broken hardware component |
| **Error** | The brake pad wears out, but you haven't braked yet | Memory corruption, wrong variable value |
| **Failure** | You press the brake, but the car doesn't stop — passengers notice | Server returns wrong output, service crashes |

**The car analogy makes the abstraction clear**: A fault *can* exist without causing failure (e.g., a spare tire is flat, but the car still drives fine). It becomes a failure only when the error propagates to the user-visible behavior.

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight for KTU exams**: Most students lose marks by using *fault* and *failure* interchangeably. A **fault is internal/potential**; a **failure is external/observable**.

## 1.3 Why Fault Models Matter in Distributed Systems

A **fault model** is an abstraction that specifies:
- **What kinds of faults** we expect to occur.
- **How components are assumed to fail**.
- **What guarantees** the system must provide *despite* those faults.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Fault Tolerance**: You cannot build a fault-tolerant system without first deciding which faults you are tolerating. There is **no universal fault tolerance** — the model must be specified.

This is the famous principle: *a system tolerant of fewer fault types is easier to build, but offers weaker guarantees; a system tolerant of stronger faults is more robust, but exponentially more complex.*

## 1.4 Standard Fault Classification — Broad Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Primary Categories of Faults (KTU 2024 Highlight)**:
> 1. **Transient Faults** — Occur once and disappear (e.g., cosmic ray bit-flip, network packet drop).
> 2. **Intermittent Faults** — Occur repeatedly, then disappear (e.g., loose connector, flaky memory).
> 3. **Permanent Faults** — Persist until the faulty component is repaired (e.g., burnt-out CPU, disk head crash).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Failure Model (Tanenbaum's Five-Layer Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus adopts the classic **Tanenbaum failure model hierarchy**, which classifies failures from the *least severe* to the *most severe*. This is a **unidirectional containment hierarchy** — a higher-level failure subsumes all lower-level ones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy Property**: If a system tolerates failure type $F_i$, it automatically tolerates all $F_j$ where $j < i$ (i.e., failures lower in the hierarchy). The reverse is **not** true.

### Layer 1 — Crash Failure (Benign Stop)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: A component simply halts execution. It was correct up to the moment it stopped, and remains stopped (no spontaneous recovery). This is the **simplest and most commonly assumed** fault in KTU textbook problems.

- **Behavior**: Works correctly → stops → never sends incorrect output → never resumes.
- **Sub-categories**:
  - **Fail-Stop**: Crash + other components are *reliably notified*.
  - **Fail-Silent / Crash-Recovery**: Component crashes, restarts later with clean state.
  - **Fail-Noisy (Amnesic)**: Crashes, restarts but **forgets its previous state** (no persistent memory).
- **KTU Real-World Example**: A web server process killed by `kill -9`.

### Layer 2 — Omission Failure

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: A component fails to respond to incoming messages or requests. The component is "alive" but skips certain actions.

- **Sub-categories**:
  - **Process Omission**: A process fails to perform an expected action (e.g., a database transaction is silently dropped).
  - **Channel Omission**: A message is *lost in transit* between sender and receiver (network drops a packet).
- **KTU Real-World Example**: UDP packet loss, HTTP request timeout.

### Layer 3 — Timing Failure

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: A response is produced, but it is *outside the specified time interval*. The output may be correct in *value* but wrong in *time*.

- **Sub-types**:
  - **Clock Failure**: Local clock skew exceeds bounds.
  - **Performance Failure**: Computation takes longer than the deadline.
  - **Network Timing Failure**: Message arrives too late (channel delay > threshold).
- **KTU Real-World Example**: A real-time control loop missing its 10 ms deadline.

### Layer 4 — Response Failure

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: The response is produced on time, but the *value* of the response is incorrect.

- **Sub-types**:
  - **Value Failure**: Incorrect output value (e.g., `$1+1 = 3$).
  - **State Transition Failure**: Component enters a wrong state (e.g., bank account transitions to invalid state).
- **KTU Real-World Example**: A buggy RPC server returning corrupted data.

### Layer 5 — Arbitrary (Byzantine) Failure

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: The most severe failure class. The component may produce *any arbitrary behavior* — including malicious, conflicting, or random outputs. It may collude with other faulty nodes, send different messages to different receivers, and even appear correct intermittently.

- **Why "Byzantine"?**: Named after the **Byzantine Generals Problem** (Lamport, Shostak, Pease, 1982) — the metaphor is that generals surrounding a besieged city must agree on a common plan despite some generals being traitors who may send contradictory messages.
- **KTU Real-World Examples**: Compromised node, hacked server, malware-infected process, malicious insider.

## 2.2 Complete Failure Model Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important table for KTU 14-mark answers** — examiners test this hierarchy almost every semester.

| Failure Type | Halts? | Wrong Value? | Wrong Timing? | Arbitrary? | Tolerance Cost |
|--------------|:------:|:------------:|:-------------:|:----------:|:--------------:|
| **Crash (Fail-Stop)** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | **Low** |
| **Omission** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Low–Medium |
| **Timing** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | Medium |
| **Response (Value)** | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Medium–High |
| **Byzantine (Arbitrary)** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | **Exponential** |

## 2.3 Masking Failures

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: A *masking failure* is a failure that is **hidden** from other components by the system itself. The system's external observable behavior remains correct, even though internal components have failed.

**Mechanisms used to mask failures:**

1. **Redundancy**: Use $N \geq 3$ replicas and vote on outputs (e.g., $3f+1$ replicas tolerate $f$ Byzantine faults).
2. **Retransmission**: Network layer masks omission failures using ARQ / TCP.
3. **Checksums / CRC**: Mask transient bit-errors in storage and transmission.
4. **Error-Correcting Codes (ECC)**: Memory uses Hamming codes to correct single-bit errors transparently.
5. **Replication + Consensus**: Paxos, Raft mask crash failures in distributed databases.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these formulas — they appear in KTU university exams every year.

| Formula / Rule | Statement | Used For |
|----------------|-----------|----------|
| $N \geq 2f + 1$ | To tolerate $f$ **crash failures**, need $2f+1$ replicas. | Crash fault tolerance |
| $N \geq 3f + 1$ | To tolerate $f$ **Byzantine failures**, need $3f+1$ replicas. | Byzantine consensus (PBFT) |
| $f < \dfrac{N-1}{3}$ | Maximum Byzantine faulty nodes for agreement. | Byzantine Generals bound |
| $f < \dfrac{N}{2}$ | Majority requirement for crash-tolerant consensus. | Paxos / Raft quorum |
| $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure for a component with rate $\lambda$. | Reliability analysis |
| $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function: probability of no failure until time $t$. | Hardware reliability |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair (recovery time). | Availability calculation |
| $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | **Availability** of a system. | High-availability design |
| $P(\text{system up}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N} (1-p_i)$ | Parallel redundancy: prob. at least one of $N$ replicas works (independent). | Replication design |
| Two-Army bound | Cannot guarantee agreement with only message exchange (no timeouts). | Impossibility proofs |

**Critical Notation Warning**: In all KTU formulas above, $f$ = number of faulty/tolerated nodes, $N$ = total number of nodes, $\lambda$ = failure rate per unit time.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Fault Model Used | Why This Choice |
|-------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Google Spanner | Crash + disk failure | Geo-distribution; Byzantine too expensive |
| Bitcoin Blockchain | Byzantine (up to $f < n/3$) | Public, adversarial environment |
| Airplane Fly-by-Wire | Byzantine (3f+1 voting) | Safety-critical; cannot trust any single sensor |
| AWS S3 Storage | Crash + omission | Cost-effective; uses erasure coding |
| Database Replication (Raft) | Crash only | High performance, simpler consensus |
| NASA Deep Space Comms | Crash + omission + timing | Long delays, timeouts unreliable |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark design questions**: Always justify your fault-model choice using the table above. Saying *"I chose crash-failure tolerance because Byzantine costs 3x the resources"* scores extra marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Byzantine Generals Bound ($N \geq 3f + 1$)

This is the **most heavily tested derivation** in the KTU Fault Models module. We derive why Byzantine consensus requires $3f+1$ nodes to tolerate $f$ traitors.

### Setup

We have $N$ generals, of which at most $f$ are traitors (Byzantine). All loyal generals must agree on the same action (attack/retreat). Traitors may send any arbitrary messages.

### Step 1 — Recursive Argument Setup

Byzantine agreement requires a **majority of loyal voices** to drown out traitors. The interactive consistency condition is satisfied when:

$$N_{\text{loyal}} = N - f > f_{\text{traitor}} + f_{\text{traitor}}$$

This is the *double majority* principle: loyal generals must outnumber traitors in **both** rounds of the protocol.

### Step 2 — Algebraic Expansion

The condition $N - f > 2f$ means loyal generals exceed twice the traitor count (to handle the two-stage message exchange in PBFT — pre-prepare and prepare phases).

$$N - f > 2f$$

### Step 3 — Solving for $N$

Adding $f$ to both sides of the inequality:

$$N > 3f$$

### Step 4 — Final Result (Integer Form)

Since $N$ must be a positive integer, and $N > 3f$ implies $N \geq 3f + 1$:

$$\boxed{N \geq 3f + 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent alternative form (used in KTU textbook problems)**:

$$f < \frac{N - 1}{3}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example (Numerical)

**Problem**: How many generals are needed to tolerate $f = 1$ traitor?
**Solution**: $N \geq 3(1) + 1 = 4$ generals. So 3 loyal + 1 traitor.

**Problem**: How many generals are needed to tolerate $f = 3$ traitors?
**Solution**: $N \geq 3(3) + 1 = 10$ generals.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick**: A common KTU trick is to ask *"If $N = 10$, how many traitors can be tolerated?"* The answer is $f = 3$ (because $f < (10-1)/3 = 3$, so $f \le 3$). Many students wrongly answer 3.33.

## 3.2 Derivation: Availability Formula $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$

### Step 1 — Definition of Availability

Availability $A$ is the **fraction of time** the system is operational:

$$A = \frac{\text{Time UP}}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{\text{Time UP}}{\text{Time UP + Time DOWN}}$$

### Step 2 — Long-Term Average

As measurement time $T \to \infty$, the system goes through many failure-recovery cycles. The expected duration of one cycle is:

$$E[\text{cycle}] = MTTF + MTTR$$

Within each cycle, the system is UP for an expected $MTTF$ duration. So:

$$A = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\sum \text{UP times}}{T} = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem**: A server has $MTTF = 1000$ hours and $MTTR = 10$ hours. What is its availability?

**Solution**:

$$A = \frac{1000}{1000 + 10} = \frac{1000}{1010} \approx 0.9901 = 99.01\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Do NOT confuse *availability* with *reliability*. $R(t)$ is the probability of *no failure up to time $t$*; $A$ is the *long-run uptime fraction* after many failures. They converge only when $MTTR \to 0$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Parallel Redundancy Reliability

If $N$ independent components operate in parallel (system fails only if *all* fail), each with reliability $p_i$ at time $t$:

### Step 1 — Probability of Single Component Failure

$$P(\text{component } i \text{ failed}) = 1 - p_i$$

### Step 2 — Probability ALL Components Fail (Independence Assumption)

$$P(\text{all failed}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N}(1 - p_i)$$

### Step 3 — System Survival (At Least One Works)

$$P(\text{system survives}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N}(1 - p_i)$$

### Step 4 — Symmetric Case (All Replicas Identical, $p_i = p$)

$$P(\text{system up}) = 1 - (1 - p)^N$$

**Numerical check**: With $p = 0.9$ (each replica 90% reliable) and $N = 3$ replicas:

$$P(\text{system up}) = 1 - (0.1)^3 = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight**: Tripling the replicas from 90% to 99.9% reliability — a **1000x improvement** in the failure probability. This is the foundation of all cloud-region redundancy.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing a Fault Model

Use this matrix to justify design decisions in KTU 14-mark answers:

| Design Constraint | Recommended Fault Model | Justification |
|-------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| **Closed LAN cluster** (trusted nodes) | Crash (fail-stop) | Low cost; high trust |
| **Wide-area DB replication** | Crash + network omission | Realistic; Paxos/Raft sufficient |
| **Internet-facing API** | Crash + value failures | Add input validation, retries |
| **Public blockchain** | Byzantine | Adversarial environment, no trust |
| **Avionics / medical device** | Byzantine + timing | Safety-critical; triple-modular redundancy |
| **IoT sensor swarm** | Omission + timing | Energy-constrained, intermittent comms |
| **Financial trading** | Timing + value | Latency matters; correctness equally critical |

## 3.5 Failure Detection Techniques — Analytical Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Failure detection is the **complement** of fault tolerance — you cannot recover from a fault you don't know about.

| Technique | Mechanism | Detects Crash? | Detects Omission? | Detects Byzantine? | Network Overhead |
|-----------|-----------|:--------------:|:-----------------:|:------------------:|:----------------:|
| **Ping / Ack** | Probe with reply | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Low |
| **Heartbeat** | Periodic "I'm alive" | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Medium |
| **Gossip-style detection** | Probabilistic | ✅ | Partial | ❌ | Medium |
| **Challenge–Response** | Crypto nonce + reply | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | High |
| **Voting / Quorum** | Majority response | ✅ | ✅ | Partial | High |
| **Byzantine agreement** | PBFT, multi-round | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Fault-Error-Failure Chain (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Component Activated] --> B{Fault Present?}
    B -- No --> C[Correct Execution]
    B -- Yes --> D[Error Injected]
    D --> E{Error Propagates?}
    E -- No --> F[Masked: Service OK]
    E -- Yes --> G[Failure: Service Deviation]
    G --> H[Detection + Recovery]
    H --> I[System Resumes Correct Service]
    C --> J[Normal Operation]
    F --> J
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Tanenbaum Failure Hierarchy (Containment Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1: Crash Failure - least severe]
    L2[Level 2: Omission Failure]
    L3[Level 3: Timing Failure]
    L4[Level 4: Response Failure]
    L5[Level 5: Byzantine Arbitrary Failure - most severe]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 -.-> N[Note: Tolerating L5 implies tolerating L1-L4]
    L1 -.-> N
```

## 4.3 Fault Classification by Duration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fault Type] --> B[Transient]
    A --> C[Intermittent]
    A --> D[Permanent]
    B --> B1[One-time occurrence - e.g., cosmic ray bit flip]
    C --> C1[Recurring then vanishing - e.g., loose cable]
    D --> D1[Persists until repair - e.g., burnt chip]
```

## 4.4 Byzantine Generals — Voting Architecture (3f+1 with f=1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Commander Node] -- order attack --> G1[General 1 Loyal]
    C -- order retreat --> G2[General 2 Traitor]
    C -- order attack --> G3[General 3 Loyal]
    C -- order attack --> G4[General 4 Loyal]
    G1 -- attack --> G2
    G2 -- conflicting lies --> G3
    G3 -- attack --> G4
    G4 -- attack --> G1
    G1 -- attack --> G3
    G2 -- conflicting lies --> G4
    G3 --> V[Vote Tally: 3 attack, 1 retreat - majority wins]
    G4 --> V
    G1 --> V
    G2 --> V
    V --> D[Decision: All loyal agree on ATTACK]
```

## 4.5 Failure Detection Architecture — Heartbeat Protocol

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Monitor
    participant N1 as Node 1
    participant N2 as Node 2
    participant N3 as Node 3
    Note over M: Starts heartbeat timer T_hb
    M->>N1: HB_REQ
    M->>N2: HB_REQ
    M->>N3: HB_REQ
    N1-->>M: HB_ACK (alive)
    N2--xM: HB_ACK (lost)
    N3-->>M: HB_ACK (alive)
    Note over M: N2 timeout - declare SUSPECTED
    M->>M: Mark N2 as suspected faulty
    Note over M: Re-verify after T_suspect elapses
    M->>N2: HB_REQ x3 retries
    N2--xM: No response
    M->>M: Confirm N2 CRASHED
```

## 4.6 Masking Failures — Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I[Input Signal] --> R1[Replica 1]
    I --> R2[Replica 2]
    I --> R3[Replica 3]
    R1 --> V[Voter Majority Logic]
    R2 --> V
    R3 --> V
    V --> O[Correct Output]
    R1 -.fault.-> X[Masked: voter ignores outlier]
    R2 -.fault.-> X
    R3 -.fault.-> X
    X --> O
```

## 4.7 Fault Tolerance Decision Tree (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Fault Model] --> Q1{Environment trusted?}
    Q1 -- Yes, LAN --> Q2{Critical safety?}
    Q1 -- No, Internet --> Q3{Need to resist adversaries?}
    Q2 -- No --> Crash[Crash Failure Tolerance - use Raft]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4{Real-time deadlines?}
    Q3 -- No --> CrashOm[Crash + Omission - use Paxos]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Byz[Byzantine - use PBFT or Blockchain]
    Q4 -- No --> Resp[Response Failure - use ECC + replication]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Byz2[Byzantine + Timing - use TMR + voting]
```
