# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Differentiate between a fault, an error, and a failure in a distributed system. Give one example for each.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks)**:
>
> - **Fault**: A defect in a system component (hardware or software) that has the *potential* to cause incorrect behavior. It is the *root cause* and is internal. *Example*: A bug in the consensus algorithm.
> - **Error**: An internal incorrect state of the system that arises when a fault manifests. *Example*: A process variable holds a corrupted value due to a memory bit-flip.
> - **Failure*: The external, observable deviation of system behavior from its specification. *Example*: The web server returns an HTTP 500 error to the client.
>
> The relationship is causal: **Fault → Error → Failure**. (Award 1 mark for each correct definition + example.)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**List and briefly explain any three types of failures in distributed systems as per Tanenbaum's failure model.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each)**:
>
> 1. **Crash Failure**: A process halts permanently and does not produce any further outputs. *Example*: A web server process terminated abruptly.
> 2. **Omission Failure**: A process or channel fails to deliver a message that was supposed to be sent. *Example*: A UDP packet is dropped by a congested router.
> 3. **Timing Failure**: A response is produced outside the specified real-time bound. *Example*: A real-time sensor misses its 100 ms deadline.
>
> (Other valid options: *Response Failure* and *Byzantine/Arbitrary Failure*.)

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **five types of failures** in Tanenbaum's hierarchical failure model, with one real-world example for each. How does the *severity* of failure affect the *cost of fault tolerance*?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the bound $N \geq 3f + 1$ for Byzantine agreement. Using this bound, calculate the minimum number of generals required to tolerate $f = 2$ traitors.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

The five failure types, in **ascending order of severity**:

**1. Crash (Fail-Stop) Failure** [1.5 marks]
The process simply halts. It was correct up to the crash point and does not produce any incorrect output.
- *Example*: An OS kills a process with `kill -9` due to OOM.

**2. Omission Failure** [1.5 marks]
The process or channel fails to perform an action that it was supposed to perform.
- *Example*: A network switch drops a packet under congestion (channel omission).

**3. Timing Failure** [1.5 marks]
The response is correct in value but produced too early or too late.
- *Example*: A real-time airbag controller fires 50 ms after the crash, missing the safety window.

**4. Response Failure** [1.5 marks]
The response is on time but has the *wrong value* or causes an incorrect *state transition*.
- *Example*: A buggy RPC server returns `$2 + 2 = 5` due to integer overflow.

**5. Byzantine (Arbitrary) Failure** [1 mark — conclusion]
The worst case — the component may produce any arbitrary, even malicious, output, including conflicting messages to different receivers.
- *Example*: A hacked node in a public blockchain sending conflicting transactions.

**Severity vs. Cost Trade-off** (1 mark for synthesis):
> The higher the severity tolerated, the more replicas, more communication rounds, and more complex consensus protocols are required. Crash-tolerant systems need $2f+1$ replicas; Byzantine needs $3f+1$ — a 50% increase in resources for a stronger guarantee.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of $N \geq 3f + 1$** (5 marks):

Byzantine agreement requires that the loyal generals reach a *single common decision* despite traitors. The protocol operates in multiple rounds, and the key condition is that the **loyal voices must outnumber the traitors by a margin of 2** in each round:

$$N - f > 2f \quad \text{(loyal count must exceed twice the traitor count)}$$

Step-by-step:
$$\begin{aligned}
N - f &> 2f \\
N &> 2f + f \\
N &> 3f \\
\therefore \quad N &\geq 3f + 1 \quad \text{(integer form)}
\end{aligned}$$

The condition $N - f > 2f$ is required because, in the worst case, each traitor can send a *different* lie to every other general, and the loyal generals must outvote *all* of those lies plus the traitors' own votes.

**Numerical calculation** (2 marks):

Given $f = 2$ traitors, the minimum number of generals is:
$$N \geq 3(2) + 1 = 7$$

So **7 generals** are required (5 loyal + 2 traitors).

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define **fault masking**. Explain with a neat diagram how **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** can mask a single Byzantine failure.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A server has $MTTF = 2000$ hours and $MTTR = 4$ hours. (i) Calculate its availability. (ii) If three such servers run in parallel as independent replicas, calculate the probability that the system is up at any given instant (assume $p = A$ for each replica, no common-mode failures).

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Fault Masking** (2 marks):
> A masking failure is one that is **hidden from the system's external users**. Even though internal components may have failed, the system continues to deliver correct, on-time service. Fault masking is achieved by **redundancy** — multiple replicas producing results that are compared and voted on.

**Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) — Explanation** (3 marks):

In TMR, three identical modules receive the same input and produce outputs in parallel. A **voter** (majority logic circuit) compares the three outputs and forwards the value that appears at least twice. As long as no more than one module is faulty, the voter correctly identifies the correct output and the fault is *masked*.

**Diagram** (2 marks — see Section 4.6 for the Mermaid block diagram):

The three replicas' outputs feed into a voter. If one replica is Byzantine and produces a different value, the voter discards it (since the other two agree) and forwards the correct value downstream. The faulty replica is *masked* from the system's output.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Part (i) — Availability of single server** (3 marks):

Using $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$:

$$A = \frac{2000}{2000 + 4} = \frac{2000}{2004} \approx 0.9980 = 99.80\%$$

**Part (ii) — Parallel redundancy of three servers** (4 marks):

Step 1 — Probability of single-server failure:
$$P(\text{single fails}) = 1 - A = 1 - 0.9980 = 0.0020$$

Step 2 — Probability that *all three* fail (independent assumption):
$$P(\text{all fail}) = (0.0020)^3 = 8 \times 10^{-9}$$

Step 3 — System availability (at least one server up):
$$A_{\text{system}} = 1 - 8 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.999999992 \approx 99.9999992\%$$

This represents an improvement of nearly **5 nines** in availability over a single server, demonstrating the value of redundancy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (READ CAREFULLY)**:
> 1. **Mistaking Fault for Failure** (–1 to –2 marks): Use *fault* only for root causes; use *failure* for observable deviations. Mixing them costs marks.
> 2. **Skipping the derivation steps** (–2 marks): In $N \geq 3f+1$ derivations, examiners want to see the *intermediate* inequality $N - f > 2f$ explicitly written, not just the final answer.
> 3. **Forgetting the integer form**: $N > 3f$ is incomplete; KTU requires $N \geq 3f+1$ as the final form.
> 4. **Confusing Availability with Reliability**: $A$ is a long-run fraction; $R(t)$ is a probability at time $t$. Many students interchange these and lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the independence assumption**: In parallel-redundancy problems, you MUST state the assumption "components are independent" — otherwise the multiplication $\prod(1-p_i)$ is invalid.
> 6. **Wrong hierarchy direction**: Some students argue that tolerating a crash implies tolerating Byzantine. The hierarchy is the *opposite* — Byzantine tolerance implies crash tolerance, not vice-versa.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Fault Models**
>
> ✅ **Core Definitions** (must be memorized word-for-word):
>    - *Fault* = root-cause defect (internal, latent).
>    - *Error* = incorrect internal state.
>    - *Failure* = observable deviation in service.
>
> ✅ **Five-Layer Tanenbaum Hierarchy** (least → most severe):
>    1. Crash (fail-stop) → 2. Omission → 3. Timing → 4. Response → 5. Byzantine.
>    - *Containment rule*: Tolerating layer $k$ implies tolerating all layers $< k$.
>
> ✅ **Three Fault Durations**: Transient, Intermittent, Permanent.
>
> ✅ **Two Critical Formulas** (always quoted in KTU 14-mark answers):
>    - $N \geq 2f + 1$ for **crash** tolerance.
>    - $N \geq 3f + 1$ for **Byzantine** tolerance.
>
> ✅ **Availability Formula**: $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$.
>
> ✅ **Reliability Function**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
>
> ✅ **Parallel Redundancy** (independent replicas): $P(\text{up}) = 1 - \prod(1-p_i)$.
>
> ✅ **Masking Failures Techniques**: Replication + voting, ECC, ARQ retransmission, checksums, consensus protocols.
>
> ✅ **Failure Detection Methods**: Ping/Ack, Heartbeat, Gossip, Challenge-Response, Quorum Voting, Byzantine Agreement (PBFT).
>
> ✅ **Real-World Mapping** (used in design questions):
>    - Google Spanner → Crash + Disk
>    - Bitcoin → Byzantine
>    - Paxos/Raft → Crash only
>    - Airplane control → Byzantine + Timing (TMR)
>
> ✅ **Byzantine Generals Problem** (Lamport–Shostak–Pease, 1982): Origin of the $3f+1$ bound; commanders and lieutenants must agree despite traitors.
>
> ✅ **KTU High-Yield Buzzwords** to use in answers: *fail-stop, fail-noisy, amnesic failure, interactive consistency, common-mode failure, N-version programming, watchdog timer, suspicion mechanism*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Replication Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Replication Techniques # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Replication** in distributed systems is the process of maintaining multiple identical copies (replicas) of a data object or service across geographically dispersed nodes to enhance **availability**, **fault tolerance**, **scalability**, and **performance**. A *replica manager* is the software process responsible for coordinating updates and reads across these copies in accordance with a defined **consistency model**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Replication is a technique where multiple copies of data (replicas) are stored at different nodes so that data remains accessible even if some nodes fail. It is governed by *replica managers* that enforce ordering and consistency of operations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multiple Bank Branches" Model Imagine a national bank with **5,000 customers** but only **1 branch**. If the branch shuts down due to a power cut, **nobody can withdraw money** — the system is unavailable. Now imagine the bank opens **50 branches**, each holding an identical copy of the ledger. - A customer in **Kochi** doesn't need to travel to **Delhi** — they go to the *nearest* branch (improved **performance** and **latency**). - If a **flood destroys 5 branches**, the other **45 still operate** (improved **availability & fault tolerance**). - But now, when Mr. A deposits ₹10,000 in Kochi and immediately his wife tries to withdraw from Delhi, *how do both branches agree on the balance?* This is the **replication consistency problem**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight:** Replication trades off *consistency guarantees* for *availability and performance*. The famous **CAP Theorem** (covered later) formalizes this trade-off — a distributed system can deliver at most **two out of three** of: **C**onsistency, **A**vailability, **P**artition tolerance. ## 1.3 Why Replicate? The Five Pillars | Goal | Description | Standard Metric | |------|-------------|-----------------| | **Reliability** | Probability of data survival despite node failures | Measured by **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** | | **Availability** | Fraction of time the system serves requests | `A = MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR)` | | **Performance** | Reduced access latency via geographic locality | **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** in **ms** | | **Scalability** | Ability to handle increased load by adding replicas | **Throughput = ops/sec** | | **Fault Tolerance** | Continued operation despite `k` simultaneous failures | Tolerates `k-1` failures if `k` replicas exist | ## 1.4 Classification of Replication > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme High-Yield Classification:** Replication can be categorized along three orthogonal axes. Examiners frequently test these distinctions. 1. **By Timing of Replication** - **Eager (Synchronous) Replication** — updates propagated *before* the operation completes. - **Lazy (Asynchronous) Replication** — updates propagated *after* the operation completes. 2. **By Number of Masters** - **Single-Master (Primary-Backup)** — only one node accepts writes. - **Multi-Master (Peer-to-Peer)** — any node can accept writes. 3. **By Location of Control** - **Centralized** — a single coordinator orchestrates replication. - **Decentralized** — replicas self-coordinate using protocols. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Passive (Primary-Backup) Replication In **passive replication**, there is a distinguished **primary** node that receives *all* client requests. The primary executes the operation, sends updates to **backups**, waits for acknowledgements, and then replies to the client. ### Operational Logic Steps - **Step 1 — Request Arrival:** Client sends a write request `W(x)` to the primary `P`. - **Step 2 — Local Execution:** `P` computes the new value and updates its local copy. - **Step 3 — Update Propagation:** `P` multicasts the update to all backups $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$. - **Step 4 — Acknowledgement:** Each backup $B_i$ applies the update and sends `ACK` back to `P`. - **Step 5 — Response:** After receiving a quorum of ACKs, `P` sends success reply to the client. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Passive"?** Backups do not actively participate in serving requests — they remain *passive* until the primary fails. Upon primary failure, one backup is **elected** as the new primary (often via a consensus algorithm like **Paxos** or **Raft**). > [!WARNING] > **Single Point of Failure Risk:** If `P` crashes *after* applying the update locally but *before* propagating to backups, the system suffers a **split-brain** scenario. This is why production systems use **Quorum-Based Paxos** with at least `2F + 1` replicas to tolerate `F` failures. ## 2.2 Active (Multi-Primary / State-Machine) Replication In **active replication**, *every* replica independently processes client requests. All replicas are initialized to the same state and process the *same sequence* of operations — if they start identical and process identical inputs deterministically, they remain identical. ### Core Mechanism: Totally Ordered Multicast - All replicas must receive updates in the **same order**. - This requires a **total order broadcast** primitive. - Common implementations: **Paxos**, **Zab**, **Raft**, **Viewstamped Replication**. ## 2.3 Quorum-Based Protocols Quorum protocols are the mathematical foundation of fault-tolerant replicated storage. The key idea: **read and write operations must intersect** in at least one fresh node. ### Standard Notation - $N$ = total number of replicas - $R$ = read quorum size - $W$ = write quorum size ### The Two Fundamental Quorum Rules > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule 1 (Intersection for Fresh Read):** > $$R + W > N$$ > This ensures *every* read set overlaps with *every* write set, guaranteeing the read sees the most recent write. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule 2 (Majority for Single Writer):** > $$W > \frac{N}{2}$$ > This prevents two concurrent writes from succeeding simultaneously, avoiding **write-write conflicts**. ## 2.4 Consistency Models Spectrum > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiners Love This Spectrum:** Memorize the ordering from strictest to weakest. | # | Model | Guarantee | Engineering Trade-off | |---|-------|-----------|----------------------| | 1 | **Strict Consistency** | Reads always return the *most recent* write globally | Requires global clock; practically impossible at scale | | 2 | **Sequential Consistency** | All operations appear in *some* total order, respecting each client's program order | Weaker than strict; allows reordering across clients | | 3 | **Causal Consistency** | Causally related operations seen in order; concurrent operations may differ | Allows high availability; used in **COPS**, **Eiger** | | 4 | **Eventual Consistency** | All replicas *converge* to same value if no updates arrive for a while | Highest availability; used in **Amazon Dynamo**, **Cassandra** | | 5 | **Read-your-Writes** | A client always sees its own writes | Weaker but commonly expected UX | | 6 | **Monotonic Reads** | Successive reads never return older values | Causal + Read-your-Writes | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THIS TABLE — these formulas appear in 60%+ of KTU ESE questions on Replication.** | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Constraints | Engineering Use | |---|---------|----------------------|--------------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Availability | $A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | System uptime analysis | | 2 | Quorum Intersection (Fresh Read) | $R + W > N$ | $N$ = total replicas, $R$ = read quorum, $W$ = write quorum | DynamoDB quorum tuning | | 3 | Write Majority | $W > N/2$ | Strict inequality | Conflict-free write | | 4 | Maximum Fault Tolerance | $f = \lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ | Number of failed nodes tolerated | Paxos deployment sizing | | 5 | Epidemic Infection Rate (SI Model) | $\frac{di}{dt} = \beta \cdot s(t) \cdot i(t)$ | $\beta$ = infection rate, $s$ = susceptible, $i$ = infected | Gossip protocol convergence | | 6 | Logarithmic Rounds for Full Spread | $T_{spreading} = O(\log_b N)$ | $b$ = branching factor (typically 2-4) | Anti-entropy trees | | 7 | Byzantine Fault Tolerance | $N \geq 3f + 1$ | Requires $3f+1$ replicas for `f` Byzantine failures | Blockchain consensus | | 8 | CAP Trade-off Bound | At most 2 of {C, A, P} | Partition tolerance is mandatory in real systems | Architecture decision | | 9 | Bandwidth Cost (Anti-Entropy) | $C_{AE} = N \cdot \text{obj\_size} \cdot \text{push\_freq}$ | Network cost per unit time | Epidemic deployment sizing | | 10 | Storage Overhead | $S_{over} = (N - 1) \times \text{data\_size}$ | Additional storage due to replicas | Capacity planning | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Quirk Note:** In the table above, I deliberately used `\cdot` (multiplication dot) and the full fraction `\frac{N}{2}` instead of inline notation like `N/2` next to inequalities. This preserves table formatting integrity. **In your exam answer sheet, write these in plain text** — KTU examiners do not expect LaTeX in handwritten answers. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Amazon DynamoDB** uses $R + W > N$ with `N=3` by default (`R=2, W=2`). - **Google Spanner** uses synchronous Paxos across **5 datacenters** for strict consistency. - **Cassandra** allows tunable consistency per query (`ANY`, `ONE`, `QUORUM`, `ALL`). - **DNS** uses lazy replication with eventual consistency. - **Cassandra + Gossip** uses epidemic dissemination for cluster membership. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why $R + W > N$ Guarantees Fresh Reads We start from the fundamental requirement: *any successful read must include at least one node from the most recent successful write set*. **Given:** - $N$ = total replicas in the system - $W$ = number of nodes that acknowledged the *last write* - $R$ = number of nodes contacted by the *current read* **The Problem:** We must guarantee that the read set of $R$ nodes and the write set of $W$ nodes share *at least one common node*. If they share no node, the read may return stale data from an un-updated replica. **Mathematical Formulation Using Set Theory:** Let $W_{set}$ = set of replicas that received the last write, and $R_{set}$ = set of replicas the read contacts. The size of $W_{set}$ is at most $W$, and the size of $R_{set}$ is at most $R$. Both sets are subsets of the $N$ total replicas. For *guaranteed intersection*, we apply the **Pigeonhole Principle**: $$ \begin{aligned} |W_{set} \cup R_{set}| &\leq N \\ |W_{set}| + |R_{set}| - |W_{set} \cap R_{set}| &\leq N \\ |W_{set}| + |R_{set}| - N &\leq |W_{set} \cap R_{set}| \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step reasoning:** - Line 1: The union of any two subsets of $N$ elements cannot exceed $N$. - Line 2: Standard set identity: $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$. - Line 3: Rearrangement to solve for the intersection. **For guaranteed non-empty intersection (i.e., at least one fresh node is read), we require:** $$ \begin{aligned} |W_{set} \cap R_{set}| &\geq 1 \\ |W_{set}| + |R_{set}| - N &\geq 1 \\ |W_{set}| + |R_{set}| &\geq N + 1 \end{aligned} $$ Since $|W_{set}| \leq W$ and $|R_{set}| \leq R$, the *necessary* condition for the guarantee is: $$ R + W \geq N + 1 \quad \text{(equivalently, } R + W > N\text{)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** With $N=3$, choosing $R=2, W=2$ satisfies $2+2=4>3$. This is why Amazon DynamoDB's default of `(R=2, W=2, N=3)` provides strong consistency despite being "eventually consistent" by design. ## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Tolerable Failures in a Quorum System **Question:** Given $N$ replicas, a write quorum of $W$, and read quorum of $R$, what is the maximum number $f$ of simultaneous node failures the system can tolerate while *still being able to complete reads and writes*? **For a write to succeed:** $$ W \leq N - f_w $$ where $f_w$ is the number of failures allowed for writes to still be possible. So $f_w = N - W$. **For a read to succeed:** $$ R \leq N - f_r $$ So $f_r = N - R$. **For both reads AND writes to succeed simultaneously:** $$ f_{system} = \min(f_w, f_r) = \min(N - W, N - R) $$ For the *symmetric* case $R = W = \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$ (a majority quorum): $$ \begin{aligned} f_{system} &= N - \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil \\ &= N - \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil \\ &= \lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor \end{aligned} $$ **Example for $N=5$:** $f = \lfloor 4/2 \rfloor = 2$. The system tolerates 2 simultaneous failures. ## 3.3 Derivation: Epidemic (Gossip) Protocol Convergence Time In an **anti-entropy epidemic protocol**, each node periodically picks a random peer and exchanges its state. We model this using a simplified **Susceptible-Infected (SI)** epidemic model. **Differential Equation for Spread:** $$ \frac{di}{dt} = \beta \cdot s(t) \cdot i(t) $$ where: - $i(t)$ = fraction of nodes that have the update at time $t$ - $s(t) = 1 - i(t)$ = fraction of nodes *not yet* updated - $\beta$ = infection rate constant (depends on gossip frequency) **Step-by-step solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{di}{dt} &= \beta \cdot (1 - i) \cdot i \\ \frac{di}{i(1-i)} &= \beta \, dt \\ \int \left( \frac{1}{i} + \frac{1}{1-i} \right) di &= \int \beta \, dt \\ \ln\left( \frac{i}{1-i} \right) &= \beta t + C \\ \frac{i}{1-i} &= e^{\beta t + C} = K e^{\beta t} \\ i(t) &= \frac{K e^{\beta t}}{1 + K e^{\beta t}} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Solution (Logistic Growth Form):** $$ i(t) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\beta t - C}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Interpretation:** This is the famous **logistic (S-curve)** growth. Initially growth is slow (few infected, few encounters), accelerates rapidly in the middle, then saturates as `i → 1`. For practical gossip systems, $O(\log_b N)$ rounds (where $b$ is the fanout) suffice to infect *all* nodes — this is why epidemic protocols scale to millions of nodes (e.g., Cassandra, Consul). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Replication Strategies | Strategy | Consistency | Latency | Fault Tolerance | Complexity | Best Use Case | |----------|-------------|---------|-----------------|------------|---------------| | **Synchronous Primary-Backup** | Strong | High (waits for ACKs) | Low–Medium | Low | Banking, Configuration stores | | **Asynchronous Primary-Backup** | Eventual | Low | High | Low | DNS, CDN content | | **Active Replication (State Machine)** | Strong (with total order) | Medium | High | High (needs consensus) | Kafka, etcd, ZooKeeper | | **Quorum-based (N=3, R=2, W=2)** | Strong | Medium | 1 failure | Medium | DynamoDB, Cassandra | | **Read-Repair (Anti-Entropy)** | Eventual | Low | High | Low | Eventually consistent KV stores | | **Epidemic (Gossip)** | Eventual | Lowest | Very High | Low | Membership, failure detection | | **Chain Replication** | Strong | Medium | Medium | Medium | Microsoft Azure Storage | | **Byzantine Quorum** | Strong under BFT | Highest | Tolerates `f` malicious | Very High | Blockchain, audit logs | # 4.
