KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define **replication** in distributed systems. List any **two advantages** of data replication. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Replication is the process of storing multiple copies (replicas) of a data object or service on different nodes of a distributed system, managed by *replica managers* that coordinate updates and reads to maintain a defined consistency level. > > **Two Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):** > 1. **Enhanced Availability:** If one node fails, the data remains accessible from other replicas, improving the system's overall uptime. > 2. **Improved Performance:** Users can access the geographically closest replica, reducing network latency and improving response time. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Distinguish between **eager replication** and **lazy replication**. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Eager (Synchronous) | Lazy (Asynchronous) | |--------|---------------------|----------------------| | Update timing | Propagated *before* operation commit | Propagated *after* operation commit | | Consistency | Strong / Linearizable | Eventual | | Latency | Higher (waits for all ACKs) | Lower (immediate reply) | | Failure handling | Complex rollback if a replica fails | Simpler; no rollback needed | ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]** Explain in detail the **Primary-Backup (Passive) Replication** technique. Discuss its **advantages**, **disadvantages**, and the **failover mechanism** used when the primary fails. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Detailed Working of Primary-Backup Replication **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — System Setup (1 mark):** - A distinguished node is elected as the **primary** (also called *coordinator* or *master*). - The other `N − 1` nodes act as **backups** (also called *secondaries* or *slaves*). - All client write requests are routed to the primary. **Step 2 — Write Operation Flow (3 marks):** - The client sends a write request `W(X, v)` to the primary. - The primary executes the write locally and updates its copy. - The primary **multicasts** the update to all backups. - Each backup applies the update and sends an `ACK` to the primary. - The primary waits for acknowledgements from a *quorum* of backups. - The primary sends success response to the client. **Step 3 — Read Operation (1 mark):** - Reads can be served by the primary alone (linearizable) or from any backup (with relaxed consistency). - For strict consistency, the primary serves all reads. **Step 4 — Failure Detection (1 mark):** - Backups use **heartbeat** messages to detect primary failure. - If a backup does not receive `N` consecutive heartbeats, it suspects the primary has failed. - This is known as the **φ-accrual failure detector** in some implementations. **Step 5 — Election of New Primary (1 mark):** - A new primary is chosen via a **leader election** algorithm. - Common algorithms: **Raft**, **Paxos**, **Zab**. - The elected primary resumes service after synchronization with the other backups. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Advantages, Disadvantages & Failover **Advantages (2.5 marks):** 1. **Simplicity of Design:** Easy to implement compared to active replication. 2. **Strong Consistency:** Since only the primary orders operations, consistency is naturally linearizable. 3. **No Conflicting Writes:** Single point of write coordination eliminates write conflicts. 4. **Easy Reasoning:** Client behavior is simple — all operations go to one node. **Disadvantages (2.5 marks):** 1. **Single Point of Failure:** If the primary crashes and failover is slow, the system becomes unavailable. 2. **High Latency on Writes:** Synchronous propagation to backups increases write latency. 3. **Throughput Bottleneck:** The primary becomes a bottleneck for all writes. 4. **Split-Brain Risk:** Network partition may cause two backups to *both* think they are the new primary, leading to data divergence. 5. **Cost of Synchronous Replication:** High network bandwidth and storage overhead. **Failover Mechanism (2 marks):** - **Heartbeat Monitoring:** The primary sends periodic heartbeats to all backups. - **Failure Detection:** Backups declare the primary failed after a timeout `T`. - **Election:** A new primary is elected from the backups (lowest-ID, longest-running, or most up-to-date). - **Catch-up:** The new primary requests missed updates from remaining backups before serving traffic. - **Quorum Required:** A majority of backups must agree to avoid split-brain (used in **Raft** and **Paxos**). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students describe *active* replication features when asked about *passive* — this costs 2 marks. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Skipping the **synchronous vs asynchronous** distinction in passive replication — examiners award marks for clarity on this. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Not mentioning **split-brain** problem — this is a guaranteed 1-mark item for any 14-mark question on replication. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]** Explain the **Quorum-Based Replication Protocol**. With a suitable example, derive the condition $R + W > N$ and explain its significance in ensuring **data consistency**. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Quorum Concepts and Derivation **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definitions (1 mark):** - $N$ = total number of replicas of a data item. - $R$ = read quorum (number of replicas contacted for a read). - $W$ = write quorum (number of replicas that must acknowledge a write). **Step 2 — The Two Requirements (2 marks):** - **Requirement A (Fresh Read):** A read must see the result of the most recent write. - **Requirement B (No Lost Write):** A write must not be silently lost or overwritten. **Step 3 — Set-Theoretic Derivation (3 marks):** Let $W_{set}$ = the set of replicas that received the most recent write. Let $R_{set}$ = the set of replicas contacted by the current read. For the read to see the fresh value, we need $|W_{set} \cap R_{set}| \geq 1$. Since both sets are subsets of $N$ replicas: $$ \begin{aligned} |W_{set} \cup R_{set}| &\leq N \\ |W_{set}| + |R_{set}| - |W_{set} \cap R_{set}| &\leq N \end{aligned} $$ For guaranteed intersection of size at least 1: $$ |W_{set}| + |R_{set}| \geq N + 1 $$ Since $|W_{set}| \leq W$ and $|R_{set}| \leq R$, the necessary condition is: $$ R + W \geq N + 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R + W > N $$ **Step 4 — Example Calculation (1 mark):** - For $N = 5$: choose $R = 3, W = 3$. Then $R + W = 6 > 5$. ✓ - For $N = 3$: choose $R = 2, W = 2$. Then $R + W = 4 > 3$. ✓ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Engineering Significance & Trade-offs **Significance of $R + W > N$ (3 marks):** 1. **Strong Consistency:** Ensures every read returns the most recent committed value. 2. **No Read Staleness:** Guarantees the read set always overlaps with the write set. 3. **Configurable Trade-off:** Higher $W$ → faster reads, higher $R$ → faster writes. **Trade-off Analysis (2 marks):** | Configuration | Behavior | |---------------|----------| | $W = 1$ | Fast writes, slow reads (need $R = N$ to be consistent) | | $W = N$ | Slow writes (sync to all), fast reads (any 1) | | $W = R = (N+1)/2$ | Balanced; classic majority quorum | **Real-World Implementations (2 marks):** - **Amazon DynamoDB:** Default `(N=3, R=2, W=2)` → strong consistency. - **Cassandra:** Tunable per query — `ONE`, `QUORUM`, `ALL`. - **Riak:** Configurable `R, W, N` per bucket. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting the *strict* inequality `>` vs `≥`. KTU examiners specifically look for the `R + W > N` form — the `≥` version loses 1 mark. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Not explaining *why* the pigeonhole/set-intersection argument is used — this is the **core derivation step** and worth 2 marks. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Ignoring the *trade-off* between $R$ and $W$ — always mention that increasing $W$ slows writes but accelerates reads. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — memorize these bullet points the night before the KTU ESE exam.** - **Replication** = multiple identical copies of data on different nodes, coordinated by *replica managers*. - **Three Pillars:** *Reliability*, *Availability*, *Performance* — the main engineering motivations. - **Two Major Approaches:** *Passive (Primary-Backup)* and *Active (State-Machine)*. - **Passive Replication:** Primary executes, backups standby. Used in MySQL replication, PostgreSQL streaming. - **Active Replication:** All replicas execute every request in the *same order* using totally ordered multicast. - **Eager vs Lazy:** Eager = propagate before commit (strong consistency); Lazy = propagate after commit (eventual). - **Quorum Rule:** $R + W > N$ ensures fresh reads. $W > N/2$ prevents concurrent writes. - **Maximum Faults Tolerated:** $\lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ with majority quorum. - **Byzantine Fault Tolerance:** Requires $N \geq 3f + 1$ replicas. - **Consistency Spectrum (strict → weak):** Strict → Sequential → Causal → Read-your-Writes → Monotonic Reads → Eventual. - **CAP Theorem:** Partition tolerance is *mandatory*; you must choose between C and A during a partition. - **Eventual Consistency:** All replicas converge *if updates stop*; used by DynamoDB, Cassandra, DNS. - **Epidemic (Gossip) Protocols:** Spread updates in $O(\log N)$ rounds using exponential fanout; used in Cassandra membership, Consul, Consul. - **Anti-Entropy:** Background process that synchronizes replicas using Merkle trees or hash-based comparison. - **Read-Repair:** When a read detects a stale replica, it triggers an update to fix that replica. - **Hinted Handoff:** A node writes to another node on behalf of a temporarily failed replica; syncs back when the replica returns. - **Version Vectors / Vector Clocks:** Used to detect *concurrent updates* in eventually consistent systems; cannot prevent conflicts but can detect them. - **Conflict Resolution:** Last-Write-Wins (LWW), application-specific merge, CRDTs. - **Chain Replication:** Writes propagate head → tail, reads served from tail; used in Microsoft Azure Storage. - **Quorum Intersections Matter:** Always state both `R + W > N` AND `W > N/2` together. - **Paxos / Raft:** Consensus protocols used to elect leaders and replicate logs in strongly consistent systems. - **Split-Brain:** Two replicas both believe they are the primary after a partition — always mitigated by majority quorum. - **Storage Overhead:** $N$ replicas means $N$ times the storage cost — a key engineering trade-off. - **Network Cost:** Synchronous replication requires `N-1` message exchanges per write — bandwidth-bound. - **Real-world Examples to Mention in Answers:** DNS (lazy), HTTP Caches (lazy), MySQL Replication (passive), Kafka (active, partition-based), DynamoDB (quorum), Cassandra (tunable quorum), ZooKeeper (Zab consensus), Google Spanner (Paxos + TrueTime).

topics.title: Consensus Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Consensus Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In distributed systems, a **Consensus Algorithm** is a protocol through which a group of distributed processes (nodes) agree on a single common value or sequence of values, even in the presence of failures or network asynchrony. The agreement must be reached despite crashes, message losses, or in some models, malicious (Byzantine) behaviour of a subset of nodes.

Mathematically, every consensus protocol must satisfy four core properties for all correct processes $P_i$ and $P_j$:

1. **Agreement (Uniformity)** — No two correct processes decide on different values.
   $$\forall i, j \in Correct : Decided_i = Decided_j$$

2. **Validity (Non-triviality)** — The decided value must have been proposed by some process in the system.
   $$\forall i \in Correct : Decided_i \in \{v \mid v \text{ was proposed by some process}\}$$

3. **Integrity (Authenticity)** — Every correct process decides on at most one value.
   $$\forall i \in Correct : Decided_i \text{ is assigned at most once}$$

4. **Termination (Liveness)** — Every correct process eventually reaches a decision in finite time.
   $$\forall i \in Correct : \Diamond \text{Decided}_i \quad \text{(eventually decides)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Consensus is the cornerstone of state-machine replication, distributed databases (e.g., Spanner, CockroachDB, etcd), blockchain consensus (PoW, PoS, PBFT), and cluster coordination services like **ZooKeeper** and **etcd**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Boardroom Voting Problem

Imagine a corporate boardroom with **5 directors** sitting in different cities, connected only by unreliable couriers (network links). They must unanimously approve the next acquisition deal. Some directors may be:
- **Unreachable** (process crashes),
- **Slow to respond** (network delays),
- **Lying deliberately** (Byzantine / malicious fault).

The **consensus algorithm** is the "rulebook" that allows them to reach a single, agreed-upon decision. Just as the company needs certainty, a distributed database must guarantee that all replicas converge to the **same state** — a "single source of truth" — without ambiguity. This is the fundamental problem that consensus algorithms solve.

> [!NOTE]
> The "decision" in distributed systems is rarely a yes/no vote — it is most often the **next operation to apply to a replicated log** (e.g., `SET balance = 500`).

## 1.3 System Model & Failure Categories

Distributed consensus operates under specific assumptions about timing and failures:

| Model | Timing Assumption | Failure Type | Example System |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous** | Known upper bound on message delay & processing | Crash & Byzantine | Hardware TMR systems |
| **Partially Synchronous** | Bounds hold eventually (GST) | Crash & Byzantine | Raft, PBFT |
| **Asynchronous** | No timing assumptions | Crash only (FLP) | Theoretical FLP model |

**Failure Categories:**
- **Crash-stop failure** — Process halts permanently; no recovery. (Paxos, Raft model)
- **Crash-recovery failure** — Process halts, may restart with stable storage. (Practical Raft)
- **Byzantine (arbitrary) failure** — Process behaves maliciously, sends conflicting messages. (PBFT, Blockchain)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A node is called **faulty** if it deviates from the algorithm. The fraction of tolerated faulty nodes defines the **resilience bound** of the system.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FLP Impossibility Result (1985)

The **Fischer-Lynch-Paterson (FLP) theorem** is a foundational impossibility result in distributed computing:

> **Theorem (FLP, 1985):** In a purely **asynchronous** distributed system, **no deterministic consensus protocol can guarantee termination** in finite time if even **a single process may crash**.

**Implications for Engineers:**
- Real systems must relax **one of three properties**:
  1. Use **partial synchrony** (timing assumptions eventually hold — used by Raft, Paxos).
  2. Use **randomisation** (probabilistic termination — used by eventual leader oracles).
  3. Use **failure detectors** (e.g., $\Omega$ — perfect leader election oracle).

## 2.2 CAP Theorem & Its Connection to Consensus

**Brewer's CAP Theorem** (Eric Brewer, 2000; Gilbert & Lynch, 2002 proof):

A distributed data store **cannot simultaneously** provide all three guarantees:
- **C** — Consistency
- **A** — Availability
- **P** — Partition tolerance

$$\text{CAP:} \quad \neg (C \land A \land P)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since network partitions (P) are **inevitable** in real systems, the practical choice is **CP** (consistent, may be unavailable during partitions — e.g., ZooKeeper, etcd) or **AP** (available, may be inconsistent — e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB). Consensus algorithms fall into the **CP** category.

## 2.3 Byzantine Generals Problem (Lamport, Shostak, Pease, 1982)

The problem: A commanding general must coordinate an attack (or retreat) with $n$ lieutenant generals, some of whom may be **traitors**. They communicate only by messenger.

**Key Result:** Consensus is achievable in a synchronous system with $n$ nodes if and only if:

$$n \geq 3f + 1$$

where $f$ is the maximum number of Byzantine (malicious) nodes. The bound $3f+1$ arises because the system must tolerate $f$ traitors in both the messenger's side and the receiver's side, plus a deciding node.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Statement | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Agreement | $\forall i,j \in Correct : v_i = v_j$ | All correct nodes agree on one value |
| Validity | $v \in \text{ProposedSet}$ | Decided value must be validly proposed |
| Byzantine Resilience | $n \geq 3f + 1$ | Minimum replicas to tolerate $f$ traitors |
| Crash Resilience | $n \geq 2f + 1$ | Minimum replicas to tolerate $f$ crashes (quorum) |
| Quorum Size | $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$ | Majority quorum for Paxos/Raft |
| FLP Impossibility | Async + 1 crash $\Rightarrow$ No deterministic consensus | Use partial synchrony or randomness |
| CAP Trade-off | $\neg(C \land A \land P)$ | At most 2 of 3 guarantees |
| Paxos Round | Proposer → Prepare $\to$ Promise $\to$ Accept $\to$ Accepted | Two-phase protocol |
| Raft Term | Monotonically increasing logical clock | Detects stale leaders |
| Commit Quorum | $\lceil n/2 \rceil$ nodes ack an entry | Entry considered committed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **$3f+1$** bound is why production blockchain networks (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric, Tendermint) deploy 4, 7, 10, 13... validators. A 4-node cluster tolerates only **1** Byzantine failure; a 7-node cluster tolerates **2**; a 13-node cluster tolerates **4**.

## 2.5 Why Consensus Is Hard — The Core Dilemma

A correct consensus algorithm must handle **three sources of disagreement**:
1. **Concurrent proposals** — Multiple leaders propose different values simultaneously.
2. **Network delays & reordering** — Messages arrive out of order or never.
3. **Failures mid-protocol** — Leader crashes after partial replication.

> **The genius of Paxos & Raft** lies in using a **majority quorum** ($\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$) so that any two quorums intersect. This intersection ensures that a newly elected leader will always know about all previously committed values — the key to safety.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Byzantine Bound ($n \geq 3f+1$)

**Goal:** Prove that to tolerate $f$ Byzantine nodes among $n$ total nodes, we need $n \geq 3f+1$.

**Step 1 — Setup.** Consider a single commander $C$ sending an order $v$ to $n-1$ lieutenants. The lieutenants exchange their received values to reach agreement.

**Step 2 — Partition the network.** Split the system into three groups of equal size for worst-case analysis:
- Group A: $f$ traitors (lying lieutenants).
- Group B: $f$ traitors (lying lieutenants).
- Group C: $n - 2f$ honest lieutenants.

**Step 3 — Intersection condition.** For a lieutenant $L$ in Group A and a lieutenant $L'$ in Group B, after exchanging messages, each must obtain the **same** set of values. Therefore, the honest lieutenants seen by $L$ and $L'$ must be the same set.

**Step 4 — Worst case.** To make $L$ and $L'$ disagree, the adversary can:
- Send $L$ messages only from $f$ traitors and some honest lieutenants.
- Send $L'$ messages only from the other $f$ traitors and some honest lieutenants.

**Step 5 — Counting.** For $L$ and $L'$ to both have enough information, the total honest lieutenants must satisfy:

$$
n - 2f > f
$$

**Step 6 — Solve the inequality.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n - 2f &> f \\
n &> 3f \\
n &\geq 3f + 1 \quad \text{(since $n$ is an integer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum number of nodes required to reach Byzantine consensus is $\boxed{n_{\min} = 3f + 1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance) recommends **4** nodes (tolerates 1) or **7** nodes (tolerates 2) for production deployments.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis of the Paxos Algorithm

Paxos (Leslie Lamport, 1998) is the canonical consensus protocol. It proceeds in **phases** with roles: **Proposer**, **Acceptor**, and **Learner**.

### Phase 1: Prepare (Discovery)

**Step 1.** The Proposer selects a proposal number $n$ (globally unique, monotonically increasing) and sends `PREPARE(n)` to a **majority** of Acceptors.

**Step 2.** Each Acceptor responds with `PROMISE(n, n_accepted, v_accepted)` if and only if:
- $n$ is greater than any proposal number it has already responded to, **AND**
- It is willing to not accept any proposal numbered less than $n$.

If the Acceptor has already accepted a value, it returns the **highest-numbered accepted value** to the Proposer.

### Phase 2: Accept (Proposal)

**Step 3.** The Proposer waits for `PROMISE` from a **majority** of Acceptors.

**Step 4.** The Proposer selects the value to propose:

$$
v_{proposed} = \begin{cases} v_{accepted} & \text{if some Acceptor returned a previously accepted value} \\ v_{new} & \text{otherwise (Proposer's own value)} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5.** The Proposer sends `ACCEPT(n, v_proposed)` to the majority of Acceptors.

**Step 6.** Each Acceptor accepts the value **unless** it has already promised a higher-numbered proposal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accept}(n, v) &\iff n \geq n_{promised} \\
&\iff \text{Update } accepted = (n, v)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Phase 3: Learn (Dissemination)

**Step 7.** The Proposer sends `ACCEPTED(n, v)` to all Learners upon receiving a majority of `ACCEPTED` responses.

**Step 8.** Learners consider the value **chosen** once a majority of Acceptors have accepted it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety invariant of Paxos:** Two majorities always intersect in at least one Acceptor. This shared Acceptor ensures that the second Proposer, in Phase 1, will **learn about the previously chosen value** and re-propose it in Phase 2. This is the magic that guarantees Agreement.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Analysis of the Raft Algorithm

Raft (Diego Ongaro & John Ousterhout, 2014) is a **more understandable** alternative to Paxos. It decomposes consensus into three sub-problems:

### Sub-problem 1: Leader Election

**Step 1.** Each node has one of three states: `Follower`, `Candidate`, or `Leader`.

**Step 2.** A Follower increments its `electionTimeout` (random, 150–300 ms) and, on timeout with no heartbeat, transitions to `Candidate`.

**Step 3.** The Candidate:
- Increments its `currentTerm`.
- Votes for itself.
- Sends `RequestVote RPC` to all other nodes.

**Step 4.** A node grants its vote if and only if:
- It has not voted in this term, **AND**
- The candidate's log is **at least as up-to-date** as its own.

**Step 5.** The Candidate becomes `Leader` upon receiving votes from a **majority** ($\lceil n/2 \rceil$).

### Sub-problem 2: Log Replication

**Step 6.** The Leader receives a client command and appends it as a new `LogEntry` to its local log.

**Step 7.** The Leader sends `AppendEntries RPC` containing the new entry to all Followers.

**Step 8.** A Follower appends the entry to its log and replies with success.

**Step 9.** The Leader marks the entry as `committed` once it has been replicated to a **majority**, then applies it to its state machine and responds to the client.

**Step 10.** The Leader sends the new `commitIndex` in subsequent heartbeats.

### Sub-problem 3: Safety (Log Matching Property)

The **Log Matching Property** is guaranteed by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If two logs contain an entry with same (term, index),} \\
\text{then the logs are identical in all entries up to that index.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Election Restriction:** A candidate must have a log that is **at least as complete** as any other log in the cluster. This prevents a stale candidate from being elected and overwriting committed entries.

**Leader Completeness Property:** If a log entry is committed in term $T$, then every leader in any term $> T$ will also have that entry in its log.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Raft is easier than Paxos:** Raft uses a **strong leader** model — all log entries flow from the leader outward. Paxos allows any Proposer to drive consensus, which is more flexible but much harder to understand.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Paxos vs. Raft vs. PBFT

| Property | Paxos | Raft | PBFT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Failure Model** | Crash | Crash | Byzantine |
| **Min. Nodes (tolerate $f$)** | $2f+1$ | $2f+1$ | $3f+1$ |
| **Leader Model** | Weak (any Proposer) | Strong (1 Leader) | Strong (1 Primary) |
| **Communication Rounds** | 2 (Prepare, Accept) | 2 (RequestVote, Append) | 3 (Pre-prepare, Prepare, Commit) |
| **Understandability** | Very low | High | Medium |
| **Throughput** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Used In** | ZooKeeper (Zab) | etcd, Consul | Hyperledger, Tendermint |
| **View Changes** | Implicit | Term-based | Explicit view-number |
| **Liveness Guarantee** | Requires Leader Oracle | Randomized timeout | Requires view change |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Raft Consensus State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Follower

    Follower --> Candidate : electionTimeout expires
    Candidate --> Candidate : electionTimeout expires, split vote
    Candidate --> Follower : discovers higher term
    Candidate --> Leader : receives majority votes
    Leader --> Follower : discovers higher term

    Follower : state1 = Follower
    Follower : reset election timer on AppendEntries
    Candidate : state2 = Candidate
    Candidate : increment currentTerm
    Candidate : vote for self
    Candidate : send RequestVote RPC
    Leader : state3 = Leader
    Leader : send empty AppendEntries on heartbeat timeout
    Leader : accept client requests
```

## 4.2 Paxos Two-Phase Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant P as Proposer
    participant A1 as Acceptor 1
    participant A2 as Acceptor 2
    participant A3 as Acceptor 3
    participant L as Learner

    Note over P : Phase 1 - Prepare
    P->>A1: PREPARE n equals 5
    P->>A2: PREPARE n equals 5
    P->>A3: PREPARE n equals 5
    A1-->>P: PROMISE n equals 5 no prior
    A2-->>P: PROMISE n equals 5 no prior
    A3-->>P: PROMISE n equals 5 no prior

    Note over P : Phase 2 - Accept
    P->>A1: ACCEPT n equals 5 v equals X
    P->>A2: ACCEPT n equals 5 v equals X
    P->>A3: ACCEPT n equals 5 v equals X
    A1-->>P: ACCEPTED n equals 5 v equals X
    A2-->>P: ACCEPTED n equals 5 v equals X
    A3-->>P: ACCEPTED n equals 5 v equals X

    Note over P,L : Phase 3 - Learn
    P->>L: CHOSEN v equals X
```

## 4.3 PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client c] -->|REQUEST op| Primary[Primary node P]
    Primary -->|PRE PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup1[Backup B1]
    Primary -->|PRE PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup2[Backup B2]
    Primary -->|PRE PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup3[Backup B3]
    Primary -->|PRE PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup4[Backup B4]

    Backup1 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup2
    Backup1 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup3
    Backup1 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup4

    Backup2 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup1
    Backup2 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup3
    Backup2 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup4

    Backup3 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup1
    Backup3 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup2
    Backup3 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup4

    Backup4 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup1
    Backup4 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup2
    Backup4 -->|PREPARE view v seq n digest| Backup3

    Backup1 -->|COMMIT view v seq n digest| Primary
    Backup2 -->|COMMIT view v seq n digest| Primary
    Backup3 -->|COMMIT view v seq n digest| Primary
    Backup4 -->|COMMIT view v seq n digest| Primary

    Primary -->|REPLY result| Client
```

## 4.4 Consensus Decision Flow — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Request] --> B[Leader Receives Command]
    B --> C{Entry Already in Log?}
    C -->|No| D[Append to Leader Log]
    C -->|Yes| E[Return Existing Value]
    D --> F[Send AppendEntries RPC]
    F --> G[Followers Append to Log]
    G --> H{Replicated on Majority?}
    H -->|No| I[Wait and Retry]
    H -->|Yes| J[Mark Entry Committed]
    J --> K[Apply to State Machine]
    K --> L[Reply to Client]
    K --> M[Notify Learners]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Byzantine Generals Problem. State and explain the condition under which Byzantine agreement is achievable. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Byzantine Generals Problem**, formulated by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease (1982), describes the challenge of reaching agreement among $n$ distributed parties when some of them may behave **arbitrarily** (maliciously or "Byzantine") — sending conflicting, false, or no messages at all.

**Condition for solvability:** In a synchronous system with $n$ generals and at most $f$ traitors, Byzantine consensus is achievable **if and only if**:

$$
n \geq 3f + 1
$$

**Reasoning:** The system must be partitioned into three groups — two groups of $f$ traitors and a group of honest nodes. To ensure any two honest generals can reach the same conclusion by exchanging messages, the number of honest generals must exceed the number of traitors: $n - 2f > f$, which yields $n > 3f$, i.e., $n \geq 3f + 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> A correct protocol also requires signed messages (digital signatures) to prevent traitors from forging messages from other generals.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Paxos and Raft consensus algorithms. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Paxos | Raft |
|---|---|---|
| **Leader Model** | Weak — any node can be Proposer | Strong — exactly one Leader per term |
| **Understandability** | Low (Lamport's paper is famously cryptic) | High (designed for understandability) |
| **Sub-problems** | Monolithic (single protocol) | Decomposed: Leader Election, Log Replication, Safety |
| **Term / Round** | Proposal numbers | Logical terms (monotonic counter) |
| **Use Case** | ZooKeeper (Zab), Google Chubby | etcd, Consul, CockroachDB |
| **Log Structure** | Implicit (values, not entries) | Explicit log with (term, index, command) |

Both guarantee **safety** under crash failures and require a **majority quorum** ($\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$) for progress.

---

## Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Paxos & Raft Detailed Analysis `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q(a)** [7 Marks] — Explain the **three phases** of the Basic Paxos algorithm in detail. Show how the **majority quorum** property guarantees agreement.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

Basic Paxos achieves consensus through three phases. We consider $n$ Acceptors, of which at most $f$ may fail, and the system uses a **majority quorum** $Q = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$.

**Phase 1 — Prepare (Leader Election / Discovery):**
- The Proposer chooses a globally unique proposal number $n$ and sends `PREPARE(n)` to a majority of Acceptors.
- On receipt, an Acceptor responds with `PROMISE(n, n_accepted, v_accepted)` if $n$ is greater than any prior proposal number it has responded to.
- If the Acceptor has previously accepted a value, it returns that value with the proposal number.

**Phase 2 — Accept (Value Proposal):**
- Once the Proposer receives `PROMISE` from a majority of Acceptors, it picks a value:
  - If any Acceptor reported a previously accepted value, the Proposer **must re-propose** that value (to preserve any earlier decision).
  - Otherwise, it proposes its own value $v$.
- The Proposer sends `ACCEPT(n, v)` to the majority of Acceptors.
- An Acceptor accepts the value if $n$ is not smaller than any promise it has made.

**Phase 3 — Learn (Decision Dissemination):**
- Upon receiving `ACCEPTED(n, v)` from a majority, the value is **chosen**.
- The Proposer notifies all Learners, who apply it to their state machines.

**Why Quorum Guarantees Agreement:**

Two majorities $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ in a set of $n$ Acceptors must intersect:

$$
|Q_1| + |Q_2| = 2 \left\lfloor \frac{n}{2} \right\rfloor + 2 > n
$$

By the **Pigeonhole Principle**, $Q_1 \cap Q_2 \neq \emptyset$. Therefore, when a new leader runs Phase 1, it will learn from the intersecting Acceptor about the previously chosen value and re-propose it in Phase 2 — **never allowing two different values to be chosen**.

**Q(b)** [7 Marks] — Describe the **Raft consensus algorithm** with its three sub-problems. Explain the role of the **Log Matching Property** in ensuring safety.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

Raft is a consensus algorithm designed to be **understandable** while providing the same guarantees as Paxos. It decomposes consensus into three independent sub-problems.

**Sub-problem 1 — Leader Election:**
- Each node is initially a `Follower`. It has a randomized `electionTimeout` (150–300 ms).
- On timeout without receiving a heartbeat from a Leader, the Follower becomes a `Candidate`, increments its `currentTerm`, votes for itself, and issues `RequestVote RPC` to all other nodes.
- A node grants its vote if (a) it has not voted in this term, and (b) the candidate's log is **at least as up-to-date** as its own (by comparing last log index and term).
- The Candidate becomes the new `Leader` upon receiving votes from a **majority**.
- A split vote triggers a new election after a randomized timeout.

**Sub-problem 2 — Log Replication:**
- The Leader accepts client commands, appends them as `LogEntry(term, command)` to its local log, and sends `AppendEntries RPC` to all Followers.
- Followers append the entry and respond with success.
- The Leader marks the entry `committed` once it is stored on a **majority** of nodes. Once committed, the entry is applied to the state machine, and the result is returned to the client.
- The Leader periodically sends empty `AppendEntries` as heartbeats to maintain authority.

**Sub-problem 3 — Safety (Log Matching Property):**

The **Log Matching Property** is the cornerstone of Raft's safety:
> *If two logs contain an entry with the same (index, term), then the logs are identical in all preceding entries.*

This is preserved by:
- **Election Restriction:** Only candidates with sufficiently up-to-date logs can win an election (prevents a stale leader from overwriting committed entries).
- **Leader Completeness:** If a log entry is committed in term $T$, then every leader in all terms $> T$ will have that entry in its log.
- **AppendEntries consistency check:** When sending an entry at index $i$, the Leader includes the (term, index) of the preceding entry. The Follower rejects the append if its log does not contain a matching preceding entry.

Together, these ensure that **all committed entries are durable and never lost**, while the **Election Restriction** prevents safety violations during leader transitions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Note):** Many students forget to mention that Raft's **Election Restriction** is what distinguishes it from a naive "first to win votes" algorithm. Without it, a stale candidate with an incomplete log could be elected and overwrite committed entries — **a fatal safety violation**. Always mention this restriction in your answer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Byzantine Consensus & FLP Impossibility `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q(a)** [7 Marks] — State and explain the **FLP Impossibility Theorem**. Discuss the practical approaches used to circumvent it in real systems like Raft.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Statement of FLP (Fischer, Lynch, Paterson, 1985):**

> *In a fully asynchronous distributed system, there is no deterministic protocol that can solve the consensus problem in finite time if at least one process may fail (crash).*

**Explanation of the Proof Sketch:**

The proof proceeds by contradiction. Assume a deterministic consensus protocol exists. Consider a configuration $C$ where:
- Some process $p$ is crashed.
- The remaining processes are *indecisive* — they have not yet reached a decision.

From $C$, the protocol can transition to either:
- A configuration where decision is **reached** (but only with values already proposed), or
- A configuration where the processes remain **bivalent** (still undecided).

The key insight is that the protocol must be able to delay a message arbitrarily (asynchronous model). By strategically delaying messages, the adversary can keep the system in a bivalent state forever. The proof shows that **for any run of the protocol, there exists an indistinguishable run that never decides** — hence no deterministic consensus is possible in pure asynchrony.

**Practical Workarounds in Real Systems:**

| Approach | Mechanism | Used By |
|---|---|---|
| **Partial Synchrony** | Assume message delays are bounded *eventually* (after GST) | Raft, Paxos, PBFT |
| **Randomization** | Use coin flips to break symmetry (e.g., randomized leader election) | Ben-Or's algorithm |
| **Failure Detectors** | Add an abstract $\Omega$ oracle that eventually elects a stable leader | Chandra–Toueg |
| **Timeouts** | Real systems *do* have timing — use timeouts to detect crashes | Raft (150–300 ms timeout) |

Raft uses **timeouts + partial synchrony** to circumvent FLP. It assumes that the **Global Stabilization Time (GST)** exists, after which messages are delivered within bounded delay. During asynchrony, Raft may **fail to elect a leader** (liveness issue), but it will **never violate safety** (no two leaders can both think they are the leader). This is the standard safety/liveness trade-off in real systems.

**Q(b)** [7 Marks] — Explain the **Byzantine Generals Problem** with a system of 7 generals where at most 2 may be traitors. Show how the `OM(m)` algorithm reaches agreement using oral messages (no signatures).

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Problem Setup:**
- 7 generals (1 commander + 6 lieutenants).
- At most $f = 2$ are traitors.
- They communicate via **oral messages** (no cryptographic signatures).
- The commander proposes a value `Attack` or `Retreat`.

**Verification of the bound:**

$$
n \geq 3f + 1 \implies 7 \geq 3(2) + 1 = 7 \quad \checkmark
$$

The bound is **tight** — exactly 7 generals can tolerate exactly 2 traitors.

**The OM(m) Recursive Algorithm (Lamport et al.):**

For oral messages, the algorithm is called $OM(m)$ where $m$ is the maximum number of traitors. Each lieutenant $L_i$ maintains a value set $V_i$ initialized from the commander's message.

For our case, $m = 2$, so we use $OM(2)$, which recursively calls $OM(1)$ and $OM(0)$.

**Step 1 — Commander sends initial order.**
The commander sends `Attack` to all 6 lieutenants. (Assume commander is loyal; the case where commander is a traitor is symmetric.)

**Step 2 — Lieutenants exchange via $OM(1)$.**
Each lieutenant $L_i$ treats the value it received as a new "commander" and runs $OM(1)$ with the other 5 lieutenants. Specifically, $L_i$ acts as a commander and sends its received value to the other lieutenants, then collects their responses and runs $OM(0)$ to obtain a single value (by majority vote).

**Step 3 — Each lieutenant applies majority function.**
After $OM(1)$ recursion, each lieutenant has a value set of size 3 (from itself + 2 levels of recursion). It then applies the `majority(value_1, value_2, value_3)` function. Since at most 1 of these 3 can be a traitor's lie (under $f=2$ with $n=7$), the majority value is the true commander's order.

**Generalization of $OM(m)$:**

$$
OM(m) = \begin{cases} \text{majority of all values, if no recursion needed} \\ \text{For each lieutenant: act as commander and run } OM(m-1) \\ \text{Then apply majority to the resulting value set} \end{cases}
$$

**Why 3 levels of recursion for $m=2$:**

The recursion depth ensures that at every level, the **majority** of sub-protocols is run by loyal lieutenants. Since loyal lieutenants always report the same value, the majority function at the top level always returns the correct order.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A common student mistake is to claim that $OM(m)$ works for $m = 1$ with only 3 generals. This is correct ($3 \geq 3 \times 1 + 1$), but the recursion depth required is **$m$**, not $\lceil m/2 \rceil$. For $m=2$, you need 3 levels of recursion, not 2. Marks are often deducted for not justifying the recursion depth.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### Core Concepts
- **Consensus** = Agreement + Validity + Integrity + Termination.
- **FLP Impossibility**: No deterministic consensus in pure asynchrony with even 1 crash.
- **CAP Theorem**: Choose CP (consensus-based) or AP (eventually consistent) — consensus systems are CP.

### Algorithms to Master
- **Paxos** — Two phases (Prepare, Accept) + majority quorum + value re-proposal rule.
- **Raft** — Leader Election + Log Replication + Safety. Uses strong leader and terms.
- **PBFT** — Three phases (Pre-prepare, Prepare, Commit) + view changes for Byzantine fault tolerance.

### Critical Formulas
- **Byzantine resilience:** $n \geq 3f + 1$
- **Crash resilience:** $n \geq 2f + 1$
- **Quorum size:** $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$
- **Intersection property:** Any two majorities share at least one node.

### Real-World Mapping (Frequently Asked)
- **etcd, Consul** → Raft
- **ZooKeeper** → Zab (Paxos-like)
- **Spanner** → Paxos (Google)
- **Hyperledger Fabric** → PBFT variants
- **Bitcoin / Ethereum** → PoW (Nakamoto consensus)
- **Tendermint / Cosmos** → BFT-PoS

### Common KTU Exam Traps
1. **Forgetting Election Restriction in Raft** — Always mention it to get full marks.
2. **Confusing Paxos phases with Raft phases** — Paxos: Prepare/Accept. Raft: Election/Replication.
3. **Writing $n \geq f+1$ for Byzantine** — Wrong! The correct bound is **$3f+1$** (oral messages) or **$f+1$** (signed messages with cryptographic authentication).
4. **Skipping the recursion depth in $OM(m)$** — Always state the depth is $m$.
5. **Ignoring GST** — Real systems like Raft assume **partial synchrony** to bypass FLP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** When drawing diagrams in the exam, always label **all quorum sizes** and clearly mark the **leader / proposer / primary**. Examiners reward well-annotated diagrams generously (up to 2 bonus marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed File System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distributed File System Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed File System (DFS) Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Distributed File System (DFS)** is a distributed software system that implements file services across a network of interconnected computers, presenting a unified, transparent, and location-independent logical view of files stored on physically dispersed storage nodes. It allows multiple clients to access, share, and manipulate files as if they were stored locally, while the system handles naming, location, replication, caching, concurrency, and fault tolerance transparently.

The DFS is one of the most fundamental middleware services in distributed computing, forming the backbone of modern cloud storage, big data analytics, and enterprise computing. KTU 2024 Scheme treats DFS as a critical study area under middleware because it encapsulates all the core challenges of distributed systems — **transparency, scalability, consistency, fault tolerance, and concurrency control**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Goals of a DFS (as per Tanenbaum & Van Steen, foundational reference):**
> 1. **Network Transparency** – Hide the fact that files are remote.
> 2. **Access Transparency** – Both local and remote files accessed identically.
> 3. **Location Transparency** – File name does not reveal storage location.
> 4. **Failure Transparency** – Client & server faults handled invisibly.
> 5. **Replication Transparency** – Multiple copies managed transparently.
> 6. **Scalability** – Ability to grow to thousands of nodes.
> 7. **Heterogeneity** – Support diverse OS and hardware.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The National Library Card System"**
> Imagine a massive library system where books are physically stored in 500 branch libraries across Kerala, but every citizen holds **one universal library card**. When you request a book, you don't know (or care) which branch holds it, you don't know which physical copy you get, and if one branch is closed, another automatically serves you. The librarian's network transparently finds the nearest copy, lends it to you, and if you make notes in it, all future readers see the same updates (or at least a consistent version).
>
> **This is exactly how a DFS works:** the **"books"** are files, the **"branches"** are storage servers, your **"library card"** is the client interface (system call API), and the **"central cataloging system"** is the metadata service.

## 1.3 Why DFS? — The Motivation

A traditional file system is **physically bound to one machine's disks**. Distributed computing demands:
- **Data sharing** among geographically distributed users.
- **Storage scalability** beyond a single machine's capacity.
- **High availability** through replication.
- **Performance** via parallel access and caching.

Without a DFS, each machine would have an isolated "island" of files — defeating the very purpose of distributed computing.

## 1.4 Standard DFS Characteristics (KTU Expected Keywords)

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Transparency** | User unaware of file's physical location |
| **Concurrency** | Multiple clients access same file safely |
| **Replication** | Multiple copies maintained for availability |
| **Heterogeneity** | Cross-platform support |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Survives server/network failures |
| **Security** | Authentication, authorization, encryption |
| **Scalability** | Grows with nodes/petabytes added |
| **Caching** | Local copies for performance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A DFS is fundamentally a **client–server middleware application** layered on top of the operating system, providing standardized file service APIs (POSIX, NFS v4, SMB, HDFS API) over a network protocol stack.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Architecture of a DFS

A DFS has three logical tiers:

1. **Client Tier (Application Layer)** – Issues file operations (open, read, write, close).
2. **Service Tier (Middleware Layer)** – Interprets requests, enforces naming, security, concurrency. This is where **DFS middleware** lives.
3. **Storage Tier (Physical/Virtual Disks)** – Actual file data on local disks of file servers.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically calls out **"Middleware"** in the course title — the DFS service tier IS the middleware. It brokers between applications and the OS-level file system.

## 2.2 File Service Architecture — Three Components

According to standard distributed systems taxonomy, a file service has three core components:

| Component | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **File Service** | Operations on individual files (Read, Write) | NFS file operations |
| **Directory Service** | Operations on directories (Create, Lookup) | `/home/user/` mapping |
| **Naming Service** | Maps file names to physical locations | Mount table, NameNode in HDFS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinguishing Service vs. Server:** A *file service* is the **specification** (interface/contract). A *file server* is the **implementation** (process running on a machine). KTU examiners love this distinction.

## 2.3 File Models — How Files Are Organized

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes file modelling. There are three classical approaches:

### Model 1: Unstructured / Flat Files
- File is an opaque byte stream (e.g., Unix files, HDFS blocks).
- **No internal structure** imposed by the DFS.
- Most flexible, simplest.

### Model 2: Structured / Record-Oriented Files
- File contains a sequence of **records**, each with fixed fields.
- Operations: `get-record`, `put-record`, `append-record`.
- Used in legacy mainframe DFS (e.g., DFS on DEC VMS).

### Model 3: Tree-of-Byte-Sequences (Modern)
- Modern POSIX/HDFS approach: file is a tree of named byte sequences.
- Hierarchical directory structure with subdirectories.
- The dominant model today.

## 2.4 File Accessing Models — The 4 KTU Canonical Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** These four models appear frequently in 7- and 14-mark questions.

| # | Access Model | Mechanism | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Remote Access Model** | File operations (read/write) sent to server; data stays on server | High latency for bulk data; low network traffic for small ops |
| 2 | **Upload/Download Model** | Entire file transferred to client, modified, returned | High latency; efficient for heavy editing |
| 3 | **Mobile Code Model** | Code (e.g., Java applet) sent to data | Good for distributed computation; security risk |
| 4 | **Hybrid Model** | Combines upload/download with remote access | Balanced, used in modern systems |

### 2.4.1 Remote Access Model — Detailed Logic
1. Client opens a connection to the server.
2. Client sends **small read/write commands** (e.g., "read bytes 1000–2000").
3. Server executes on local file, returns only the requested portion.
4. Connection held during session.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** NFS v3 uses this model. `read(fd, buf, 1024, offset=1000)` becomes an RPC.

### 2.4.2 Upload/Download Model — Detailed Logic
1. Client requests the **entire file** (or large chunk) via `GET`.
2. Server ships whole file → client stores in **local cache/disk**.
3. Client edits locally → uploads modified file via `PUT`.
4. Server replaces old file with new version.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** FTP, WebDAV, HDFS read pattern (read whole block to local, process).

## 2.5 File Sharing Semantics — Consistency Models

When multiple users access the same file concurrently, what do they expect to see? This is the **sharing semantics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Topic — File Sharing Semantics. These appear in almost every 14-mark question.**

### Semantics 1: Unix Semantics (Strict / POSIX Semantics)
- **Every read sees the effect of all previous writes.**
- Implemented by **absolute synchronization** of all file operations.
- Imposes global time ordering — **expensive & hard in distributed systems**.
- Example: a write is immediately visible to *all* readers worldwide.

### Semantics 2: Session Semantics
- Writes are **visible only within the same session** (i.e., after the writer closes the file).
- Other open sessions see the **old version**.
- Used in early Andrew File System (AFS) and many practical systems.
- **Relaxed** vs. Unix semantics — fewer consistency conflicts.

### Semantics 3: Immutable-Shared Semantics
- Once a file is **declared shared**, it becomes **read-only**.
- No modifications allowed — concurrency problem eliminated.
- Used for **binaries, libraries, configuration files**.
- Cleanest, simplest — but restrictive.

### Semantics 4: Transaction-Like Semantics
- All file operations wrapped in **atomic transactions** (ACID).
- Either **all changes commit** or **none do**.
- Provides serializability via locking and 2PC.
- **Highest consistency, highest cost.**

| Semantics | Consistency | Performance | Implementation Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unix | ★★★★★ | ★ | ★★★★★ | Single-machine POSIX |
| Session | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | AFS, NFS v3 |
| Immutable | ★★★★★ (trivially) | ★★★★ | ★ | Binaries, archives |
| Transactional | ★★★★★ | ★ | ★★★★★ | Database-backed FS |

## 2.6 Caching in DFS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caching is the #1 performance optimization in DFS.** KTU 2024 weightage: high.

### 2.6.1 Cache Location
| Location | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Disk cache (client)** | Full file cached on client's local disk | Survives reboots; large capacity | Slow access |
| **Memory cache (client)** | File cached in client RAM | Very fast | Volatile; limited size |
| **Server cache** | Hot files held in server memory | Shared across clients | Server bottleneck |

### 2.6.2 Cache Update Policy
| Policy | Mechanism | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Write-Through** | Write to cache AND to server immediately | High consistency, slow writes |
| **Delayed Write (Write-Back)** | Write to cache; defer server write | Fast, but data loss risk |
| **Write-On-Close** | Server updated only when file closed (AFS) | Compromise; good for session semantics |

### 2.6.3 Cache Validation
- **Client-Initiated:** Client pings server to check if cached file is still valid (TTL, callback).
- **Server-Initiated:** Server **pushes invalidation** to clients holding the file (callback promise).
- AFS pioneered **callback-based invalidation** — central to its scalability.

## 2.7 Replication in DFS

Replication creates **multiple physical copies** of a file for:
- **Reliability** (survive disk/server failure).
- **Performance** (parallel read from nearest replica).
- **Scalability** (distribute read load).

### 2.7.1 Replication Strategies
| Strategy | Description | Example System |
|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous Replication** | Write completes only after all replicas updated | HDFS (write-once) |
| **Asynchronous Replication** | Write to one replica, propagate later | DNS, AFS |
| **Lazy Replication** | Replicas updated after commit | Cloud storage (S3) |
| **Eager Replication** | All replicas updated before commit | Distributed databases |

### 2.7.2 Replica Placement
- **Locality-aware:** Place replicas near expected clients.
- **Failure-domain separation:** Place replicas on different racks/datacenters.
- HDFS default: **3 replicas** — 1 on local node, 1 on local rack, 1 on different rack.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — Distributed File System Design

> [!NOTE]
> DFS has few "formulas" in the traditional sense, but key quantitative relationships exist for performance, scalability, and replication design.

| # | Relationship / Concept | Expression / Value | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Effective Access Time with Cache** | $T_{eff} = (1 - h) \cdot T_{remote} + h \cdot T_{cache}$ | $h$ = cache hit ratio |
| 2 | **Network Bandwidth Requirement** | $B = \dfrac{S \cdot N}{T_{window}}$ | $S$ = file size, $N$ = replicas, $T$ = time window |
| 3 | **Mean Time to Data Loss (MTTDL)** | $MTTDL \approx \dfrac{MTTF^2}{N \cdot (MTTR)}$ for N replicas | $MTTF$ = Mean Time To Failure, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair |
| 4 | **HDFS Default Block Size** | $\mathbf{128 \; MB}$ (or $\mathbf{64 \; MB}$ in older versions) | Block size for chunking files |
| 5 | **HDFS Default Replication Factor** | $\mathbf{3}$ | Number of copies maintained |
| 6 | **NFS Default UDP/TCP Port** | **Port 2049** | Standard NFS service port |
| 7 | **AFS Volume Quota** | Typically **5 GB to 25 GB** | Per-user volume limit |
| 8 | **Ceph CRUSH Map Hash Function** | $R = H(\text{object\_id}) \mod N$ | Maps objects to OSDs |
| 9 | **Cache Coherence Invalidation Time** | $T_{invalid} = T_{callback} + T_{propagation}$ | Time for invalidation message to reach client |
| 10 | **Scalability – NameNode Capacity** | $\approx \mathbf{10^8}$ files per NameNode (HDFS) | Practical limit of centralized metadata |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never use the pipe character `|` in any table cell above** — for absolute value notation in your answer scripts, use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX instead.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Storage (AWS S3, Azure Blob, Google Cloud Storage):** Direct commercial implementations of DFS with massive scale (trillions of objects).
- **Big Data Analytics (Hadoop HDFS):** Underlies the entire Hadoop/Spark ecosystem.
- **Web Infrastructure (CDNs, NFS mounts):** Serves content for millions of users.
- **Enterprise Storage (NetApp, GlusterFS, Ceph):** Mission-critical business data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Access Time with Caching

This is a **classic KTU derivation** that appears in numerical/analytical questions.

### Setup
A client makes a file access. If the data is in the local cache (probability $h$ = hit ratio), access takes time $T_{cache}$. Otherwise, it must fetch from the remote server, taking time $T_{remote}$. We wish to find the **effective average access time** $T_{eff}$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the two mutually exclusive outcomes:**
- Outcome A: Cache hit (probability $h$), time taken = $T_{cache}$
- Outcome B: Cache miss (probability $1 - h$), time taken = $T_{remote}$

**Step 2 — Apply the law of total expectation:**

$$
T_{eff} = P(\text{hit}) \cdot T_{cache} + P(\text{miss}) \cdot T_{remote}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $P(\text{miss}) = 1 - h$:**

$$
T_{eff} = h \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{remote}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand to show trade-off:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= h \cdot T_{cache} + T_{remote} - h \cdot T_{remote} \\
T_{eff} &= T_{remote} - h \cdot (T_{remote} - T_{cache})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** As $h$ increases (better caching), $T_{eff}$ decreases linearly toward $T_{cache}$. If $h = 0$ (no hits ever), $T_{eff} = T_{remote}$. If $h = 1$ (all hits), $T_{eff} = T_{cache}$.

### Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Question:** A DFS client has a cache with $T_{cache} = 0.5 \; ms$ (local RAM access). Remote server access time is $T_{remote} = 20 \; ms$. If the hit ratio is $h = 0.9$, calculate $T_{eff}$.

**Solution:**

$$
T_{eff} = 0.9 \cdot 0.5 + 0.1 \cdot 20
$$

Compute the first term:

$$
0.9 \cdot 0.5 = 0.45 \; ms
$$

Compute the second term:

$$
0.1 \cdot 20 = 2.0 \; ms
$$

Add:

$$
T_{eff} = 0.45 + 2.0 = 2.45 \; ms
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Caching reduced the effective access time by a factor of $\dfrac{20}{2.45} \approx 8.16\times$ — a substantial gain. This is why DFS designs invest heavily in caching.

## 3.2 Derivation: MTTDL for Replicated File System

This derivation is a **favourite 14-mark analytical question** in KTU.

### Setup
Each file server has a Mean Time To Failure ($MTTF$) and Mean Time To Repair ($MTTR$). We replicate the file on $N$ independent servers. We want the **Mean Time To Data Loss** — i.e., the expected time until *all* replicas are simultaneously unavailable (data lost).

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Express single-server availability:**

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability that *all* $N$ replicas fail simultaneously (assuming independence):**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = (1 - A_1)^N
$$

**Step 3 — Express single-server unavailability in terms of repair rate:**

Assume $MTTF \gg MTTR$ (common case). The "down time fraction" is:

$$
U = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTTF}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability that a single replica is unavailable when needed:**

$$
P_{\text{down}} = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTTF + MTTR} \approx \dfrac{MTTR}{MTTF}
$$

**Step 5 — Probability that *all* $N$ replicas are down:**

$$
P_{\text{all\_down}} = \left( \dfrac{MTTR}{MTTF} \right)^N
$$

**Step 6 — Mean Time To Data Loss (in expectation):**

$$
MTTDL = \dfrac{MTTF}{P_{\text{all\_down}}}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $P_{\text{all\_down}}$:**

$$
MTTDL = \dfrac{MTTF}{\left( \dfrac{MTTR}{MTTF} \right)^N}
$$

**Step 8 — Simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTDL &= MTTF \cdot \left( \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTR} \right)^N \\
      &= \dfrac{MTTF^{N+1}}{MTTR^N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Question:** A DFS stores files on servers with $MTTF = 10{,}000$ hours and $MTTR = 10$ hours. Compare $MTTDL$ for $N = 1, 2, 3$ replicas.

**Solution for N = 1:**

$$
MTTDL = \dfrac{MTTF^2}{MTTR} = \dfrac{10{,}000^2}{10} = 10{,}000{,}000 \; \text{hours}
$$

**Solution for N = 2:**

$$
MTTDL = \dfrac{MTTF^3}{MTTR^2} = \dfrac{(10{,}000)^3}{(10)^2} = 10^{12} \; \text{hours}
$$

**Solution for N = 3:**

$$
MTTDL = \dfrac{MTTF^4}{MTTR^3} = \dfrac{(10{,}000)^4}{(10)^3} = 10^{13} \; \text{hours}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Going from 1 → 2 replicas increases MTTDL by a factor of **100×**. Going from 2 → 3 adds another factor of **10×**. Replication dramatically improves availability — but with diminishing returns and storage cost overhead.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Three Canonical DFS Implementations

This matrix is **critical for KTU 14-mark comparison questions**.

| Feature | **NFS (Sun Microsystems, 1984)** | **AFS (Andrew FS, CMU, 1983)** | **HDFS (Apache, 2006)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Design Era** | LAN era, workstation networks | Campus-wide networks | Big data, petabyte scale |
| **Architecture** | Stateless client-server | Stateful (callbacks) | Master-slave (NameNode + DataNodes) |
| **Caching** | Client memory cache; no disk cache | Client disk + memory cache | No client cache; block-level |
| **Sharing Semantics** | Close-to-Unix (loose) | Session semantics | Write-once, read-many |
| **Replication** | None (manual) | Read-only replicas (volumes) | Automatic 3-way block replication |
| **Consistency** | Weak (eventual) | Callback-based strong | Strong (single writer) |
| **Failure Handling** | Stateless retries | Callbacks broken on failure | Heartbeats + re-replication |
| **Granularity** | File-level | File-level (whole-file caching) | Block-level (128 MB chunks) |
| **Scalability** | Hundreds of clients | Thousands of clients | Thousands of nodes, petabytes |
| **Access Model** | Remote access (RPC) | Upload/download (whole file) | Streaming read; append-only write |
| **Use Case** | UNIX file sharing in LAN | Campus distributed computing | Hadoop batch analytics |
| **Protocol** | ONC-RPC over UDP/TCP, port **2049** | UDP-based Venus protocol | Custom TCP-based, Hadoop RPC |
| **Auth** | UNIX UID/GID (kerberos in v4) | Kerberos | Kerberos / simple |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a clear **3-column comparison** in your answer scripts. The matrix above is the canonical structure examiners expect.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Andrew File System (AFS) Read Operation

AFS is a **favourite KTU topic** for 14-mark descriptive questions. Let us walk through a full read.

**Step 1 — Client's `open(filename)`:**
- The **Venus client process** (AFS client) intercepts the system call.
- Venus checks its local **cache manager**: is the file already cached?

**Step 2 — Cache hit path:**
- If cached AND callback is valid → return local file descriptor immediately. No network traffic. **Fast path.**

**Step 3 — Cache miss path:**
- Venus contacts the **Vice server** (file server) responsible for the file's volume.
- Sends: file identifier (FID) = `(volume\_id, vnode\_id, uniquifier)`.

**Step 4 — Server response:**
- Vice server returns the entire file (whole-file transfer in AFS).
- Venus stores file in client's local disk cache (typically under `/var/cache/afs/` or `/usr/vice/cache/`).

**Step 5 — Callback registration:**
- Vice server registers a **callback** for this client and file.
- If the file is later modified, the server **promises to notify** the client (invalidate the callback).

**Step 6 — Local open:**
- Venus returns a local file descriptor to the application.
- Subsequent `read()` calls are pure **local I/O** — no network.

**Step 7 — File modification (if any):**
- On `close()`, the modified file is **uploaded back** to the Vice server in one transfer.
- Server commits the change and **breaks all callbacks** for that file.

**Step 8 — Other clients notified:**
- When other clients next access the file, their **callback is invalid** → they refetch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the famous **"whole-file caching + callback"** paradigm that made AFS scalable to thousands of clients. NFS only later adopted similar concepts (delegation in v4).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General DFS Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    appA["APPLICATION LAYER<br>User Programs<br>Editors Compilers Browsers"]
    sysCall["SYSTEM CALL INTERFACE<br>open read write close<br>POSIX API"]
    venus["DFS CLIENT MIDDLEWARE<br>Venus NFS Client DFS Agent<br>Cache Manager Callback Handler"]
    localCache["LOCAL CACHE<br>Memory Cache<br>Disk Cache"]
    network["NETWORK LAYER<br>TCP IP UDP RPC<br>Sun ONC RPC"]
    serverA["FILE SERVER A<br>Vice Server<br>Volume Manager"]
    serverB["FILE SERVER B<br>Replica Mirror"]
    serverC["FILE SERVER C<br>Replica Mirror"]
    diskA["PHYSICAL DISK A<br>File Blocks"]
    diskB["PHYSICAL DISK B<br>File Blocks"]
    diskC["PHYSICAL DISK C<br>File Blocks"]
    metaService["METADATA SERVICE<br>NameNode Directory Map<br>Volume Location Database"]

    appA --> sysCall
    sysCall --> venus
    venus <--> localCache
    venus <--> network
    network <--> serverA
    network <--> serverB
    network <--> serverC
    network <--> metaService
    serverA --> diskA
    serverB --> diskB
    serverC --> diskC
    metaService -.controls.-> serverA
    metaService -.controls.-> serverB
    metaService -.controls.-> serverC
```

## 4.2 File Accessing Models — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    start["DFS Client Request"]
    model1["MODEL 1: REMOTE ACCESS<br>Send operations to server<br>Server holds the data"]
    model2["MODEL 2: UPLOAD DOWNLOAD<br>Fetch whole file to client<br>Modify locally, upload back"]
    model3["MODEL 3: MOBILE CODE<br>Send program to data<br>Code executes on server"]
    model4["MODEL 4: HYBRID<br>Combine approaches<br>Adaptive selection"]
    choice{"File Size<br>and<br>Operation Type?"}
    outcome["Operation Result<br>to Client"]

    start --> choice
    choice -->|Small ops frequent| model1
    choice -->|Large file heavy edit| model2
    choice -->|Compute on data| model3
    choice -->|Dynamic workload| model4
    model1 --> outcome
    model2 --> outcome
    model3 --> outcome
    model4 --> outcome
```

## 4.3 Cache Coherence in AFS — Callback Mechanism

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ClientA as Client A (Venus)
    participant Server as File Server (Vice)
    participant ClientB as Client B (Venus)

    ClientA->>Server: open(FileX) - cache miss
    Server-->>ClientA: Whole file + Callback granted
    Note over ClientA,Server: ClientA holds valid callback

    ClientB->>Server: open(FileX) - cache miss
    Server-->>ClientB: Whole file + Callback granted

    ClientA->>Server: write(FileX) - close
    Server->>Server: Commit new version
    Server-->>ClientA: ACK - write successful
    Server-XClientA: Callback BROKEN (invalidate)
    Server-XClientB: Callback BROKEN (invalidate)
    Note over ClientB,Server: ClientB callback is now INVALID

    ClientB->>Server: read(FileX) - local attempt
    Note over ClientB,Server: Detects invalid callback
    ClientB->>Server: fetch new version
    Server-->>ClientB: New file + New callback
```

## 4.4 HDFS Architecture — Master-Slave Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    hadoopClient["HADOOP CLIENT<br>FileSystem API<br>hdfs dfs put get"]
    nameNode["NAMENODE MASTER<br>Metadata in RAM<br>FsImage and EditLog<br>Block Map"]
    secondaryName["SECONDARY NAMENODE<br>Checkpoint Node<br>Merge FsImage and Edits"]
    dataNodeA["DATANODE 1<br>Stores Blocks<br>Heartbeat to NameNode"]
    dataNodeB["DATANODE 2<br>Stores Blocks<br>Heartbeat to NameNode"]
    dataNodeC["DATANODE 3<br>Stores Blocks<br>Heartbeat to NameNode"]
    blockA1["Block 1 Replica 1<br>128 MB chunk"]
    blockA2["Block 1 Replica 2<br>128 MB chunk"]
    blockA3["Block 1 Replica 3<br>128 MB chunk"]
    blockB1["Block 2 Replica 1<br>128 MB chunk"]
    blockB2["Block 2 Replica 2<br>128 MB chunk"]
    blockB3["Block 2 Replica 3<br>128 MB chunk"]

    hadoopClient --> nameNode
    hadoopClient --> dataNodeA
    hadoopClient --> dataNodeB
    hadoopClient --> dataNodeC
    nameNode -.controls.-> dataNodeA
    nameNode -.controls.-> dataNodeB
    nameNode -.controls.-> dataNodeC
    nameNode --> secondaryName
    dataNodeA --> blockA1
    dataNodeA --> blockB1
    dataNodeB --> blockA2
    dataNodeB --> blockB2
    dataNodeC --> blockA3
    dataNodeC --> blockB3
```

## 4.5 File Sharing Semantics Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    requirement["Application<br>Consistency<br>Requirement?"]
    unixPath["UNIX SEMANTICS<br>Strict global ordering<br>Read sees ALL writes<br>POSIX: open read write"]
    sessionPath["SESSION SEMANTICS<br>Writes visible after close<br>AFS style<br>Per session isolation"]
    immutablePath["IMMUTABLE SEMANTICS<br>Shared files are read only<br>No conflicts possible<br>Binaries archives"]
    txPath["TRANSACTIONAL SEMANTICS<br>ACID guarantees<br>Atomic commit rollback<br>2PC distributed locking"]

    requirement -->|Maximum consistency| unixPath
    requirement -->|Performance critical| sessionPath
    requirement -->|Static shared content| immutablePath
    requirement -->|Database style ops| txPath
```

> **Diagram Legend Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and labels are kept as raw uppercase alphanumeric text to avoid Mermaid parsing errors. No markdown bold/italic tags are embedded inside node label strings.
