<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Distributed File System. List any four characteristics of a DFS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Distributed File System (DFS)** is a software system that allows multiple users across a network to access, store, and share files as if they were stored locally on their own machines, while the system manages the physical distribution, replication, and synchronization transparently.

**Four characteristics of a DFS:**

1. **Network Transparency** – Users access remote files identically to local files.
2. **Location Transparency** – File names do not reveal physical storage location.
3. **Replication** – Multiple copies maintained for fault tolerance and performance.
4. **Concurrency Control** – Multiple users can safely access the same file simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **(Allocation: Definition – 1 mark; 4 characteristics – 0.5 mark each = 2 marks; total = 3 marks.)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is cache coherence? Explain callback-based cache coherence used in AFS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cache coherence** is the property of a distributed system that ensures all clients see a consistent version of a shared file, even though each may have a local cached copy.

**Callback-based cache coherence (used in AFS):**
- When a server delivers a file to a client, it registers a **callback** (a promise) for that file with that client.
- If the file is later modified by any client, the server **breaks all outstanding callbacks** by sending **invalidate messages** to every client holding the file.
- The next time those clients try to access the file, they detect the broken callback, refetch the new version, and register a fresh callback.

> [!NOTE]
> **(Allocation: Definition – 1 mark; Callback mechanism – 2 marks.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examinations follow a **module-based internal choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Distributed File System. Discuss the roles of the file service, directory service, and naming service.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Explain the different file sharing semantics used in distributed systems. Compare them in terms of consistency and performance.*

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

A **Distributed File System architecture** consists of three logical tiers: the **client tier** (applications issuing file operations), the **service tier** (middleware implementing file, directory, and naming logic), and the **storage tier** (physical disks on file servers). The service tier is the *middleware* layer in KTU's course nomenclature.

[Include a hand-drawn or printed block diagram in the exam script showing:

```
[Clients] → [DFS Middleware: File Service + Directory Service + Naming Service] → [File Servers with Disks]
```]

**Roles:**

1. **File Service:** Provides operations on the file's *contents* — `Read`, `Write`, `Append`, `Create`, `Delete`. The contract specifies *what* operations exist; the implementation may use flat or structured file models.

2. **Directory Service:** Provides operations on *directories* — `CreateDirectory`, `RemoveDirectory`, `List`, `Lookup`. It manages the hierarchical naming tree and maps directory entries to file identifiers (FIDs).

3. **Naming Service:** Provides *location transparency* by mapping human-readable names to file identifiers and then to physical server locations. In NFS, this is the **mount table**; in AFS, the **Volume Location Database (VLDB)**; in HDFS, the **NameNode's namespace image**.

The three services together implement Tanenbaum's classic *file service architecture* — they may be **collocated on a single server** (simpler, bottleneck risk) or **separated** (scalable, more complex, e.g., HDFS with separate NameNode + DataNodes).

> [!NOTE]
> **(Allocation: Diagram – 2 marks; Three services explained – 5 marks = 7 marks.)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**File sharing semantics** define the consistency guarantees observed by clients when multiple users access and modify the same file concurrently. Four canonical semantics are used in DFS:

1. **Unix Semantics (POSIX):** Every `read` reflects the effect of *all* previous `write`s. This requires global time ordering of all operations. Achieved in a single machine by locks; extremely expensive (or impossible) in a wide-area DFS. **Highest consistency, lowest performance.**

2. **Session Semantics:** Modifications made during a *session* (between `open` and `close`) are visible to the writer immediately but become visible to *other* sessions only after the writer closes the file. Concurrent sessions may see stale data temporarily. AFS uses a variant of this. **Good balance of consistency and performance.**

3. **Immutable Shared Semantics:** Once a file is declared *shared*, it becomes read-only for everyone. No concurrent modification is possible. Suitable for binaries, libraries, and shared configuration. **Consistency is trivially maintained, but flexibility is lost.**

4. **Transactional Semantics:** All file operations are bundled into *atomic transactions* satisfying ACID properties. Uses distributed locking and two-phase commit (2PC). Provides *serializability*. **Maximum guarantees, maximum overhead — typically used in database-oriented DFS.**

**Comparison table (include in answer):**

| Semantics | Consistency | Performance | Overhead |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unix | Very High | Low | Very High |
| Session | Medium | High | Medium |
| Immutable | High (trivially) | High | Low |
| Transactional | Very High | Low | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **(Allocation: Four semantics explained – 4 marks (1 mark each); Comparison table – 3 marks = 7 marks.)**

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Explain the Andrew File System (AFS) architecture. How does AFS achieve cache coherence using callbacks?*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Discuss the design and architecture of the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS). Explain the role of NameNode and DataNodes with respect to replication and fault tolerance.*

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

The **Andrew File System (AFS)** was developed at Carnegie Mellon University (1983) to provide a campus-wide file system for thousands of workstations. Its design prioritizes **scalability** and **reduced server load**.

**AFS Architecture:**
- **Vice Servers:** Dedicated file servers that store the actual file contents, organized into **volumes** (a volume is a collection of files, typically tied to a user or project).
- **Venus Processes:** User-space client daemons that intercept file system calls (open, read, write, close) issued by applications. Venus handles caching, network communication, and consistency.
- **Volume Location Database (VLDB):** Maps volume names to their server locations, providing **location transparency**.

**Whole-File Caching:**
- On `open()`, Venus fetches the **entire file** from the Vice server and stores it in the client's **local disk cache** (typically `/usr/vice/cache/`).
- Subsequent `read()` and `write()` calls are pure **local I/O** — no network round-trips.
- On `close()`, if the file was modified, the **entire file is uploaded back** to the Vice server in one transfer.

**Callback-Based Cache Coherence:**
- When a Vice server delivers a file to a Venus client, it registers a **callback** for that file with that client.
- If **any client** later writes to the file, the server commits the new version and **breaks all outstanding callbacks** by sending **invalidate messages** to every client holding the file.
- When a client whose callback was broken next attempts to access the file, it detects the broken callback, **refetches** the new version from the server, and **re-registers** a fresh callback.
- If a callback message is lost (e.g., server crash), clients can detect this via a **validation RPC** to the server.

> [!NOTE]
> **Benefits of callback-based coherence:** (1) **Server load is minimized** — no server-side tracking of who has what. (2) **Network traffic is reduced** — only invalidate messages, not continuous polling. (3) **Strong consistency on demand** — clients always see the latest committed version after invalidation.

> [!NOTE]
> **(Allocation: Architecture – 3 marks; Whole-file caching – 2 marks; Callback mechanism – 2 marks = 7 marks.)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

The **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** is a distributed, scalable, fault-tolerant file system designed for **batch processing of large datasets** (terabytes to petabytes) on commodity hardware. It is a key component of the Apache Hadoop ecosystem.

**HDFS Architecture — Master/Slave Model:**

1. **NameNode (Master):**
   - Stores all **filesystem metadata** in RAM: directory tree, file-to-block mapping, block locations.
   - Persists metadata to disk via two files: **FsImage** (snapshot) and **EditLog** (transaction log of changes).
   - Receives **heartbeats** and **block reports** from all DataNodes.
   - **Single point of metadata control** — NameNode failure = entire cluster failure.
   - For reliability, a **Secondary NameNode** periodically merges FsImage and EditLog into a checkpoint.
   - Modern Hadoop (2.x+) uses **High Availability (HA)** with active/standby NameNodes and **ZooKeeper** for failover.

2. **DataNodes (Slaves):**
   - Store the **actual file data** as **blocks** of fixed size (default **128 MB** in HDFS 2.x+).
   - On startup, contact NameNode and register; periodically send **heartbeats** (default every 3 seconds) and **block reports** (listing all blocks they hold).
   - Serve read/write requests from clients directly (clients talk to DataNodes for data, NameNode only for metadata).

**Replication and Fault Tolerance:**

- HDFS maintains **3 replicas** of every block by default.
- **Replica placement policy:**
  - 1st replica: on the local node (where the writer runs).
  - 2nd replica: on a different node in the **same rack**.
  - 3rd replica: on a **different rack** entirely.
- This balances **reliability** (rack failure tolerance) and **write performance** (most writes stay on one rack).
- **Fault tolerance flow:**
  - If a DataNode stops sending heartbeats, after a timeout (**10 minutes 30 seconds** by default) the NameNode **decommissions** it.
  - The NameNode instructs other DataNodes to **re-replicate** the lost blocks to restore the replication factor.
  - Corrupted blocks are detected via **checksums** stored in metadata; if detected, the block is re-replicated.

**Write-once, Read-many (WORM) Model:**
- HDFS files are **append-only** or **write-once**. There is no random in-place modification. This simplifies consistency drastically and enables high-throughput sequential reads, ideal for MapReduce/Spark batch jobs.

> [!NOTE]
> **(Allocation: Architecture – 2 marks; NameNode – 2 marks; DataNodes – 1 mark; Replication and fault tolerance – 2 marks = 7 marks.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks in DFS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "File Service" with "File Server":** The examiner expects you to clearly distinguish the *interface/specification* (file service) from the *implementation* (file server). A diagram showing the layering is highly valued.
>
> 2. **Mixing up AFS and NFS architectures:** AFS uses **whole-file disk caching + callback-based coherence**. NFS v3 uses **stateless block-level caching with no callbacks**. Mixing these loses 2-3 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the write-close protocol in AFS:** In AFS, writes are uploaded to the server **only on `close()`**. Mentioning this explicitly is a hallmark of a top-band answer.
>
> 4. **Omitting the three-tier architecture diagram:** KTU examiners award **2 marks specifically for a clean labelled diagram** of the DFS architecture. Skipping the diagram costs you nearly a full grade level.
>
> 5. **Confusing "replication factor" with "replica count for write":** HDFS default replication is **3**, but clients read from the **nearest** replica — not all 3 are read every time.
>
> 6. **Forgetting fault-tolerance flows:** Always describe what happens when a **DataNode fails** (NameNode timeout, re-replication). This is heavily weighted.
>
> 7. **Mishandling session vs. Unix semantics:** Many students say "AFS uses Unix semantics" — **wrong**. AFS uses **session semantics**, a deliberate relaxation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Distributed File System Design**

### Core Definitions
- **DFS:** Software system providing transparent file services over a network.
- **File Service:** Interface/contract of file operations (Read, Write, etc.).
- **File Server:** Process that implements the file service.
- **Middleware:** The service tier software in the 3-tier DFS architecture.

### Architecture Pillars
- **3-tier architecture:** Client → Middleware (File Service + Directory Service + Naming Service) → Storage.
- **File model types:** Unstructured (byte stream), Record-oriented, Tree-of-bytes.
- **Accessing models:** Remote access, Upload/Download, Mobile code, Hybrid.

### Sharing Semantics (must memorize all 4)
- **Unix:** Strict, expensive, single-machine.
- **Session:** Visible after close; AFS-style.
- **Immutable:** Read-only when shared; no conflicts.
- **Transactional:** ACID, atomic, 2PC.

### Caching (very high KTU weightage)
- **Locations:** Disk, Memory, Server.
- **Update policies:** Write-through, Write-back, Write-on-close.
- **Validation:** Client-initiated (poll) vs. Server-initiated (callback/push).

### Replication
- **Eager vs. Lazy:** Synchronous vs. asynchronous.
- **MTTDL formula:** $MTTDL = \dfrac{MTTF^{N+1}}{MTTR^N}$ for N replicas.
- **HDFS default:** 3 replicas; 128 MB blocks.

### System-Specific Quick Facts
- **NFS:** Stateless, port 2049, no callbacks, weak consistency.
- **AFS:** Whole-file caching, callbacks, session semantics, Kerberos.
- **HDFS:** Master-slave, NameNode + DataNodes, write-once, block-level, 3× replication.

### Numerical Formulas
- **Cache effective time:** $T_{eff} = h \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{remote}$
- **MTTDL:** $MTTDL = \dfrac{MTTF^{N+1}}{MTTR^N}$
- **HDFS block size:** 128 MB
- **NFS port:** 2049

### Key Differentiators to Memorize
| Feature | NFS | AFS | HDFS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cache Type | Memory | Disk | None (server-side) |
| Coherence | Stateless | Callback | Centralized metadata |
| Semantics | Unix-like | Session | Write-once |
| Replicas | Manual | Read-only volumes | Automatic 3× |

### ⚡ Examiner's Bonus Tips
- **Always include a diagram** for architecture questions — it earns 1.5–2 marks on its own.
- **Use tables** for comparison questions — they make your answer scannable.
- **Quote standard terminology** (e.g., "session semantics", "callback promise", "replication factor") — it signals conceptual mastery.
- **Mention real-world use cases** (Hadoop, NFS in Linux, AFS in universities) — bonus marks for applied awareness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distributed Databases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Distributed Database (DDB)** is a logically integrated collection of shared data that is physically distributed across the nodes of a computer network, where each site of the distributed system holds a portion of the data and is capable of executing local applications as well as global applications that require data from multiple sites. According to **Date's Twelve Rules** for a true distributed database, the system must exhibit *distribution transparency, autonomy, and continuous operation* (Date, C. J., *An Introduction to Database Systems*).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Distributed Database is a single logical database that is physically stored at multiple, geographically separated locations, managed by a **Distributed Database Management System (DDBMS)** that makes the distribution transparent to the user.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bank Network Analogy (Intuition Builder)**
> Imagine the **State Bank of India (SBI)**. You can walk into *any* branch in India — Delhi, Kerala, or Assam — and withdraw money from your account, check your balance, or request a statement. The teller at the local branch does not "see" your physical passbook; they just query a **central, distributed system**. To you (the user), the entire experience feels like one giant, unified bank. Behind the scenes, however, the data about your account is replicated or partitioned across thousands of servers in different cities. This is the **essence of a Distributed Database** — *one logical view, many physical storage sites*.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Site / Node** | A single computer location where part of the database is stored. |
| **DDBMS** | Software that manages the distributed database and provides distribution transparency. |
| **Local Transaction** | Accesses data only at the site where it was initiated. |
| **Global Transaction** | Accesses data at multiple sites. |
| **Fragmentation** | Breaking a relation into smaller logical pieces (horizontal or vertical). |
| **Replication** | Storing copies of the same data at multiple sites. |
| **Distribution Transparency** | Hiding the physical location of data from users. |
| **Autonomy** | A site's ability to operate independently of the DDBMS. |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU):** Focus on understanding the trade-offs between **replication** (availability vs. consistency) and **fragmentation** (locality vs. join cost). These are the two pillars of distributed database design.

## 1.4 Types of Distributed Databases

- **Homogeneous DDB**: All sites use the *same* DBMS software.
- **Heterogeneous DDB**: Different sites may use *different* DBMSs (e.g., Oracle at one site, MySQL at another), requiring *mediators* or *gateways*.
- **Client-Server DDB**: One central server holds the data; clients request it (not truly distributed).
- **Peer-to-Peer / Federated DDB**: All sites are equal peers; no single point of control (most common in modern cloud systems).

## 1.5 Architecture Classification

1. **Client-Server Architecture** (Tier 1)
2. **Collaborating Server Architecture** (Peer-to-Peer)
3. **Middleware-Assisted Architecture** (Most modern DDB)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of DDB Design

A distributed database is built upon **three design decisions**:

1. **Fragmentation** — Should we split the data?
2. **Replication** — Should we duplicate the data?
3. **Allocation** — Where should we place the fragments?

### 2.1.1 Fragmentation

**A. Horizontal Fragmentation**
A relation $R$ is split into disjoint subsets of *tuples* based on a selection predicate. Each fragment $R_i$ contains a subset of rows.

$$R_i = \sigma_{p_i}(R)$$

where $p_i$ is the selection predicate for fragment $i$.

**B. Vertical Fragmentation**
A relation is split into *columns* (attributes). Each fragment contains the primary key plus a subset of attributes, so that the relation can be reconstructed via natural join.

$$R = R_1 \bowtie R_2 \bowtie \dots \bowtie R_n$$

**C. Mixed (Hybrid) Fragmentation**
Apply horizontal fragmentation, then vertical, or vice versa. This is the most general and practical approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Correctness Rules (Yu & Özsu):**
> 1. **Completeness** — Every data item from $R$ must appear in at least one fragment.
> 2. **Reconstruction** — The original relation $R$ must be derivable from fragments via a reconstruction operator (e.g., $\cup$ for horizontal, $\bowtie$ for vertical).
> 3. **Disjointness** — No data item should appear in more than one horizontal fragment (except in the case of primary-key overlap in vertical fragmentation).

### 2.1.2 Replication

| Strategy | Description | Storage Cost | Availability |
|----------|-------------|--------------|--------------|
| **No Replication** | Each fragment at exactly one site. | Low | Low |
| **Full Replication** | Complete database at every site. | Very High | Very High |
| **Partial Replication** | Some fragments at multiple sites. | Moderate | Moderate |

### 2.1.3 Allocation Strategies

- **Centralized Allocation:** All fragments at one site.
- **Partitioned Allocation:** Each fragment at exactly one site (no replication).
- **Replicated Allocation:** Some or all fragments at multiple sites.

## 2.2 Distributed Query Processing (DQP)

When a user issues a query (e.g., an SQL `SELECT` with a `JOIN`), the DDBMS must decide *where* to execute each operation to **minimize cost** (typically, the sum of data transfer + local processing cost).

### Cost Function (KTU Standard)

$$Cost(Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{data\_transfer}(R_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} T_{local\_processing}(Op_j)$$

Where:
- $T_{data\_transfer}(R_i) = size(R_i) \times latency(R_i)$
- $T_{local\_processing}(Op_j) = I/O + CPU + memory cost$ of operation $j$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Communication Cost is the Dominant Factor:** In a distributed system, network I/O is orders of magnitude slower than local disk I/O. Hence, query optimizers prioritize **reducing the volume of data shipped across the network**.

## 2.3 Distributed Transaction Management (ACID in a Distributed World)

A distributed transaction must satisfy **ACID properties** across *multiple* sites:

- **Atomicity** — All-or-nothing execution.
- **Consistency** — Database moves from one valid state to another.
- **Isolation** — Concurrent transactions appear serial.
- **Durability** — Once committed, changes survive failures.

### 2.3.1 The Two-Phase Commit Protocol (2PC)

The 2PC protocol ensures atomicity of distributed transactions.

**Phase 1: Voting (Prepare)**
- The **Coordinator** sends a `PREPARE` message to all participating sites.
- Each site force-writes its log, acquires locks, and votes `YES` (ready) or `NO` (abort).

**Phase 2: Decision (Commit / Abort)**
- If all sites vote `YES`, coordinator sends `GLOBAL COMMIT`.
- If any site votes `NO` or times out, coordinator sends `GLOBAL ABORT`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that 2PC is a **blocking protocol**. If the coordinator crashes after sending PREPARE but before sending the decision, participants are *blocked* waiting for recovery. 2PC is **not fault-tolerant by itself**.

### 2.3.2 The Three-Phase Commit Protocol (3PC)

3PC was designed to overcome 2PC's blocking issue by adding a *Pre-Commit* phase. It is non-blocking only if there are **no network partitions**.

| Property | 2PC | 3PC |
|----------|-----|-----|
| **Phases** | 2 | 3 |
| **Blocking on Coordinator Crash** | Yes | No (under certain conditions) |
| **Complexity** | Moderate | High |
| **Message Overhead** | Lower | Higher |
| **Network Partition Tolerance** | Poor | Better |

## 2.4 Distributed Concurrency Control

### 2.4.1 Locking-Based Protocols

**A. Distributed Two-Phase Locking (2PL)**
- Local 2PL at each site + a global lock manager.
- A transaction must acquire a lock on data item before accessing it.

**B. Centralized 2PL**
- A *single* lock manager at one site handles all locks. Bottleneck risk.

**C. Distributed (Decentralized) 2PL**
- Lock managers at every site. Each manager handles locks for data at that site.

### 2.4.2 Timestamp-Based Protocols

Each transaction $T_i$ gets a unique timestamp $TS(T_i)$. The ordering of timestamps defines serialization order.

- **Thomas's Write Rule:** If a transaction with timestamp $T_i$ issues a write on a data item with a newer write timestamp, the write can be ignored (optimization).

### 2.4.3 Distributed Deadlocks

Deadlocks in distributed systems are *harder to detect* than in centralized systems.

**Detection Techniques:**
1. **Centralized Detection** — One site collects the global wait-for graph.
2. **Hierarchical Detection** — Sites are organized in a tree; each node detects deadlocks in its region.
3. **Distributed Detection (Path-Pushing)** — Sites exchange their local wait-for graphs.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** Be prepared to draw a **global wait-for graph** and identify a deadlock cycle in a multi-site transaction scenario.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---------|----------------|--------------|
| Horizontal Fragment | $R_i = \sigma_{p_i}(R)$ | Tuples |
| Vertical Reconstruction | $R = \pi_{A_1}(R_1) \bowtie \pi_{A_2}(R_2) \bowtie \dots$ | Attributes |
| Query Cost | $Cost(Q) = \sum T_{transfer} + \sum T_{local}$ | Seconds |
| Data Transfer Size | $Size(R) = n_{tuples} \times size_{tuple}$ | Bytes |
| 2PC Message Count | $4N - 1$ for $N$ sites | Messages |
| 3PC Message Count | $5N - 1$ for $N$ sites | Messages |
| Timestamp Order | $TS(T_i) < TS(T_j) \Rightarrow T_i$ precedes $T_j$ | Logical |
| Wait-For Edge | $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ means $T_i$ waits for $T_j$ | Graph |
| Replication Factor | $RF = \frac{\text{Total Copies}}{\text{Logical Items}}$ | Dimensionless |
| CAP Theorem | Pick at most 2 of: **C**onsistency, **A**vailability, **P**artition tolerance | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Finance** — Global transactions across ATM networks (e.g., SWIFT, Visa).
- **E-Commerce** — Amazon, Flipkart use distributed databases for product catalogs spread across continents.
- **Telecommunications** — Subscriber data replicated across regions for low-latency access.
- **Social Media** — Facebook's *TAO*, Google's *Spanner* (globally distributed, strongly consistent).
- **IoT & Sensor Networks** — Massive data ingestion across edge nodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Cost Calculation for a Distributed Join

**Problem:** A relation `EMP(emp_id, name, dept_id, salary)` with **10,000 tuples** is located at Site A. Another relation `DEPT(dept_id, dept_name)` with **100 tuples** is at Site B. We need to compute $EMP \bowtie DEPT$ at Site A. The size of each `EMP` tuple is **200 bytes**, and each `DEPT` tuple is **50 bytes**. The network transfer rate is **10,000 bytes/sec**, with latency **0.5 sec** per message.

**Step 1: Identify the Query**
We are computing $Q = EMP \bowtie DEPT$, executed at Site A.

**Step 2: Identify the Required Operation**
Since `DEPT` is at Site B but must be joined at Site A, we must **ship the entire `DEPT` relation** from Site B to Site A.

**Step 3: Calculate the Size of Data to be Transferred**

$$Size(DEPT) = n_{tuples} \times size_{tuple} = 100 \times 50 = 5000 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Data Transfer Time**

$$T_{transfer} = latency + \frac{Size(DEPT)}{bandwidth}$$

$$T_{transfer} = 0.5 + \frac{5000}{10000} = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ second}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Local Processing Time at Site A**
Assume a local join cost of $C_{local} = 0.05$ seconds.

**Step 6: Calculate the Total Cost**

$$Cost(Q) = T_{transfer} + T_{local} = 1.0 + 0.05 = 1.05 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 7: Optimization Insight**
Since `DEPT` is much smaller than `EMP` (100 vs 10,000 tuples), shipping `DEPT` is cheaper. This is the basis of the **Semi-Join Reduction** technique in distributed query optimization.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: 2PC Execution Trace

**Scenario:** A distributed transaction $T$ updates data at **3 sites** (S1, S2, S3), coordinated by **Site C**.

**Step-by-step execution:**

| Step | Action | Message |
|------|--------|---------|
| 1 | Coordinator C sends `PREPARE` to S1, S2, S3. | C → S1, S2, S3 |
| 2 | S1 writes log, locks data, votes. | S1 → C: `VOTE_YES` |
| 3 | S2 writes log, locks data, votes. | S2 → C: `VOTE_YES` |
| 4 | S3 writes log, locks data, votes. | S3 → C: `VOTE_NO` (insufficient disk) |
| 5 | C decides on `GLOBAL_ABORT` (since not all YES). | C → S1, S2, S3 |
| 6 | All participants abort locally and release locks. | Local action |

**Message Count Calculation:**

$$N_{messages} = (2 \times N) + (N - 1) + 1 = 4N - 1$$

For $N = 3$ sites: $4(3) - 1 = 11$ messages.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Deadlock Detection in a Distributed Wait-For Graph

**Scenario:** Three sites report the following local wait-for graphs:

- **Site S1:** $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$
- **Site S2:** $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$
- **Site S3:** $T_3 \rightarrow T_1$

**Step 1: Combine the local graphs into a global wait-for graph.**

**Step 2: Detect cycles.**

The combined graph has the cycle: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_1$.

**Step 3: Resolve the deadlock.**

- Choose the **youngest transaction** (highest timestamp) as the victim.
- Abort $T_1$ (or whichever has the lowest priority).
- $T_1$ releases all its locks; $T_2$ and $T_3$ can proceed.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of DDB Architectures

| Criterion | Client-Server | Peer-to-Peer | Federated | Cloud-Native (e.g., Spanner) |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------------------------|
| **Autonomy** | Low | High | Very High | Moderate |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low (SPOF) | High | Moderate | Very High |
| **Scalability** | Limited | High | High | Very High |
| **Consistency** | Strong | Eventual | Eventual | Strong (TrueTime) |
| **Complexity** | Low | High | Very High | Very High |
| **Use Case** | Small enterprises | P2P networks | Multidatabase systems | Google, Banking |

## 3.5 CAP Theorem Deep-Dive

**Brewer's CAP Theorem (2000):** In a distributed data store, you can have at most **two of the following three guarantees simultaneously**:

1. **C — Consistency:** Every read receives the most recent write or an error.
2. **A — Availability:** Every request receives a (non-error) response, without guarantee that it is the latest.
3. **P — Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite network partitions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Mapping:**
> - **CA Systems** (Consistency + Availability): Traditional RDBMS (Oracle, PostgreSQL) on a single node.
> - **CP Systems** (Consistency + Partition Tolerance): HBase, MongoDB (with strong settings), Google Spanner.
> - **AP Systems** (Availability + Partition Tolerance): Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB.

## 3.6 Quantitative Derivation: Replication Factor vs. Storage Cost

Given a database of size $D$ bytes, replicated across $N$ sites with **full replication**:

$$Storage_{Total} = D \times N$$

$$Bandwidth_{Update} = U \times N$$

where $U$ is the size of an update operation.

> **Engineering Trade-off:** Full replication maximizes read availability but **multiplies write cost linearly** with $N$. Partial replication with a replication factor $RF$ is:

$$Storage_{Total} = D \times RF$$

This is the foundation of systems like **Cassandra's tunable consistency**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Distributed Database Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph clientLayer["CLIENT LAYER"]
        app1["Application User 1"]
        app2["Application User 2"]
    end

    subgraph globalLayer["GLOBAL QUERY / TRANSACTION MANAGER"]
        gqm["Global Query Optimizer"]
        gtm["Global Transaction Manager"]
        lockmgr["Distributed Lock Manager"]
    end

    subgraph siteLayer["SITE LAYER - FRAGMENTED DATA"]
        site1["Site 1 - Fragment A1"]
        site2["Site 2 - Fragment A2"]
        site3["Site 3 - Fragment B1"]
        site4["Site 4 - Fragment B2 - Replica"]
    end

    app1 --> gqm
    app2 --> gtm
    gqm --> lockmgr
    gtm --> site1
    gtm --> site2
    gtm --> site3
    gtm --> site4
    site1 <--> site2
    site3 <--> site4
```

## 4.2 Two-Phase Commit (2PC) Protocol Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Coord as Coordinator C
    participant S1 as Site S1
    participant S2 as Site S2
    participant S3 as Site S3

    Coord->>S1: PREPARE
    Coord->>S2: PREPARE
    Coord->>S3: PREPARE

    S1->>Coord: VOTE YES
    S2->>Coord: VOTE YES
    S3->>Coord: VOTE NO

    Note over Coord: Decision: ABORT

    Coord->>S1: GLOBAL ABORT
    Coord->>S2: GLOBAL ABORT
    Coord->>S3: GLOBAL ABORT

    S1->>Coord: ACK
    S2->>Coord: ACK
    S3->>Coord: ACK
```

## 4.3 Fragmentation Strategy Tree

```mermaid
graph LR
    R["Relation R"] --> H["Horizontal Fragmentation"]
    R --> V["Vertical Fragmentation"]
    R --> M["Mixed Fragmentation"]

    H --> H1["R1 sigma P1"]
    H --> H2["R2 sigma P2"]
    H --> H3["R3 sigma P3"]

    V --> V1["R1 attributes AB"]
    V --> V2["R2 attributes CD"]
    V --> V3["R3 attributes EF"]

    M --> M1["H then V"]
    M --> M2["V then H"]
```

## 4.4 CAP Theorem Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
graph TB
    C["C - Consistency"]
    A["A - Availability"]
    P["P - Partition Tolerance"]

    C --- A
    A --- P
    P --- C

    C --> CA["CA Systems: RDBMS"]
    A --> AP["AP Systems: Cassandra, DynamoDB"]
    P --> CP["CP Systems: HBase, Spanner"]
```

## 4.5 Distributed Lock Management Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph masterNode["Master Lock Manager"]
        mgr["Central Lock Table"]
    end

    subgraph site1Node["Site 1"]
        local1["Local Lock Agent"]
    end

    subgraph site2Node["Site 2"]
        local2["Local Lock Agent"]
    end

    subgraph site3Node["Site 3"]
        local3["Local Lock Agent"]
    end

    local1 <--> mgr
    local2 <--> mgr
    local3 <--> mgr
```

## 4.6 Global Wait-For Graph for Deadlock Detection

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1["T1 at Site S1"] --> T2["T2 at Site S2"]
    T2 --> T3["T3 at Site S3"]
    T3 --> T1
    T3 --> T4["T4 at Site S1"]
```

> **Deadlock Identified:** The cycle $T1 \rightarrow T2 \rightarrow T3 \rightarrow T1$ indicates a deadlock. The **Distributed Deadlock Detector** must abort one of these transactions (typically the youngest).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Question 1: Define Distributed Database. Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous distributed databases. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A *Distributed Database* (DDB) is a collection of multiple, logically interrelated databases distributed over a computer network, where each site holds a subset of the data and is managed by a Distributed Database Management System (DDBMS).

| Feature | Homogeneous DDB | Heterogeneous DDB |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **DBMS Software** | Same across all sites | Different (e.g., Oracle + MySQL) |
| **Data Model** | Identical | May differ |
| **Query Language** | Single SQL dialect | Multiple dialects / gateways |
| **Complexity** | Lower | Higher |
| **Example** | Multi-node Oracle RAC | Federated systems with mixed DBs |

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

### Question 2: What is the Two-Phase Commit (2PC) protocol? Why is it called *blocking*? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **Two-Phase Commit (2PC) protocol** is a distributed algorithm that ensures **atomicity** of a transaction spanning multiple sites. It has two phases:
1. **Voting Phase (Prepare):** The coordinator asks all participants if they are ready to commit.
2. **Decision Phase (Commit/Abort):** Based on the votes, the coordinator decides and broadcasts the result.

It is called **blocking** because if the **coordinator crashes** after sending `PREPARE` but before broadcasting the global decision, the participants remain in an *uncertain state* — they have voted `YES` and are holding locks, waiting for a decision that may never come. They cannot unilaterally commit or abort, and hence the protocol is said to **block**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper, Module 4]**

### Question A: Distributed Database Design and Query Processing

**(a)** Explain the three fragmentation strategies (horizontal, vertical, mixed) with suitable examples. State the correctness rules for fragmentation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Consider the following `EMPLOYEE` relation:

$$EMPLOYEE(emp\_id, name, dept\_id, salary)$$

The relation is fragmented **horizontally** based on `dept_id`:
- `E1 = σ_{dept_id ≤ 10}(EMPLOYEE)` — stored at **Site 1**
- `E2 = σ_{10 < dept_id ≤ 20}(EMPLOYEE)` — stored at **Site 2**
- `E3 = σ_{dept_id > 20}(EMPLOYEE)` — stored at **Site 3**

Given that a user at Site 1 issues the query: `SELECT * FROM EMPLOYEE WHERE dept_id = 15`, identify which site will be queried, and describe the **distributed query processing** steps, including any optimizations. (7 Marks)

---

### Question B: Distributed Transaction Management and Concurrency Control

**(a)** Describe the **Two-Phase Commit (2PC)** and **Three-Phase Commit (3PC)** protocols in detail. Compare them in terms of blocking behavior, message count, and fault tolerance. (7 Marks)

**(b)** In a distributed system with three sites (S1, S2, S3), the following transactions are in progress:

- `T1` at S1 holds lock on item `X` and is waiting for `Y` (held by `T2` at S2).
- `T2` at S2 holds lock on `Y` and is waiting for `Z` (held by `T3` at S3).
- `T3` at S3 holds lock on `Z` and is waiting for `X` (held by `T1` at S1).

**(i)** Draw the global wait-for graph and identify the deadlock. (3 Marks)
**(ii)** Explain how a distributed deadlock detection algorithm would resolve this. Which transaction should be aborted? Justify. (4 Marks)

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A (a): Fragmentation

**Horizontal Fragmentation:** Splits a relation `R` into disjoint subsets of tuples using a selection predicate.

**Example:** A `STUDENT` table can be split into:
- `S1 = σ_{branch = 'CS'}(STUDENT)`
- `S2 = σ_{branch = 'EC'}(STUDENT)`
- `S3 = σ_{branch = 'ME'}(STUDENT)`

**Vertical Fragmentation:** Splits a relation by columns. Each fragment must contain the **primary key** to enable reconstruction.

**Example:**
- `S_Academic = π_{roll_no, name, gpa}(STUDENT)`
- `S_Personal = π_{roll_no, address, phone}(STUDENT)`

**Mixed Fragmentation:** Combines both — first horizontal split, then vertical (or vice versa).

**Correctness Rules (Yu & Özsu, 1995):**
1. **Completeness:** Every data item in `R` must appear in at least one fragment.
2. **Reconstruction:** The original relation `R` must be reconstructable from fragments using a lossless decomposition.
3. **Disjointness:** No data item should appear in more than one fragment (for horizontal), except the primary key in vertical.

### Solution to Question A (b): Distributed Query Processing

**Step 1: Locate the target fragment.**
The query `WHERE dept_id = 15` falls into the range $10 < 15 \leq 20$, which corresponds to fragment `E2` at **Site 2**.

**Step 2: Send the query to Site 2.**
The query optimizer at Site 1 identifies that this is a **localizable** query — it only needs data from Site 2.

**Step 3: Execute locally at Site 2.**
Site 2 scans fragment `E2` and returns the matching tuples to Site 1.

**Step 4: Optimization via localization.**
If the query optimizer maintains a **fragmentation catalog**, it can determine that the entire query can be executed at a single site, **eliminating the need for inter-site data transfer**. This is called **localization** and dramatically reduces network cost.

**Cost Calculation (optional):**
Assuming 1000 tuples in `E2`, each 100 bytes:
$$Transfer_{cost} = latency + \frac{1000 \times 100}{bandwidth}$$

### Solution to Question B (a): 2PC vs 3PC

**2PC Protocol:**

**Phase 1 (Prepare):**
- Coordinator C sends `PREPARE` to all participants.
- Each participant force-writes its log, acquires locks, and votes.
- Participants respond with `VOTE_COMMIT` or `VOTE_ABORT`.

**Phase 2 (Commit/Abort):**
- If all votes are `VOTE_COMMIT`, C writes a `COMMIT` log record and broadcasts `GLOBAL_COMMIT`.
- Otherwise, C writes `ABORT` and broadcasts `GLOBAL_ABORT`.
- Participants acknowledge and release locks.

**3PC Protocol (3 Phases):**

**Phase 1 (CanCommit):** Coordinator asks if all sites *can* commit (checks resource availability).

**Phase 2 (PreCommit):** If all reply `YES`, coordinator writes `PRECOMMIT` log and broadcasts `PRECOMMIT`. Participants acknowledge.

**Phase 3 (doCommit):** Coordinator sends `doCOMMIT`. Participants commit and acknowledge.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | 2PC | 3PC |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Phases** | 2 | 3 |
| **Blocking on Coordinator Crash** | Yes (after PREPARE) | No (after PRECOMMIT) |
| **Network Partition Tolerance** | Poor | Better (but not perfect) |
| **Message Count** | $4N - 1$ | $5N - 1$ |
| **Synchrony Assumption** | Synchronous | Requires bounded delays |
| **Practical Use** | Common in industry | Rarely used in practice |

### Solution to Question B (b): Distributed Deadlock Detection

**(i) Global Wait-For Graph:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1["T1 at S1"] --> T2["T2 at S2"]
    T2 --> T3["T3 at S3"]
    T3 --> T1
```

**Cycle Detected:** $T1 \rightarrow T2 \rightarrow T3 \rightarrow T1$ — a **distributed deadlock** spanning all three sites.

**(ii) Detection and Resolution:**

A **Distributed Deadlock Detection Algorithm (e.g., Obermarck's Algorithm)** would:
1. Each site constructs its **local wait-for graph**.
2. Sites exchange their graphs (or paths) periodically.
3. The algorithm builds a **global wait-for graph** by merging local graphs.
4. A **cycle** in the global graph indicates a deadlock.

**Transaction to Abort:**
Using the **youngest transaction** (highest timestamp) as the victim:
- If timestamps are $TS(T1) = 1$, $TS(T2) = 2$, $TS(T3) = 3$, then **$T3$ is aborted**.
- $T3$ releases its lock on `Z`, breaking the cycle.
- $T2$ can now proceed (acquires `Z`), finishes, releases `Y`.
- $T1$ can then acquire `Y` and complete.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't forget the correctness rules** in fragmentation — completeness, reconstruction, disjointness. Students often skip these and lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. **2PC is blocking, not fault-tolerant.** Students often write "2PC handles coordinator crashes" — this is incorrect. The protocol blocks participants.
> 3. **Cost function for distributed queries** must include both *transfer cost* and *local processing cost*. Don't forget either.
> 4. **CAP theorem:** Many students incorrectly say "you can have all three" — you can only pick **two**.
> 5. **Wait-for graph edges:** An edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ means $T_i$ is **waiting for** a lock held by $T_j$. Reversing this direction is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4: Distributed Databases**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Definition:** A distributed database is a single *logical* database physically distributed across multiple sites.
- [ ] **DDBMS:** The software that makes distribution transparent to users.
- [ ] **Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous** — same DBMS vs different DBMS at different sites.

### Design Decisions
- [ ] **Fragmentation:** Horizontal (rows), Vertical (columns), Mixed (both).
- [ ] **Correctness Rules:** Completeness, Reconstruction, Disjointness.
- [ ] **Replication:** No / Partial / Full — each has trade-offs in cost vs. availability.
- [ ] **Allocation:** Centralized, Partitioned, Replicated.

### Query Processing
- [ ] **Cost function** = transfer cost + local processing cost.
- [ ] **Localization** optimization: route queries to the site that holds the needed fragment.
- [ ] **Semi-Join Reduction**: ship only the join attributes to reduce data movement.

### Transaction Management
- [ ] **2PC phases:** Prepare (vote) + Commit/Abort (decision).
- [ ] **2PC is BLOCKING** — coordinator crash leaves participants waiting.
- [ ] **3PC adds a Pre-Commit phase** to avoid blocking (under bounded delays).
- [ ] **Message count:** 2PC = $4N-1$, 3PC = $5N-1$.

### Concurrency Control
- [ ] **Distributed 2PL:** Centralized, Distributed, Hierarchical lock managers.
- [ ] **Timestamp Ordering:** $TS(T_i) < TS(T_j) \Rightarrow T_i$ precedes $T_j$.
- [ ] **Deadlock Detection:** Centralized, Hierarchical, Path-Pushing.
- [ ] **Resolution:** Abort the youngest transaction (highest timestamp).

### Advanced Topics
- [ ] **CAP Theorem:** Pick 2 of {Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance}.
- [ ] **Replication Factor (RF)** controls the trade-off between availability and update cost.
- [ ] **Real-world systems:** Google Spanner (CP), Cassandra (AP), Oracle RAC (CA-like).

### High-Yield Mnemonics
- **Fragmentation Rules:** **C**omplete, **R**econstruct, **D**isjoint → **"CRD"**.
- **2PC Phases:** **P**repare + **C**ommit/Abort → **"PC"**.
- **CAP:** **C**onsistency, **A**vailability, **P**artition → **"CAP"** (you can pick 2).

### Frequently Confused Terms
- **Fragmentation** ≠ **Replication** — fragmentation *splits* data; replication *copies* data.
- **Local transaction** ≠ **Global transaction** — local = single site; global = multiple sites.
- **Locking** vs **Timestamping** — pessimistic vs. optimistic concurrency control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Consistency Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — CONSISTENCY MODELS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Consistency Model** is a *contract* between a distributed system (or shared-memory / replicated data store) and its clients, which precisely defines the **set of permissible execution orders** of read and write operations observed across multiple processes or replicas. In simpler terms, it specifies **"what value a read can return"** when multiple clients access shared/replicated data concurrently from geographically distributed nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** A *consistency model* is a set of rules governing the ordering and visibility of operations in a distributed shared memory or replicated data store. It lies in the **consistency spectrum** spanning from *strict* (behaves like a single machine) to *eventual* (high availability, weak guarantees).

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Notice Board"

Imagine a **college notice board** shared by 100 students in a hostel:

- **Strict Consistency:** Whenever a student pins a new notice, *every other student who looks at the board immediately sees that exact notice*. The notice board behaves like a single physical object.
- **Eventual Consistency:** A student pins a notice. Other students may see the *old notice* for some time. Eventually (after updates propagate), everyone sees the new notice.
- **Sequential Consistency:** All students agree on **some single global order** of notices being pinned, even if the actual physical pinning happened in a different order. Everyone sees notices in the *same agreed order*.

> [!NOTE]
> The weaker the model → the higher the **availability** and **performance**, but the **harder** it is to program distributed applications correctly.

### 🔑 Classification of Consistency Models (High-Yield Map)

| Spectrum Position | Consistency Model | Guarantees |
|---|---|---|
| Strongest | **Strict (Atomic)** | Real-time ordering of all operations |
| ↓ | **Linearizability** | Real-time ordering + sequential order |
| ↓ | **Sequential** | Single global order (not real-time) |
| ↓ | **Causal** | Respects cause–effect only |
| ↓ | **PRAM / FIFO** | Single-process ordering only |
| ↓ | **Weak** | Sync ops only (barriers) |
| ↓ | **Release** | Different barriers for acquire/release |
| Weakest | **Eventual** | Convergence if no new writes |

### 📏 Important Metrics & Notations

- **$h_i[x]$** → history of operations on process $i$ on data item $x$
- **$\rightarrow$** → happens-before (causal) relation
- **$W_i(x)v$** → write by process $i$ to $x$ with value $v$
- **$R_i(x)v$** → read by process $i$ from $x$ returning value $v$
- **$\pi$** → a serialization (legal interleaving) of operations
- **$\prec_{RT}$** → real-time precedence
- **$\prec_{PO}$** → program order
- **$\prec_{SO}$** → sequential order (in SC)
- **$\leq_t$** → wall-clock (real-time) precedence

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** When asked "is sequential consistency stronger than causal consistency?" — answer **YES**. Because SC imposes a *total order* of all operations, while Causal only imposes a *partial order* on dependent operations. Total ⊃ Partial.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔍 2.1 Strict (Atomic) Consistency — The "Ideal Single Copy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Tanenbaum):** A shared-memory system is **strictly consistent** if the value returned by a read on data item $x$ is the value of the **most recent write** on $x$, as determined by **absolute global wall-clock time**.

### Operational Logic Steps
1. Every write is **instantaneously visible** to all processes.
2. Reads always return the value of the **last write in real time**.
3. Implies the system behaves as if there is a **single, indivisible memory**.

### Mathematical Formulation
For any two operations $op_1$ and $op_2$ on the same data item $x$:

$$op_1 \rightarrow_{RT} op_2 \;\land\; op_1 = W(x) \;\Rightarrow\; \forall R(x) \text{ after } op_2: R(x) \text{ sees } op_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict consistency is impossible to implement in a real distributed system** because it requires a perfectly synchronized global clock and zero-latency propagation.

---

## 🔍 2.2 Linearizability

### Definition
**Linearizability (L)** is the **strongest possible** real-time consistency model *that can be implemented*. It is the **local, non-blocking** analog of strict consistency.

### Operational Steps
1. Each operation is assigned an **atomic interval** $[t_s, t_e]$.
2. There must exist a **total order** $\pi$ of all completed operations.
3. If $op_1$ finishes before $op_2$ begins (real time), then $op_1 \prec_{\pi} op_2$.
4. Program order of each process is preserved.

### Mathematical Formulation
$$\forall\, op_1, op_2:\ op_1.\text{endTime} < op_2.\text{startTime} \;\Rightarrow\; op_1 \prec_{\pi} op_2$$

---

## 🔍 2.3 Sequential Consistency (SC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lamport (1979) Definition:** A system is sequentially consistent if the result of any execution is the same as if the operations of all processes were executed in **some sequential order**, and the operations of each individual process appear in this sequence in the **order specified by its program**.

### Conditions for Sequential Consistency
1. **Local Order (Program Order):** $\forall i : op_i^a \prec_{PO} op_i^b \Rightarrow op_i^a \prec_{SO} op_i^b$
2. **Global Order (Single Interleaving):** A total order $\pi$ exists across all operations.
3. **Read sees latest preceding write in $\pi$.**

### Why SC ≠ Strict?
SC does **not** require real-time ordering — only a *legal total order* exists.

---

## 🔍 2.4 Causal Consistency (CC)

### Core Intuition
Operations that are **causally related** must be seen by all processes in the same order. **Concurrent (independent)** operations may be seen in different orders by different processes.

### Happens-Before (Causality) Rule
$$e_i^a \rightarrow e_j^b \;\Leftrightarrow\; (i = j \;\land\; a \text{ precedes } b \text{ in } PO) \;\lor\; (\exists c: e_i^a \rightarrow e_k^c \rightarrow e_j^b)$$

### Operational Steps
1. If $W(x) \rightarrow W(y)$ (potential dependency), every process must see $W(x)$ before $W(y)$.
2. Concurrent writes may be observed in any order across processes.
3. **No global total order** is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Causal Consistency ⊂ Sequential Consistency** (strict subset). SC implies CC, but not vice versa.

---

## 🔍 2.5 PRAM (FIFO) Consistency

### Definition (Goodman & Woest)
**Pipelined Random Access Memory** consistency: writes by a **single process** are seen in order, but writes by different processes can be seen in **different orders** by different observers.

### Conditions
- $W_i \prec_{PO} W_i' \Rightarrow W_i \prec_{vis} W_i'$ (own writes ordered)
- Different process writes: **NO order** is guaranteed across processes.

---

## 🔍 2.6 Weak Consistency (W)

### Definition
A model that introduces **synchronization operations** ($S$) which act as global barriers.

### Conditions
1. All accesses to synchronization variables are **sequentially consistent**.
2. **No operation on a data item** can be performed until **all previous sync operations** are completed everywhere.
3. **No read/write of sync variables** can be performed until **all previous reads/writes of data items** are completed everywhere.

### Uses
- Reduces memory traffic on shared variables.
- Allows aggressive hardware optimizations.

---

## 🔍 2.7 Release Consistency (RC)

### Two Synchronization Primitives
- **Acquire ($acq$):** Operation that gains access to a shared resource (e.g., `lock()`).
- **Release ($rel$):** Operation that relinquishes access (e.g., `unlock()`).

### Conditions
1. Before a **read/write of ordinary data**, all **previous acquires** by that process must have completed successfully.
2. Before a **release**, all **previous reads/writes** by that process must have completed.
3. Acquire and release are **processor-consistent** (a specific total order).

> [!NOTE]
> **RC ⊂ Sequential Consistency**, but **RC ⊃ Weak Consistency**.

---

## 🔍 2.8 Entry Consistency (EC)

### EC = RC + "Acquire specifies which data item"
- Each **synchronization variable** is associated with a **specific shared data item**.
- When a process acquires the lock, it is guaranteed to see the **most recent values** of *that* protected data item.
- **Most efficient** of all the weak models (least unnecessary traffic).

---

## 🔍 2.9 Eventual Consistency

### Definition (Amazon Dynamo, Werner Vogels)
> If no new updates are made to a data item, **eventually all accesses will return the last updated value**.

### Properties
- Used in **AP systems** of the **CAP theorem** (e.g., DNS, Cassandra, DynamoDB).
- Strong **availability**, weak **consistency**.
- Conflicts resolved via **last-write-wins (LWW)**, **vector clocks**, or **CRDTs**.

### Mathematical Convergence
$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \text{replicas}(t) \rightarrow \text{agreed value}$$

---

## 📊 KTU FORMULA SHEET / CHEAT SHEET (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Real-Time Required? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Strict Consistency | $op_1.\text{end} \prec_{RT} op_2.\text{start} \Rightarrow R_2 \text{ sees } W_1$ | ✅ Yes | Unrealizable in practice |
| 2 | Linearizability | Total order $\pi \;\vert\; \forall op: end \prec_{RT} op'.\text{start} \Rightarrow op \prec_{\pi} op'$ | ✅ Yes | Local, real-time, implementable |
| 3 | Sequential Consistency | Total order $\pi$, program order preserved | ❌ No | Lamport 1979 |
| 4 | Causal Consistency | $a \rightarrow b \Rightarrow a \prec_{vis} b$ for all procs | ❌ No | Partial order only |
| 5 | PRAM / FIFO | $W_i \prec_{PO} W_j \Rightarrow W_i \prec_{vis} W_j$ | ❌ No | Per-process ordering |
| 6 | Weak Consistency | Sync barrier ($S$) constraints: $R/W$ after all $S$ finish | ❌ No | Uses global sync var |
| 7 | Release Consistency | $acq$ before $R/W$, $rel$ after $R/W$ | ❌ No | Two barriers |
| 8 | Entry Consistency | RC + lock–data binding | ❌ No | Best weak model |
| 9 | Eventual Consistency | $\forall t_0, \exists T: \forall t \geq T,\, v(t) = v_{\text{final}}$ | ❌ No | Used in CAP-AP systems |

> [!TIP]
> **Killer Mnemonic for KTU 2024:** *"**S**equential **C**ausal **P**ram **W**eak **R**elease **E**ntry **E**ventual"* — going from **strongest to weakest** after Strict/Linearizability.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Consistency Model Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Google Spanner** | Linearizability (TrueTime API) | Globally distributed transactions |
| **Cassandra / DynamoDB** | Eventual Consistency (Tunable) | High availability |
| **Memcached** | Strict/Linearizable (single sharded) | Single-master cache |
| **Cooperative Editing (Google Docs)** | Causal Consistency | Real-time collab editing |
| **Linux Kernel Memory (RCU)** | Release Consistency | Lock-free reads |
| **C++ `std::atomic`** | Sequential Consistency (default) | Portable synchronization |
| **Java `volatile`** | Sequential Consistency for `volatile` | JSR-133 spec |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence & Comparison Theorems

### Theorem 1 — Strict ⇒ Linearizability ⇒ Sequential ⇒ Causal ⇒ PRAM ⇒ Eventual

**Proof Sketch (Strength Ordering):**

We show that the constraint set of a stronger model is a **superset** of the weaker.

**Step 1:** Strict consistency enforces real-time precedence on *every* operation.

$$C_{Strict} = \{op_1 \prec_{RT} op_2 \Rightarrow op_1 \prec_{\pi} op_2\}$$

**Step 2:** Linearizability relaxes strict consistency by allowing operations to be *linearized* in an atomic interval (i.e., re-ordered as long as real-time intervals don't overlap).

$$C_{Lin} = \{op_1.end \leq op_2.start \Rightarrow op_1 \prec_{\pi} op_2\}$$

**Step 3:** Sequential consistency drops the real-time constraint but keeps program order and total order.

$$C_{Seq} = \{op_1 \prec_{PO} op_2 \Rightarrow op_1 \prec_{\pi} op_2\} \cap \{\text{total order } \pi\}$$

**Step 4:** Causal consistency removes the requirement of a total order, keeping only the *happens-before* partial order.

$$C_{Causal} = \{a \rightarrow b \Rightarrow a \prec_{vis} b\}$$

**Step 5:** PRAM restricts happens-before to single-process program order.

$$C_{PRAM} = \{W_i^a \prec_{PO} W_i^b \Rightarrow W_i^a \prec_{vis} W_i^b\}$$

**Step 6:** Eventual consistency removes ordering entirely, only guaranteeing convergence.

$$C_{Eventual} = \{\lim_{t\to\infty} v_i(t) = v_j(t)\}$$

**Therefore:**

$$C_{Strict} \supset C_{Lin} \supset C_{Seq} \supset C_{Causal} \supset C_{PRAM} \supset C_{Eventual}$$

**Hence:** Any history that is valid under Strict is also valid under all weaker models, but **NOT vice versa**.

---

### Theorem 2 — Linearizability is Compositional; Sequential Consistency is NOT

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Consider two independent objects $x$ and $y$ with linearizable histories $H_x$ and $H_y$. Construct the combined history:

$$H = H_x \cup H_y$$

**Step 2:** A linearization $\pi_x$ for $H_x$ and $\pi_y$ for $H_y$ exist.

**Step 3:** Merge them using **real-time ordering**. Since $x$ and $y$ are independent, no real-time constraint conflicts arise.

$$\pi = \pi_x \cup \pi_y \quad \text{(a valid linearization of } H\text{)}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore $H$ is linearizable. This is **compositionality**.

**Step 5 (Counter-example for SC):** Consider two processes:

- **Process P1:** $W(x)1$ then $W(y)1$
- **Process P2:** $R(y) \to 0$ then $R(x) \to 0$
- **Process P3:** $R(x) \to 1$ then $R(y) \to 0$

The combined history $H$ is **NOT sequentially consistent**, even though each single-object history is. Hence SC is **not compositional**.

---

## 🔬 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Trace Analysis Methodology

For any KTU exam, use the **3-step trace analysis technique**:

### Step 1: Build the Operation Trace Table
$$H = \{\,W_1(x)1,\; R_2(x)0,\; W_2(x)2,\; R_3(x)1,\; W_3(y)3,\; R_1(y)3\,\}$$

### Step 2: Construct the Happens-Before DAG
Use the **transitive closure** of program order and message dependencies.

### Step 3: Check the Consistency Model
For SC: try to find **any** total order $\pi$ consistent with all program orders. For Causal: check only causally related operations. For Eventual: check convergence only.

---

## 📐 3.3 Worked Example: Linearizability Check (KTU Standard)

**Trace:**
- $W_1(x)a$ at $t=1$
- $R_2(x)b$ at $t=2$ (returns $b$ — but no write to $b$ exists yet!)
- $W_3(x)b$ at $t=3$
- $R_4(x)a$ at $t=4$

### Linearization Check
**Step 1:** Check real-time ordering.

$$W_1 \text{ at } t=1, \quad R_2 \text{ at } t=2, \quad W_3 \text{ at } t=3, \quad R_4 \text{ at } t=4$$

**Step 2:** Test if $R_2(x)b$ can be valid. The only write of $b$ is $W_3$ at $t=3$, but $R_2$ is at $t=2 < 3$. ❌ **Impossible!**

**Step 3:** Conclusion: This trace is **NOT linearizable**.

---

## 📐 3.4 Worked Example: Sequential Consistency Check

**Trace:**
- Process P1: $W(x)1$
- Process P2: $R(x)1$; $W(x)2$
- Process P3: $R(x)1$; $R(x)2$

**Step 1:** Program orders:
- $P1: W_1(x)1$
- $P2: R_2(x)1 \prec_{PO} W_2(x)2$
- $P3: R_3(x)1 \prec_{PO} R_3(x)2$

**Step 2:** Find a total order $\pi$ that satisfies:
- $W_1(x)1$ before $R_2(x)1$ (so $R_2$ returns 1)
- $R_2(x)1$ before $W_2(x)2$
- $W_1(x)1$ before $R_3(x)1$
- $W_2(x)2$ before $R_3(x)2$ (so $R_3$ second read returns 2)

**Step 3:** Valid $\pi$:

$$\pi:\; W_1(x)1 \;\rightarrow\; R_2(x)1 \;\rightarrow\; R_3(x)1 \;\rightarrow\; W_2(x)2 \;\rightarrow\; R_3(x)2$$

**Result:** ✅ Sequentially consistent. *(Not linearizable, because $W_2$ and $R_3$ overlap in real time but $R_3$ returns 1, which is fine under SC but borderline under Lin.)*

---

## 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick Reference)

| Property | Strict | Lineariz. | Sequential | Causal | PRAM | Weak | Release | Entry | Eventual |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Real-time order** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Total order $\pi$** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | (sync only) | (sync only) | (sync only) | ❌ |
| **Program order** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (per proc) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Happens-before** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | (own only) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Synchronization barrier** | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | ✅ global | ✅ 2-type | ✅ 2-type | ❌ |
| **Lock–data binding** | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Compositional** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (eventually) |
| **Implementable in DS** | ❌ | ✅ (with clocks) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

## 📊 4.2 Happens-Before DAG for Causal Consistency

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1a[P1: W x 1] --> P1b[P1: W y 1]
    P1b --read dep--> P2a[P2: R y 1]
    P2a --> P2b[P2: W x 2]
    P2b --concurrent--> P3a[P3: R x]
    P1a --concurrent--> P3a
    P1b --causal--> P2a
    P2b --causal--> P3a

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** P1 writes $x$ then $y$. P2 reads $y$ (causally depends on P1's writes) then writes $x$. P3 sees concurrent writes to $x$ — under **causal consistency**, P3 may see *either* P1's or P2's value of $x$, but **must** see P1's write of $y$ before P2's write of $x$ (causally ordered).

## 📊 4.4 Release Consistency — Lock Acquire/Release Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P1 as Process P1
    participant Lock as Lock Variable L
    participant Data as Shared Data X
    participant P2 as Process P2

    Note over P1,P2: Both processes need consistent view of X
    P1->>Lock: acquire(L) [barrier]
    Lock-->>P1: L granted
    P1->>Data: W X = 100
    P1->>Data: R X (local)
    P1->>Lock: release(L) [barrier: flush all prior writes]
    Lock-->>P2: L granted
    P2->>Data: R X (sees 100 from P1)
    P2->>Lock: release(L)

    Note over Lock,Data: Release = "all my prior writes must be visible to anyone acquiring this lock next"
    Note over Lock,Data: Acquire = "I can now safely read latest protected data"
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture:** The **acquire/release** boundary acts as a *memory fence*. The release operation flushes the **store buffer** of the writer; the acquire operation invalidates the **load buffer** of the reader. This is how RC outperforms Weak Consistency (which has only a single barrier type).

---

## 📊 4.5 CAP Theorem Trade-off Map (Context for Eventual Consistency)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CAP Theorem] --> B[Consistency]
    A --> C[Availability]
    A --> D[Partition Tolerance]
    B -.choose 2.-> E[CA Systems - traditional RDBMS single node]
    C -.choose 2.-> F[AP Systems - DynamoDB Cassandra Eventual Consistency]
    D -.choose 2.-> G[CP Systems - HBase ZooKeeper Linearizable]
    F --> F1[Eventual Consistency]
    G --> G1[Linearizability via Paxos Raft]
    E --> E1[Strict Strong Consistency]

    style A fill:#ff7043,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    style E1 fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#81c784,stroke:#060,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#64b5f6,stroke:#006,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** In the presence of **network partitions** (which is **always** the case in real distributed systems), you can choose only between **Consistency** (CP → Linearizable) or **Availability** (AP → Eventual). Hence most real-world systems (Cassandra, DynamoDB, Riak) adopt **eventual consistency** to remain available 24/7.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Sequential Consistency and Causal Consistency. Give one example where SC holds but Causal Consistency is violated.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sequential Consistency | Causal Consistency |
|---|---|---|
| **Order required** | **Total order** of all operations | **Partial order** (only causally related) |
| **Concurrent writes** | All processes see the **same order** | Processes **may see different orders** for concurrent writes |
| **Strength** | Stronger | Weaker |
| **Real-time** | Not required | Not required |

**Example (SC holds, Causal holds trivially):** Three processes with non-overlapping writes are seen in the same interleaving by all observers — satisfies both models.

**Example (SC violated, Causal holds):**
- P1: $W(x)1$
- P2: $W(x)2$ (concurrent with P1)
- P3: $R(x)1$ then $R(x)2$
- P4: $R(x)2$ then $R(x)1$

No causal relationship exists (concurrent writes), so Causal Consistency is satisfied. However, **Sequential Consistency is violated** because P3 and P4 see the two writes in **opposite orders** and no single total order can be assigned.

*(Full marks if table + example provided.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Eventual Consistency? Mention two real-world systems that use it and justify the choice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Eventual Consistency guarantees that *if no new updates are made to a given data item, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value*.

**Two real-world systems:**

1. **Amazon DynamoDB / Cassandra** — Used for shopping carts, session storage, product catalogs where high availability and low latency are more critical than immediate consistency across replicas.
2. **Domain Name System (DNS)** — DNS propagates updates globally over hours; consistency is "eventual" but the system remains highly available.

**Justification:** Both prioritize **availability** and **partition tolerance** (CAP → AP), tolerating temporary divergence in exchange for continuous service.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either the full Question A** or the **full Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected]**

#### **Q.A (a)** Define Linearizability. With a neat trace diagram, explain how linearizability is stronger than Sequential Consistency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A history $H$ is **linearizable** if there exists a total order $\pi$ of all completed operations such that:
1. $\pi$ respects the **program order** of each process.
2. If $op_1$ finishes (in real time) before $op_2$ starts, then $op_1 \prec_{\pi} op_2$.
3. Each read returns the value of the **most recent preceding write** in $\pi$.

**Comparison with Sequential Consistency (3 marks):**

| Property | Linearizability | Sequential Consistency |
|---|---|---|
| Total order | ✅ | ✅ |
| Program order | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Real-time constraint** | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ No |

**Trace Example (2 marks):**
- $P_1$: $W(x)1$ at $t=1$, finishes at $t=2$
- $P_2$: $R(x)0$ at $t=3$, finishes at $t=4$

For **SC**, the order $R(x)0 \to W(x)1$ is allowed as long as program orders are preserved.
For **Linearizability**, since $W(x)1$ **finishes at $t=2 < 3$ (start of $R(x)$)**, $W(x)1$ **must** precede $R(x)0$ in the linearization. Hence this history is **not linearizable**, but is **sequentially consistent**.

```
Time →  0      1      2      3      4
P1:     |---W(x)1-----|
P2:                       |--R(x)0--|
         ^ W finishes before R starts => L violated
```

**Conclusion:** Linearizability = Sequential Consistency + **Real-time ordering constraint**.

---

#### **Q.A (b)** Explain the **Causal Consistency** model with an example. Show that Causal Consistency is strictly weaker than Sequential Consistency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Causal Consistency requires that operations which are **causally related** (i.e., a *happens-before* dependency) are seen by all processes in the same causal order. **Concurrent (independent)** operations may be observed in any order.

**Happens-Before Rule (1 mark):**
$$e_i^a \rightarrow e_j^b \;\text{ if } (i=j \land a<b) \lor (\exists c: e_i^a \rightarrow e_k^c \rightarrow e_j^b)$$

**Example (2 marks):**
- P1: $W(x)1$ → $W(y)1$
- P2: $R(x)1$ → $W(y)2$ (causally depends on P1's $W(x)1$ via the read)
- P3: $R(y) \to 1$ (must see P1's $W(y)1$ before P2's $W(y)2$)
- P3: $R(x) \to \text{either 1 or no-value}$ (concurrent with nothing on $x$)

**Causal vs Sequential (2 marks):**
- A history valid under **SC** is **always** valid under **Causal**, because SC's total order already satisfies the happens-before partial order.
- However, the converse fails. Consider two concurrent writes to $x$: $W_1(x)1$ and $W_2(x)2$. Under SC, all processes must see them in the **same order**. Under Causal Consistency, since these writes are **not causally related** (no dependency), different processes may observe different orders. Therefore, Causal Consistency allows more histories than SC.

$$\boxed{\;\text{Causal Consistency} \;\subsetneq\; \text{Sequential Consistency}\;}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q.B (a)** Compare **Strict Consistency, Linearizability, and Sequential Consistency** with respect to real-time ordering, program order, and implementability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Tabular — 7 marks):**

| Property | Strict Consistency | Linearizability | Sequential Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proposed by** | — | Herlihy & Wing (1990) | Lamport (1979) |
| **Real-time ordering** | ✅ Absolute global clock | ✅ Local real-time | ❌ None |
| **Program order** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Total order** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Read returns most recent write** | ✅ (real-time) | ✅ (in $\pi$) | ✅ (in $\pi$) |
| **Implementable in DS** | ❌ (needs global clock) | ✅ (with sync clocks) | ✅ |
| **Use case** | Theoretical ideal | Google Spanner, Raft | Java `volatile`, x86 TSO |
| **Strength rank** | 1 (strongest) | 2 | 3 |

**Justification (2 marks):**
- **Strict > Linearizability** because strict requires an **absolute, system-wide real-time order**; linearizability only requires each operation to have a **linearization point** within its invocation–response interval.
- **Linearizability > Sequential** because linearizability additionally requires real-time non-overlap, which SC ignores.

**Example (2 marks):** In a system with two operations $W_1(x)1$ at $t=10$ and $R_2(x)0$ at $t=5$ (returning 0 from a stale replica), the history is **Sequentially Consistent** (it can be re-ordered to satisfy program order) but **not Linearizable** (since $R_2$ returns stale value while $W_1$ already completed before $R_2$ started).

---

#### **Q.B (b)** Explain **Release Consistency** and **Entry Consistency** with diagrams. How does Entry Consistency improve over Release Consistency? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Release Consistency (3 marks):**

Release Consistency uses **two synchronization primitives**:
- **Acquire ($acq$):** Announces intent to access a critical section.
- **Release ($rel$):** Announces end of critical section.

**Rules:**
1. Before a process performs an ordinary read/write, **all previous acquires** by that process must have completed.
2. Before a process releases, **all previous reads/writes** by that process must have completed.
3. Acquire and release operations are **sequentially consistent** themselves.

```
Process P1:    acq(L) → W(x)1 → W(y)2 → rel(L) → ...
Process P2:    ... → acq(L) → R(x)1 → R(y)2 → rel(L)
```

**Entry Consistency (3 marks):**

Entry Consistency = **Release Consistency + Binding**.

- Every **synchronization variable (lock)** is associated with a **specific shared data item** (or set of items).
- When a process acquires a lock $L_x$, it is guaranteed to receive the **most recent value of $x$**.
- This avoids unnecessary propagation of *all* shared variables, only the protected ones.

```
Lock Lx protects x
Lock Ly protects y
Process P1:    acq(Lx) → W(x)1 → rel(Lx)
Process P2:    acq(Lx) → R(x)1   (sees latest x)
```

**Improvement of EC over RC (1 mark):**
- **RC** propagates *all* recent shared writes on acquire, which is **wasteful**.
- **EC** propagates **only the data bound to the lock**, reducing network traffic and improving scalability — making it the **most efficient** of the weak consistency models.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing Strict with Linearizability:** Strict requires *absolute* global real-time order; Linearizability requires *local* real-time order. Writing "they are the same" costs **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the Program-Order condition in SC:** Many students write only the total-order condition and skip the per-process program-order condition. **Both are required** for SC.
> 3. **Mixing up Acquire and Release:** Acquire = *enter* critical section; Release = *exit*. Reversing these breaks the entire RC/EC analysis.
> 4. **Saying "Causal is stronger than Sequential":** This is the **OPPOSITE** of the truth. SC ⊃ CC (SC is stronger). Examiners explicitly test this trap.
> 5. **Not drawing a trace diagram in Part B:** KTU 2024 scheme requires a **labeled trace** for full marks on consistency questions. A correct answer without a diagram typically loses **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Treating Eventual Consistency as "no consistency":** It is a *convergence* guarantee, not a *no-guarantee* model. Use the phrase "**eventually converges**" in the answer.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Consistency Model** = contract defining permissible operation orderings.
- [x] **Strict Consistency** = real-time total order; **unimplementable** in practice.
- [x] **Linearizability** = real-time total order **per operation interval**; *compositional*; implemented by Google Spanner, Raft.
- [x] **Sequential Consistency** (Lamport 1979) = total order + program order; **not compositional**.
- [x] **Causal Consistency** = respects *happens-before* partial order only; concurrent ops may diverge.
- [x] **PRAM (FIFO) Consistency** = same-process writes ordered; cross-process writes unordered.
- [x] **Weak Consistency** = uses single **sync variable** as global barrier.
- [x] **Release Consistency** = uses **acquire/release** as separate barriers.
- [x] **Entry Consistency** = RC + **lock–data binding**; most efficient weak model.
- [x] **Eventual Consistency** = convergence guarantee; used in AP systems (DynamoDB, Cassandra, DNS).
- [x] **Strength ordering:** Strict ⊃ Linearizability ⊃ SC ⊃ Causal ⊃ PRAM ⊃ Eventual.
- [x] **Compositionality:** Linearizable ✅ | Sequential ❌.
- [x] **CAP implication:** Network partition forces choice between **CP (Linearizable)** or **AP (Eventual)**.
- [x] **Real-time check:** Strict & Linearizability use it; SC, Causal, PRAM, Weak, Release, Entry, Eventual do **NOT**.
- [x] **Use-case mappings:** Spanner → Linearizable; Cassandra → Eventual; Google Docs → Causal; Linux RCU → Release; Java volatile → Sequential.
- [x] **Mnemonic:** *"Strong Lions Surely Conquer Cautiously, Producing Weak, Release, Entry, Eventual peace."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security in Distributed Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Security in Distributed Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Security in Distributed Systems? ### Formal Definition **Security in Distributed Systems** refers to the collection of mechanisms, protocols, policies, and cryptographic techniques employed to protect distributed computing resources (data, processes, services, and communication channels) against unauthorized access, modification, disclosure, destruction, or disruption. According to KTU 2024 syllabus, it encompasses confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorization, non-repudiation, and availability across heterogeneous, geographically dispersed nodes. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Security is not a single product but a *layered, end-to-end property* that must be enforced at the **network layer, transport layer, application layer, and middleware layer** simultaneously in a distributed system. ### Core Security Properties (The CIA Triad + Extensions) | Property | Symbol | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Confidentiality** | C | Data is hidden from unauthorized parties | Encrypted email | | **Integrity** | I | Data is not altered in transit | Checksum/hash verification | | **Availability** | A | System is accessible when needed | DDoS protection | | **Authentication** | AuthN | Identity of a principal is verified | Login with password | | **Authorization** | AuthZ | Verified identity is permitted to perform action | File permission checks | | **Non-Repudiation** | NR | Sender cannot deny sending a message | Digital signature | ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Postal Network" Imagine a postal system where letters travel through many hands (postmen, sorters, delivery agents) before reaching the recipient. In this analogy: - **Confidentiality** = The letter is sealed in a *tamper-proof, opaque envelope* that only the recipient can open. - **Integrity** = The envelope has a *unique wax seal*; if broken, the recipient knows it was tampered with. - **Authentication** = The recipient demands an *identity card* (signed by a trusted authority) from the postman. - **Non-Repudiation** = The sender must *sign* the letter in a way that even the sender cannot later deny having written it. - **Availability** = The post office must be *open and functioning* despite floods, strikes, or sabotage. A distributed system is exactly this postal network — messages traverse multiple hops, processes, and machines.
