Security in distributed systems provides the equivalent of sealed envelopes, wax seals, identity cards, and signatures — but in the digital realm using **cryptography**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Philosophy:** In distributed systems, we assume the **network is hostile** (no trust in intermediaries). Therefore, security must be end-to-end, not just point-to-point. ### Threats, Risks, and Attack Surfaces in Distributed Systems ``` INTERCEPT → Eavesdropping on communication channels INTERRUPT → Cutting off a service (DoS / DDoS) MODIFY → Tampering with messages FABRICATE → Inserting forged messages (spoofing, replay) ``` A security mechanism must counter all four classes — collectively known as the **threat model**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundations of Cryptography Cryptography is the mathematical backbone of distributed system security. We classify it into three primary families: ### A. Symmetric Key Cryptography (Secret Key) - **Idea:** A single shared secret key $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption. - **Equation:** $$E_K(M) = C, \quad D_K(C) = M$$ - **Example algorithms:** DES, 3DES, **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** — current benchmark with key sizes of 128, 192, or **256 bits**. - **Strength:** Very fast; suitable for bulk data encryption. - **Weakness:** Key distribution problem — how do two parties agree on a shared key over an insecure channel? ### B. Asymmetric Key Cryptography (Public Key) - **Idea:** Each principal has a key pair $(PK, SK)$ — a public key $PK$ for encryption, and a private (secret) key $SK$ for decryption. - **Core Mathematical Equation:** $$D_{SK}(E_{PK}(M)) = M$$ - **Example algorithms:** **RSA** (based on factoring large primes), **Diffie–Hellman** (key exchange), **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)**. - **Strength:** Solves key distribution; enables digital signatures. - **Weakness:** ~1000× slower than symmetric cryptography. ### C. Cryptographic Hash Functions - **Idea:** A one-way function $H$ that maps arbitrary input $M$ to a fixed-size digest $h$ of length $n$ bits. - **Equation:** $$h = H(M), \quad h \in \{0,1\}^{n}$$ - **Security properties:** 1. *Pre-image resistance:* Given $h$, infeasible to find $M$. 2. *Second pre-image resistance:* Given $M_1$, infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. 3. *Collision resistance:* Infeasible to find any $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. - **Example algorithms:** **MD5** (broken, 128-bit), **SHA-1** (deprecated), **SHA-256 / SHA-3** (current standard, 256-bit). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Fact:** A secure hash for digital signatures must produce at least **256 bits** of output to resist brute-force birthday attacks (birthday bound $\approx 2^{n/2}$). ## 2.2 Digital Signatures A digital signature provides **authentication + integrity + non-repudiation**. **Signing process (using sender Alice's private key $SK_A$):** $$S = E_{SK_A}(H(M))$$ **Verification process (using Alice's public key $PK_A$):** $$H(M) = D_{PK_A}(S)$$ If the recovered hash equals the recomputed hash of the received message, the signature is valid. ## 2.3 Key Distribution and Certification To bind a public key $PK$ to an identity $I$ (a person, server, or service), we use a **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)**: - A **Certificate Authority (CA)** issues an **X.509 digital certificate** containing: $C = \text{Sign}_{SK_{CA}}(I, PK_I, \text{validity})$. - Clients trust the CA's public key $PK_{CA}$ (pre-installed in browsers/OS). - For large systems, we use a **Certificate Chain** rooted at a top-level CA. ## 2.4 Authentication Protocols ### Password-Based (weak) User sends $P$ to server. Insecure — should always be hashed: $$\text{Server stores } h = H(P + \text{salt})$$ ### Challenge-Response (strong) 1. Server sends random nonce $N$. 2. Client returns $f(SK, N)$ or $H(K_{shared}, N)$. 3. Server verifies by recomputing. ### Zero-Knowledge Proof (ZKP) Prover convinces verifier of knowing a secret without revealing it. Used in privacy-preserving systems. ## 2.5 Access Control Models | Model | Description | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **DAC** (Discretionary) | Owner decides who accesses resource | File systems | | **MAC** (Mandatory) | System-enforced labels (e.g., Bell-LaPadula) | Military/government | | **RBAC** (Role-Based) | Access by organizational role | Enterprises | | **ABAC** (Attribute-Based) | Access by policy over attributes | Cloud, IoT | ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Equation | Notes | |---|---|---| | Symmetric Encryption | $C = E_K(M),\, M = D_K(C)$ | Same key for both | | Asymmetric Encryption | $M = D_{SK}(E_{PK}(M))$ | $PK$ public, $SK$ private | | RSA Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | $p, q$ are large primes | | RSA Ciphertext | $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ | $e$ is public exponent | | RSA Decryption | $M = C^{d} \bmod n$ | $d \equiv e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$ | | Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $K = (g^b)^a \bmod p = (g^a)^b \bmod p$ | $a, b$ are private integers | | Digital Signature | $S = E_{SK_A}(H(M))$ | $H$ is a hash function | | Hash Output Length | $n$ bits (e.g., 256 for SHA-256) | Collision resistance $\approx 2^{n/2}$ | | Birthday Attack Work | $\approx 2^{n/2}$ hashes | To find a collision | | HMAC | $HMAC(K,M) = H((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$ | Message authentication code | | TLS Cipher Suite | $\text{TLS\_DHE\_RSA\_WITH\_AES\_256\_GCM\_SHA384}$ | Key exchange, auth, cipher, MAC | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility:** These equations form the basis of HTTPS, SSH, VPN, blockchain, digital payments, and cloud security. Every secure transaction on the internet uses some combination of these primitives. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 RSA Algorithm — Full Derivation RSA is the most widely taught public-key algorithm in KTU. The complete derivation is shown below. ### Key Generation **Step 1:** Choose two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$ (each $\ge 1024$ bits in modern systems). **Step 2:** Compute the modulus: $$n = p \cdot q$$ **Step 3:** Compute Euler's totient function: $$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$$ **Step 4:** Choose a public exponent $e$ such that: $$\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1, \quad 1 < e < \phi(n)$$ A common choice is $e = 65537$ for efficiency. **Step 5:** Compute the private exponent $d$ such that: $$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$ This is found using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. **Public key:** $(e, n)$. **Private key:** $d$. ### Encryption Given plaintext $M$ (interpreted as an integer $0 \le M < n$): $$C = M^{e} \bmod n$$ ### Decryption $$M = C^{d} \bmod n$$ ### Why does this work? (Correctness Proof) We rely on **Euler's Theorem**: for $\gcd(M, n) = 1$, $$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$ Now, $$ \begin{aligned} C^{d} \bmod n &= (M^{e})^{d} \bmod n \\ &= M^{e \cdot d} \bmod n \\ &= M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \bmod n \quad \text{(since } e \cdot d = 1 + k\phi(n)\text{)} \\ &= M^{1} \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \bmod n \\ &= M \cdot (1)^{k} \bmod n \\ &= M \end{aligned} $$ Thus $D_{SK}(E_{PK}(M)) = M$, confirming correctness. The security of RSA rests on the **factoring assumption**: given $n$, finding $p$ and $q$ is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large primes. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Favorite) **Problem:** Let $p = 61$, $q = 53$. Compute the RSA key pair and encrypt $M = 42$. **Step 1:** Modulus $$n = p \cdot q = 61 \times 53 = 3233$$ **Step 2:** Totient $$\phi(n) = (61-1)(53-1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120$$ **Step 3:** Choose $e$ such that $\gcd(e, 3120) = 1$. Let $e = 17$ (check: $3120 = 2^4 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot 13$, and 17 is coprime). **Step 4:** Find $d$ such that $17d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$. Apply Extended Euclidean Algorithm: $$ \begin{aligned} 3120 &= 17 \cdot 183 + 9 \\ 17 &= 9 \cdot 1 + 8 \\ 9 &= 8 \cdot 1 + 1 \\ 8 &= 1 \cdot 8 + 0 \end{aligned} $$ Back-substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 &= 9 - 8 \cdot 1 \\ &= 9 - (17 - 9 \cdot 1) \cdot 1 \\ &= 9 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 1 \\ &= (3120 - 17 \cdot 183) \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 1 \\ &= 3120 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 367 \end{aligned} $$ So $-367 \cdot 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$, which means $d \equiv -367 \equiv 2753 \pmod{3120}$. **Public key:** $(e=17, n=3233)$. **Private key:** $d = 2753$. **Step 5:** Encrypt $M = 42$: $$C = 42^{17} \bmod 3233$$ Compute step by step using repeated squaring: $$ \begin{aligned} 42^1 &= 42 \\ 42^2 &= 1764 \bmod 3233 = 1764 \\ 42^4 &= 1764^2 = 3,111,696 \bmod 3233 \\ 3,111,696 / 3233 &\approx 962 \Rightarrow 962 \cdot 3233 = 3,110,146 \\ 42^4 &\equiv 1550 \pmod{3233} \\ 42^8 &\equiv 1550^2 \bmod 3233 = 2,402,500 \bmod 3233 \\ 2,402,500 / 3233 &\approx 743 \Rightarrow 743 \cdot 3233 = 2,402,119 \\ 42^8 &\equiv 381 \pmod{3233} \\ 42^{16} &\equiv 381^2 \bmod 3233 = 145,161 \bmod 3233 \\ 145,161 - 44 \cdot 3233 &= 145,161 - 142,252 = 2909 \\ 42^{16} &\equiv 2909 \pmod{3233} \end{aligned} $$ Now $17 = 16 + 1$, so $42^{17} = 42^{16} \cdot 42 \equiv 2909 \cdot 42 \bmod 3233 = 122,178$. $122,178 / 3233 \approx 37 \Rightarrow 37 \cdot 3233 = 119,621$. $122,178 - 119,621 = 2557$. $$\boxed{C = 2557}$$ **Step 6:** Decrypt: $$M = 2557^{2753} \bmod 3233$$ (For brevity, modular exponentiation yields $M = 42$, confirming the scheme works.) ## 3.3 Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange — Derivation Two parties Alice and Bob want to share a secret key over an insecure channel. **Public parameters:** A large prime $p$ and a generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. **Step 1:** Alice picks private $a$, computes $A = g^a \bmod p$ and sends $A$ to Bob. **Step 2:** Bob picks private $b$, computes $B = g^b \bmod p$ and sends $B$ to Alice. **Step 3:** Alice computes $K = B^a \bmod p = (g^b)^a \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$. **Step 4:** Bob computes $K = A^b \bmod p = (g^a)^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$. Both arrive at the same shared secret $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$. An eavesdropper seeing $A$ and $B$ cannot compute $K$ without solving the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** — given $g^a \bmod p$, find $a$. This is believed to be computationally hard for well-chosen $p$. > [!NOTE] > **Diffie–Hellman is vulnerable to Man-in-the-Middle attacks** because neither party authenticates the other. Authentication is added via digital signatures or certificates (as in authenticated DH or TLS). ## 3.4 Hash Length & Birthday Bound A hash function producing $n$-bit output has at most $2^n$ possible digests. By the **birthday paradox**, an attacker needs approximately $\sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$ trials to find a collision. For SHA-256 ($n = 256$): work factor $\approx 2^{128}$ — computationally infeasible. | Hash | Output $n$ | Collision Work $2^{n/2}$ | Status | |---|---|---|---| | MD5 | 128 | $2^{64}$ | Broken | | SHA-1 | 160 | $2^{80}$ | Deprecated | | SHA-256 | 256 | $2^{128}$ | Secure | | SHA-3 | 256/512 | $2^{128}$ / $2^{256}$ | Secure | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Layered Security Architecture in a Distributed System ```mermaid flowchart TB appL["APPLICATION LAYER
Apps Services APIs"] presL["PRESENTATION LAYER
TLS SSL XML Encryption"] sessL["SESSION LAYER
AuthN AuthZ Kerberos SSO"] transL["TRANSPORT LAYER
TLS DTLS QUIC"] netL["NETWORK LAYER
IPsec VPN Firewalls"] dataL["DATA LAYER
DB Encryption TDE Backups"] physL["PHYSICAL LAYER
Secure Datacenters HSMs"] appL --> presL presL --> sessL sessL --> transL transL --> netL netL --> dataL dataL --> physL sec1["Cryptography"] -.-> presL sec2["Access Control"] -.-> sessL sec3["PKI Certificates"] -.-> transL sec4["IDS IPS"] -.-> netL sec5["Key Management KMS"] -.-> dataL ``` ## 4.2 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Key Cryptography ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SK["SYMMETRIC KEY CRYPTOGRAPHY"] direction LR sP["Plaintext M"] --> sE["Encrypt with Key K"] --> sC["Ciphertext C"] sC --> sD["Decrypt with Key K"] --> sR["Plaintext M"] end subgraph AK["ASYMMETRIC KEY CRYPTOGRAPHY"] direction LR aP["Plaintext M"] --> aE["Encrypt with Public Key PK"] --> aC["Ciphertext C"] aC --> aD["Decrypt with Private Key SK"] --> aR["Plaintext M"] end note1["Single shared secret K
Fast but key distribution hard"] -.-> SK note2["Key pair PK SK
Solves key distribution
Enables digital signatures"] -.-> AK ``` ## 4.3 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB root["ROOT CA
Self signed trusted by all OS and browsers"] intCA1["INTERMEDIATE CA 1
Issues user and server certs"] intCA2["INTERMEDIATE CA 2
Issues code signing certs"] userC["USER CERTIFICATE Alice"] serverC["SERVER CERTIFICATE www example com"] codeC["CODE SIGNING CERT"] root --> intCA1 root --> intCA2 intCA1 --> userC intCA1 --> serverC intCA2 --> codeC note["Each cert contains PK identity validity
Signed by parent CA private key
Verified using parent CA public key"] -.-> root ``` ## 4.4 Secure Channel Establishment (TLS Handshake) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant S as Server C->>S: ClientHello (supported ciphers, random NC) S->>C: ServerHello (chosen cipher, random NS, ServerCert) S->>C: ServerKeyExchange (e.g., DH params signed by cert) S->>C: ServerHelloDone C->>S: ClientKeyExchange (e.g., DH public value) C->>S: ChangeCipherSpec C->>S: Finished (MAC over handshake) S->>C: ChangeCipherSpec S->>C: Finished (MAC over handshake) Note over C,S: Both compute MasterSecret from pre-master + nonces
Symmetric session keys derived for encryption and MAC C->>S: Encrypted Application Data S->>C: Encrypted Application Data ``` ## 4.5 Threat Model: Attack Vectors in Distributed Systems ```mermaid flowchart TB attacker["ATTACKER"] a1["Eavesdropping
Intercept"] a2["Tampering
Modify"] a3["Replay
Re-send old msg"] a4["Masquerade
Impersonate"] a5["Denial of Service
Flood resources"] a6["Man in the Middle
Relay and alter"] c1["Counter: Encryption AES"] c2["Counter: Hash + Digital Signature"] c3["Counter: Nonce + Timestamps"] c4["Counter: Authentication Certs"] c5["Counter: Rate limiting Firewalls"] c6["Counter: Mutual TLS Cert Pinning"] attacker --> a1 attacker --> a2 attacker --> a3 attacker --> a4 attacker --> a5 attacker --> a6 a1 --> c1 a2 --> c2 a3 --> c2 a4 --> c4 a5 --> c5 a6 --> c6 ``` ## 4.6 Digital Signature Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR msg["Message M"] --> hash1["H computes Hash h1 = H M"] h1 --> sig["Sign h1 with Private Key SK_A
Signature S = E_SK_A h1"] msg -->|"send to Bob"| network["Network"] sig -->|"send with message"| network network --> bob["Bob receives M and S"] h2["Bob recomputes h2 = H M"] --> verify S --> verify["Decrypt S with Alice Public Key PK_A
Recovers h1"] verify --> compare{"h1 == h2 ?"} compare -->|"Yes"| accept["Signature VALID
Authentic and intact"] compare -->|"No"| reject["Signature REJECTED
Tampered or forged"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key cryptography. State one example algorithm for each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Symmetric | Asymmetric | |---|---|---| | Keys | Single shared secret $K$ | Key pair $(PK, SK)$ | | Speed | Fast (1000× faster) | Slow (modular exponentiation) | | Key distribution | Hard | Easy (publish $PK$) | | Confidentiality | $C = E_K(M)$ | $C = E_{PK}(M)$ | | Signature support | No (needs MAC) | Yes | | Example | **AES-256** | **RSA, ECC** | Symmetric uses one shared secret key for both encryption and decryption, e.g., AES-256. Asymmetric uses a public–private key pair, e.g., RSA-2048. (3 marks) --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is a cryptographic hash function? List its three key security properties.** **Model Answer:** A cryptographic hash function $H$ maps an arbitrary-length input $M$ to a fixed-length digest $h = H(M)$ of size $n$ bits (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256). It is a one-way, deterministic function. **Three security properties:** 1. **Pre-image resistance:** Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find any $M$ such that $H(M) = h$. 2. **Second pre-image resistance:** Given $M_1$, it is infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. 3. **Collision resistance:** It is infeasible to find *any* pair $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. Example: SHA-256. (3 marks) --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 #### Q3(a). **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(7 marks)** Explain the RSA public-key cryptosystem with neat mathematical derivation. Generate the key pair for $p = 11$, $q = 13$, $e = 7$, and encrypt $M = 9$. **Model Solution:** **RSA Setup (Algorithm):** 1. Choose two distinct primes $p$, $q$. 2. Compute $n = p \cdot q$ and $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$. 3. Choose $e$ with $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. 4. Compute $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. 5. Public key: $(e, n)$; Private key: $d$. **Numerical Computation:** **Step 1:** $n = 11 \times 13 = 143$ **Step 2:** $\phi(n) = 10 \times 12 = 120$ **Step 3:** Verify $\gcd(7, 120) = 1$ ✓ (120 = $2^3 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$, none of which divide 7) **Step 4:** Find $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$. Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm: $120 = 7 \cdot 17 + 1 \Rightarrow 1 = 120 - 7 \cdot 17$ So $d \equiv -17 \equiv 103 \pmod{120}$. Verify: $7 \cdot 103 = 721 = 6 \cdot 120 + 1 \Rightarrow 721 \bmod 120 = 1$ ✓ **Public key:** $(e=7, n=143)$. **Private key:** $d = 103$. **Step 5:** Encrypt $M = 9$: $$C = 9^7 \bmod 143$$ $9^2 = 81$ $9^4 = 81^2 = 6561 = 45 \cdot 143 + 126 \Rightarrow 126$ $9^7 = 9^4 \cdot 9^2 \cdot 9 = 126 \cdot 81 \cdot 9 \bmod 143$ $126 \cdot 81 = 10206 = 71 \cdot 143 + 53 \Rightarrow 53$ $53 \cdot 9 = 477 = 3 \cdot 143 + 48 \Rightarrow 48$ $$\boxed{C = 48}$$ **Decryption (verification):** $M = 48^{103} \bmod 143 = 9$ ✓ (using CRT, this simplifies to 9). **Correctness Proof:** By Euler's theorem, $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$, so $C^d = M^{ed} = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv M \pmod{n}$. The security relies on the difficulty of factoring $n$ into $p$ and $q$. (7 marks) --- #### Q3(b). **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(7 marks)** Describe the Diffie–Hellman key exchange protocol. What is the discrete logarithm problem? Why is DH vulnerable to a Man-in-the-Middle attack, and how is it prevented? **Model Solution:** **Public parameters:** Prime $p$, generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. **Protocol Steps:** 1. Alice picks secret integer $a$, computes $A = g^a \bmod p$, sends $A$ to Bob. 2. Bob picks secret integer $b$, computes $B = g^b \bmod p$, sends $B$ to Alice. 3. Alice computes $K_A = B^a \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$. 4. Bob computes $K_B = A^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$. Both share $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ — used as a symmetric session key. **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP):** Given $(g, p, A = g^a \bmod p)$, find $a$. For large $p$ (e.g., 2048 bits), this is computationally infeasible with current algorithms. This is the hardness assumption on which DH security rests. **Man-in-the-Middle Vulnerability:** Mallory can intercept $A$ and $B$, and substitute $A' = g^m \bmod p$ and $B' = g^m \bmod p$. Alice computes $K = g^{am} \bmod p$ and Bob computes $K = g^{bm} \bmod p$ — both with Mallory's secret $m$. Mallory reads/modifies all traffic. **Prevention:** - **Digital signatures** on the DH values (each party signs their public value with their private key). - **Certificates** (PKI) to bind $g^a \bmod p$ to Alice's identity. - **Authenticated DH variants:** Station-to-Station (STS) protocol, or running DH inside TLS with certificate-based authentication. (7 marks — protocol 2, DLP 2, MITM 2, prevention 1) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative) #### Q4(a). **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(7 marks)** Explain the SSL/TLS handshake protocol. Show how session keys are derived and how mutual authentication is achieved using certificates. **Model Solution:** **TLS 1.2 Handshake (Simplified):** 1. **ClientHello:** Client sends protocol version, random nonce $N_C$, list of supported cipher suites, and SNI (server name). 2. **ServerHello:** Server chooses cipher suite, sends random nonce $N_S$. 3. **ServerCertificate:** Server sends X.509 certificate containing its public key $PK_S$, signed by CA. 4. **ServerKeyExchange:** Server sends DH/ECDH parameters signed with its private key (for key exchange). 5. **ClientKeyExchange:** Client generates premaster secret, encrypts it with $PK_S$ (or sends DH public value). 6. **Both sides** compute the **Master Secret**: $$MS = \text{PRF}(\text{premaster secret}, \text{"master secret"}, N_C \parallel N_S)$$ 7. **Session keys** are derived using a PRF: $$\text{Key Block} = \text{PRF}(MS, \text{"key expansion"}, N_C \parallel N_S)$$ The key block is split into: - $K_{enc}^{C \to S}$, $K_{enc}^{S \to C}$ (encryption keys) - $K_{MAC}^{C \to S}$, $K_{MAC}^{S \to C}$ (MAC keys) - $IV^{C \to S}$, $IV^{S \to C}$ (initialization vectors) 8. **ChangeCipherSpec & Finished:** Both sides signal that subsequent messages use the new keys, and send a MAC over all handshake messages to detect tampering. **Mutual Authentication:** - The server authenticates itself by possessing the private key matching the certificate's public key (proved by signing the handshake transcript). - The *client* can also send its certificate (optional), enabling mutual TLS (mTLS), commonly used in service-to-service communication in microservices. **TLS 1.3 Improvement:** Reduced to 1 round trip; mandates PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy) via (EC)DH; removed weak ciphers. (7 marks) --- #### Q4(b). **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(7 marks)** Discuss access control models in distributed systems. Compare DAC, MAC, and RBAC with suitable examples. **Model Solution:** **1. Discretionary Access Control (DAC):** - The resource **owner** decides who can access it. - Implemented via Access Control Lists (ACLs). - Example: Unix file permissions — owner, group, others with r/w/x bits. A user can `chmod` their own file to grant access to others. - **Pros:** Flexible, simple. - **Cons:** Vulnerable to Trojan horse attacks; information can leak via copies. **2. Mandatory Access Control (MAC):** - Access is enforced by the **system** based on security labels (classifications and clearances). - Each subject has a clearance; each object has a classification. - **Bell–LaPadula model** (confidentiality): "No read up, no write down." - A subject at clearance *Secret* can read objects classified *Secret* or *Unclassified*, but not *Top Secret*. - A subject at *Secret* can write only to *Secret* or higher (no write down, to prevent leakage). - Example: Military/government systems (SELinux in enforcing mode). - **Pros:** Strong, system-enforced; resists Trojan attacks. - **Cons:** Rigid, hard to administer. **3. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):** - Access is granted based on **roles** assigned to users. - Users $\to$ Roles $\to$ Permissions $\to$ Objects. - Example: In a hospital information system: roles like *Doctor*, *Nurse*, *Receptionist*. A doctor role has permission to view/edit medical records; a receptionist can only view demographic data. - **Variants:** RBAC0 (flat), RBAC1 (hierarchical roles), RBAC2 (constraints), RBAC3 (combined). - **Pros:** Easy administration at scale; principle of least privilege. - **Cons:** Role explosion in complex organizations. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | DAC | MAC | RBAC | |---|---|---|---| | Decision maker | Owner | System | Administrator | | Granularity | Per-object | Label-based | Per-role | | Flexibility | High | Low | High | | Trojan resistance | Poor | Excellent | Good | | Example | Linux ACLs | SELinux | Hospital RBAC | (7 marks — DAC 2, MAC 2, RBAC 2, comparison 1) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **RSA computation:** Students frequently forget the **modular reduction** at every squaring step, leading to astronomically large numbers. Always reduce $\bmod n$ at every step. > 2. **DH confusion:** Confusing *encryption* (RSA) with *key exchange* (DH) — DH by itself provides **no confidentiality**, only a shared secret. > 3. **Hash vs. encryption:** Many students wrongly claim SHA-256 is "encryption." It is **not reversible** — it is a one-way hash. > 4. **CIA confusion:** Confidentiality is about *secrecy*, integrity is about *tamper-detection* — they are independent properties and must be enforced separately. > 5. **Digital signature missteps:** Forgetting that the **signature is computed on the hash, not the message** — this is what makes signatures short and efficient. > 6. **TLS handshake:** Students often omit the **Finished message** which provides authentication of the handshake itself; without it, the handshake is vulnerable to downgrade attacks. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **CIA triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the foundation of all security goals. - **Symmetric crypto** (AES) is **fast** but suffers from key distribution problems. Use for bulk data. - **Asymmetric crypto** (RSA, ECC) is **slow** but solves key distribution and enables digital signatures. Use for key exchange and signing. - **RSA security** rests on the **integer factorization problem**: $n = p \cdot q$, with $p, q$ each ≥ 1024 bits in modern systems. - **Diffie–Hellman** solves key agreement over an insecure channel; security rests on the **discrete logarithm problem**. - **Hash functions** (SHA-256) provide integrity; collision resistance work is $\approx 2^{n/2}$. - **Digital signatures** = encrypt hash with **sender's private key**; verified by decrypting with **sender's public key**. Provide authentication + integrity + non-repudiation. - **PKI / X.509 certificates** bind public keys to identities via a chain of trusted CAs. - **TLS/SSL** = the workhorse of internet security; uses asymmetric crypto for key exchange + symmetric crypto for bulk data + HMAC for integrity. - **Access control models:** DAC (owner decides), MAC (system-enforced labels), RBAC (role-based), ABAC (attribute/policy-based). - **Threats to defend against:** Intercept, Interrupt, Modify, Fabricate (the four classical threats). - **Key management** (generation, distribution, storage, rotation, revocation) is the *hardest* problem in cryptography — the algorithms are math; the keys are policy. - **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS):** Use ephemeral keys (e.g., DHE, ECDHE) so compromise of a long-term key does not decrypt past sessions. - **Defense in depth:** Never rely on a single security layer — combine cryptography, authentication, access control, firewalls, IDS/IPS, and physical security. - **KTU 2024 marks split (typical 14-mark question):** Algorithm explanation (4) + Numerical example (5) + Diagram/Protocol trace (3) + Comparison/Conclusion (2).

topics.title: Security Threats and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Threats and Vulnerabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Threats** are any potential circumstances, events, or agents that can exploit vulnerabilities in a distributed system to cause harm, unauthorized access, modification, or denial of services. **Vulnerabilities** are weaknesses, flaws, or gaps in the system's design, implementation, configuration, or operation that can be exploited to compromise security.

In the context of distributed systems, a **threat** is the *possibility* of an attack, while a **vulnerability** is the *opening* through which an attack can succeed. Together, they form the basis of **risk** in any networked computing environment.

Mathematically, we can express this relationship as:

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Impact})$$

Where:
- **Threat** = likelihood of an attack being attempted
- **Vulnerability** = probability that the attack will succeed
- **Impact** = the damage caused if the attack succeeds

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bank vault** (your distributed system) protected by multiple layers of security:
- **Threats** are like the bank robbers who *plan* to break in — they exist in the environment and have intent.
- **Vulnerabilities** are the *unlocked windows*, weak locks, or unguarded back doors that allow robbers to actually get in.
- **Security mechanisms** (locks, guards, alarms) are the countermeasures you put in place.

If you have a locked vault but robbers have no interest in your bank, the risk is low. If robbers are highly motivated but your vault is impregnable, the risk is also low. Risk is high **only when threats meet vulnerabilities**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> - *Threat* = "Who can attack us and why?"
> - *Vulnerability* = "How can they get in?"
> - *Attack* = "The actual attempt."
> - *Risk* = "How bad is the combination?"

## 1.3 Core Security Goals — The CIA Triad

The foundational model of information security comprises three pillars (often extended to five with Non-Repudiation and Authenticity):

| Goal | Symbol | Meaning in Distributed Systems |
|------|--------|-------------------------------|
| **Confidentiality** | C | Only authorized parties can read the data |
| **Integrity** | I | Data is not altered in transit or at rest |
| **Availability** | A | Services remain accessible to legitimate users |

Additionally, for distributed systems specifically:
- **Authentication** — Verifying the identity of a principal
- **Authorization** — Determining what an authenticated principal can do
- **Non-Repudiation** — A party cannot deny having performed an action
- **Accountability** — Actions can be traced back to the responsible entity

## 1.4 The Security Equation

A secure distributed system must satisfy:

$$S = \{D, M, P, A\}$$

Where:
- $S$ = Secure system
- $D$ = Deployed hardware/software components
- $M$ = Security mechanisms in place
- $P$ = Security policies being enforced
- $A$ = Auditing and monitoring infrastructure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A system is only as strong as its **weakest link**. Security must be designed *defense-in-depth* — multiple overlapping layers so that compromising one layer does not collapse the entire system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Security Threats

Security threats in distributed systems can be classified along **four primary dimensions**:

### 2.1.1 By Nature of Attack — The **4 Pillars** (Saltzer & Schroeder Model)

This is the most cited classification in KTU textbooks:

**Pillar 1: Interception**
- An unauthorized party gains access to data.
- Example: Eavesdropping on network traffic, packet sniffing.
- Violates **Confidentiality**.

**Pillar 2: Interruption**
- A system service or data becomes unavailable or unusable.
- Example: Denial-of-Service (DoS), hardware failure exploited by attacker.
- Violates **Availability**.

**Pillar 3: Modification**
- Unauthorized changes are made to data or system state.
- Example: Man-in-the-middle altering transmitted messages, tampering with files.
- Violates **Integrity**.

**Pillar 4: Fabrication (or "Generation")**
- An entity produces counterfeit objects or claims a false identity.
- Example: Spoofing IP addresses, replaying old messages, forging digital signatures.
- Violates **Authenticity**.

### 2.1.2 By Attacker's Strategy — Passive vs. Active

| Aspect | Passive Attacks | Active Attacks |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| **Goal** | Read/observe information | Modify, disrupt, or fabricate |
| **Detection** | Difficult — leaves no trace | Easier — system behavior changes |
| **Examples** | Sniffing, traffic analysis, eavesdropping | Masquerading, message modification, DoS, replay |
| **Threat Type** | Confidentiality | Integrity + Availability |
| **Defenses** | Encryption, traffic padding | Authentication, integrity checks, monitoring |

### 2.1.3 By Origin — Internal vs. External

| Aspect | Internal Threats | External Threats |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| **Source** | Inside the trust boundary (employees, insiders, compromised nodes) | Outside the trust boundary (remote attackers, hackers) |
| **Difficulty** | Hard to detect — already have credentials | Easier to block with firewalls |
| **Damage Potential** | Often very high | Variable |

### 2.1.4 By Targeted Layer (OSI / Middleware)

| Layer | Specific Threat |
|-------|----------------|
| Application | SQL injection, XSS, logic flaws |
| Middleware | RPC spoofing, CORBA security bypass |
| Transport | TCP session hijacking, SYN flooding |
| Network | IP spoofing, routing attacks, sniffing |
| Physical / Link | Wiretapping, MAC flooding |

## 2.2 Major Categories of Vulnerabilities in Distributed Systems

### 2.2.1 Design Vulnerabilities
Flaws present in the architectural decisions:
- Lack of end-to-end encryption
- Insecure protocols (e.g., plaintext HTTP, FTP, Telnet)
- Absence of mutual authentication
- Single point of failure

### 2.2.2 Implementation Vulnerabilities (Bugs)
- Buffer overflows
- Race conditions
- Improper input validation
- Use of insecure libraries

### 2.2.3 Configuration Vulnerabilities
- Default credentials left unchanged
- Open ports / unnecessary services running
- Weak cipher suites enabled
- Misconfigured firewalls / ACLs

### 2.2.4 Operational Vulnerabilities
- Missing patches
- Weak password policies
- Inadequate logging / monitoring
- Social engineering exposure

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common question asks to **classify a given threat** into one of the 4 pillars (Interception, Interruption, Modification, Fabrication). Always justify using the violated security property.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| Concept | Description | Unit / Type |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| $P(\text{breach}) = P(\text{attempt}) \times P(\text{vulnerable})$ | Probability a breach occurs | Probability [0, 1] |
| $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Composite risk model | Qualitative/Quantitative |
| $n = \lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ | Bits of secret key for $k$ possible values | bits |
| $\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | System availability ratio | Dimensionless [0, 1] |
| $\text{SLA} = 1 - \text{downtime}/\text{period}$ | Service Level Agreement uptime | e.g., 99.999% |
| $\text{CIA} = \{C, I, A\}$ | Core security triad | Set of properties |

> [!NOTE]
> The $\vert$ symbol in formulas like $P(\text{breach} \vert \text{conditions})$ is written using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

## 2.4 Trust and Trustworthiness in Distributed Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trust** is the belief that an entity will behave as expected. **Trustworthiness** is the verified property that an entity deserves trust.

In distributed systems, trust relationships can be:
- **Direct Trust** — based on personal experience with an entity
- **Indirect (Recommended) Trust** — based on recommendations from third parties
- **Hierarchical Trust** — based on a chain of certifications (PKI model)

The **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** is the set of all hardware, software, and firmware components that enforce security policy. A vulnerability in any TCB element compromises the entire system.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| Banking & Finance | Preventing transaction tampering, replay attacks |
| Healthcare (HIS) | Protecting patient records (HIPAA-style compliance) |
| IoT & Smart Grids | Mitigating botnet-based DDoS |
| Cloud Computing | Multi-tenant isolation, API gateway protection |
| Defense & Aerospace | Secure middleware for C4ISR systems |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Risk Computation Model

Let us derive the **composite risk score** used in formal threat modeling (e.g., NIST, OWASP frameworks).

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the Base Risk Components

A threat $T$ is characterized by:
- $L_T$ = Likelihood of the threat being realized
- $I_T$ = Impact if the threat succeeds

A vulnerability $V$ is characterized by:
- $E_V$ = Ease of exploitation (a score from 0 to 1)
- $A_V$ = Availability of the vulnerability (i.e., how discoverable it is)

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Compute Vulnerability Exposure Score

The exposure score combines how easy it is to exploit and how often it is exposed:

$$E_{\text{score}} = E_V \times A_V$$

where each factor is normalized in $[0, 1]$.

**Explanation:** If a vulnerability is easy to exploit (e.g., a default password) **and** is highly discoverable (publicly exposed port), then $E_{\text{score}}$ approaches **1**. Conversely, an obscure bug buried in legacy code reachable only via a closed VPN has $E_{\text{score}}$ close to **0**.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute Composite Risk

The combined risk is the product of threat potential, vulnerability exposure, and asset value:

$$R = L_T \times E_{\text{score}} \times I_T$$

**Explanation:** Risk is high *only* when all three factors are non-trivial:
- A real and likely threat ($L_T$)
- An exploitable weakness ($E_{\text{score}}$)
- A valuable target impact ($I_T$)

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Normalize to a 0–10 Scale

In practical risk registers, we rescale to a 0–10 score for ease of comparison:

$$R_{\text{norm}} = 10 \times R$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The composite risk score is used to **prioritize** remediation. Items with $R_{\text{norm}} \geq 7$ are treated as critical and patched first.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Threats to Defenses

| Threat | 4-Pillar Class | CIA Violated | Specific Defense | Middleware Example |
|--------|---------------|--------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Packet sniffing | Interception | Confidentiality | Encryption (TLS, IPsec) | CORBA over SSL |
| Replay attack | Fabrication | Authenticity | Nonces, timestamps | Kerberos TGTs |
| Data tampering | Modification | Integrity | Hashing (SHA-256), MACs | Digital signatures in RMI |
| DoS / DDoS | Interruption | Availability | Rate limiting, redundancy | Load balancers, CDN |
| IP spoofing | Fabrication | Authenticity | Source authentication | IPsec AH header |
| Man-in-the-Middle | Interception + Modification | C + I | Mutual TLS, certificates | HTTPS with PKI |
| Insider data theft | Interception | Confidentiality | RBAC, audit logs | LDAP-based access control |
| Code injection (SQLi) | Modification | Integrity | Input validation, parameterized queries | ORM frameworks |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A middleware system uses unencrypted RPC. An attacker on the same LAN captures all messages. Classify the threat and propose two defenses.

**Solution Step 1 — Identify the threat:**
- The attacker is **passively reading** messages.
- This is a **passive attack** of type **Interception**.
- **CIA property violated:** Confidentiality.

**Solution Step 2 — Map to the 4-pillar model:**
- Category: *Interception* (Saltzer & Schroeder, 1975).
- Risk equation: $R = L_T \times E_{\text{score}} \times I_T$
  - $L_T = 0.8$ (high — common in shared networks)
  - $E_{\text{score}} = 1.0$ (trivial to capture with Wireshark)
  - $I_T = 0.9$ (critical if RPCs contain credentials)
  - $R = 0.8 \times 1.0 \times 0.9 = 0.72$, which is $R_{\text{norm}} = 7.2$ → **High risk**.

**Solution Step 3 — Propose defenses:**
- **Defense 1:** Enable **TLS/SSL** for all RPC channels → encryption defeats passive interception.
- **Defense 2:** Use **mutual authentication** with digital certificates → only legitimate parties can establish sessions.

**Solution Step 4 — Verify residual risk:**
After TLS is deployed, even if the attacker captures the ciphertext, they cannot decrypt it without the session key. This drops $E_{\text{score}}$ from $1.0$ to roughly $0.05$, so:

$$R_{\text{new}} = 0.8 \times 0.05 \times 0.9 = 0.036 \rightarrow R_{\text{norm}} = 0.36$$

This is an **acceptable low risk**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Availability under DoS Attack

For a system with $n$ redundant servers, where each has availability $A_i$, the parallel availability is:

$$A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)$$

**Explanation:** The system is unavailable only if **all** servers fail simultaneously. If each server has $A_i = 0.99$, with $n = 3$:

$$A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.99)^3 = 1 - (0.01)^3 = 1 - 0.000001 = 0.999999$$

**Result:** Three nines become **six nines** simply by tripling redundancy. This is why DoS defenses rely on **redundancy + load balancing + rate limiting**.

## 3.5 Analytical Insights for Examination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Attacker's Economy of Effort:** A defender must succeed *every time*; an attacker only needs to succeed *once*. This asymmetric cost is the central reason why **defense-in-depth** is non-negotiable in distributed system design.

The classical **attack tree** model proposed by Bruce Schneier formalizes this:

$$\text{Top Goal} = \text{OR}(\text{Sub-goal}_1, \text{Sub-goal}_2, \dots, \text{Sub-goal}_n)$$

Each sub-goal itself is an AND/OR tree of preconditions, allowing the defender to evaluate the **cheapest path** the attacker is likely to take and harden that specific route.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 4-Pillar Threat Classification (Saltzer & Schroeder)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Security Threat in Distributed System] --> B{Attack Nature}
    B --> P1[Pillar 1: Interception]
    B --> P2[Pillar 2: Interruption]
    B --> P3[Pillar 3: Modification]
    B --> P4[Pillar 4: Fabrication]
    P1 --> C1[Violates Confidentiality]
    P2 --> C2[Violates Availability]
    P3 --> C3[Violates Integrity]
    P4 --> C4[Violates Authenticity]
    C1 --> D1[Defense: Encryption]
    C2 --> D2[Defense: Redundancy, Rate Limiting]
    C3 --> D3[Defense: Hashing, MACs, Signatures]
    C4 --> D4[Defense: Authentication, Nonces]
```

## 4.2 Threat → Attack → Vulnerability → Defense Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Threat Source] --> A2[Attack Vector]
    A2 --> A3[Vulnerability Exploited]
    A3 --> A4[Compromised Security Property]
    A4 --> A5[Impact on System]
    A5 --> A6[Defense Mechanism]
    A6 --> A7[Residual Risk]
    A1 -->|Example| B1[External Hacker]
    A2 -->|Example| B2[Phishing Email]
    A3 -->|Example| B3[Unpatched Mail Server]
    A4 -->|Example| B4[Confidentiality Lost]
    A5 -->|Example| B5[Data Exfiltration]
    A6 -->|Example| B6[Email Filtering, Patching, MFA]
    B7 --> A7
    A7 -->|Monitor| A8[Continuous Risk Assessment]
```

## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Perimeter] --> L2[Layer 2: Network]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Host]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Application]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Data]
    L1 -->|Firewall, DMZ| M1[Blocks External Scans]
    L2 -->|IDS, IPS, VPN| M2[Blocks Sniffing and Spoofing]
    L3 -->|Antivirus, Hardening| M3[Blocks Malware and Privilege Escalation]
    L4 -->|WAF, Input Validation| M4[Blocks Injection and Logic Flaws]
    L5 -->|Encryption, Backups| M5[Blocks Data Loss and Tampering]
```

## 4.4 Attack Tree for Distributed Middleware Compromise

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Goal: Compromise Middleware Service] --> or1[OR]
    or1 --> s1[Sub-goal A: Steal Credentials]
    or1 --> s2[Sub-goal B: Bypass Authentication]
    or1 --> s3[Sub-goal C: Exploit Protocol Flaw]
    s1 --> and1[AND: Phishing AND User Clicks Link]
    s1 --> and2[AND: Sniff Plaintext AND Decrypt Easily]
    s2 --> and3[AND: Forge Token AND Replay]
    s3 --> and4[AND: Discover CVE AND Unpatched System]
```

## 4.5 Risk Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Threat] --> TL[Likelihood L_T]
    V[Vulnerability] --> TE[Exposure E_score]
    I[Impact] --> TI[Impact I_T]
    TL --> R[R = L_T x E_score x I_T]
    TE --> R
    TI --> R
    R --> RN[Normalized Score R_norm]
    RN --> D{R_norm >= 7}
    D -->|Yes| X[Critical: Immediate Remediation]
    D -->|No| Y[Schedule in Next Cycle]
```

## 4.6 Trust and TCB Boundary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TCB[Trusted Computing Base TCB]
        HW[Hardware: TPM, Secure Enclave]
        FW[Firmware: BIOS, UEFI]
        OS[OS Kernel: SELinux, seccomp]
        MW[Middleware: CORBA Security, RMI SSL]
    end
    TCB --> POL[Enforced Security Policy]
    OUT[Outside: Untrusted Networks] -->|Attacks| TCB
    POL -->|Audit Logs| AUD[Auditing and Monitoring]
    AUD -->|Feedback| TCB
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between a security **threat** and a **vulnerability** with one real-world example for each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Threat | Vulnerability |
|--------|--------|---------------|
| Definition | Any potential event that can cause harm | A weakness that allows the threat to succeed |
| Nature | External agent or circumstance | Internal flaw or gap |
| Example | A hacker attempting SQL injection on a web form | Unsanitized input field in the form code |

A **threat** is the *possibility*; a **vulnerability** is the *opening*. Both must exist for an attack to succeed. *(1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### Q2. List and briefly explain the **four classes of security threats** as per the Saltzer and Schroeder model. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Interception** — Unauthorized party gains access to data (e.g., packet sniffing). Violates **confidentiality**. *(0.75 mark)*
2. **Interruption** — Service or data becomes unavailable (e.g., DoS attack). Violates **availability**. *(0.75 mark)*
3. **Modification** — Unauthorized alteration of data (e.g., MITM tampering). Violates **integrity**. *(0.75 mark)*
4. **Fabrication** — Counterfeit object or false identity (e.g., IP spoofing, replay). Violates **authenticity**. *(0.75 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **list** threats without classifying them under the 4-pillar model — *always* map each threat to a specific pillar and the CIA property it violates.
> - Vague answers like "use encryption" earn partial credit. Specify *what* encryption, *where* it is applied, and *what* attack it counters.
> - For 14-mark questions, examiners expect a **diagram** (attack tree, layered defense, or trust boundary). Missing diagrams = loss of 2–3 marks.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **CIA triad** in detail. Discuss how each property can be violated in a distributed middleware environment with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** Construct an **attack tree** for a distributed banking application where an attacker attempts to steal funds. Identify at least three distinct attack paths and propose the corresponding defenses.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Confidentiality (2 marks)**
- Definition: Ensuring that data is accessible only to authorized parties.
- Example violation: An attacker performs **eavesdropping** on unencrypted SOAP messages between a client and a banking web service. The account numbers, passwords, and transaction details become exposed.
- Defense: Use **TLS 1.3** with strong cipher suites; encrypt sensitive fields even within TLS using envelope encryption.

**2. Integrity (2 marks)**
- Definition: Ensuring that data has not been altered in an unauthorized manner.
- Example violation: A **man-in-the-middle** modifies a fund transfer request from "Transfer 100 USD" to "Transfer 10000 USD" while it is in transit across a middleware bus.
- Defense: Apply **digital signatures** (RSA/ECDSA) to each request; verify message integrity using **HMAC-SHA256** for performance-critical paths.

**3. Availability (2 marks)**
- Definition: Ensuring that services and data are accessible when needed.
- Example violation: An attacker launches a **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)** attack by flooding the bank's authentication server with millions of requests, preventing legitimate customers from logging in.
- Defense: Deploy **CDN-based rate limiting**, **Anycast routing**, redundant authentication servers behind a **load balancer**, and a **Web Application Firewall (WAF)**.

**4. Extended properties (1 mark)**
- **Authentication, Authorization, Non-repudiation, Accountability** — Discussed as critical extensions in middleware.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Attack Tree Construction (4 marks):**

Top Goal: **Steal funds from Account X**

```
GOAL: Steal Funds
  |
  +-- OR -- Path 1: Steal Credentials
  |          +-- AND: Phish user
  |          +-- AND: User clicks malicious link
  |          +-- AND: Credentials harvested
  |
  +-- OR -- Path 2: Replay Old Transaction
  |          +-- AND: Capture old RPC
  |          +-- AND: Bypass nonce
  |          +-- AND: Server replays transaction
  |
  +-- OR -- Path 3: Bypass Authentication
  |          +-- AND: Find SQLi in login
  |          +-- AND: Forge session token
  |          +-- AND: Issue new transactions
  |
  +-- OR -- Path 4: Insider Abuse
             +-- AND: Bank employee with access
             +-- AND: Approves fraudulent transaction
```

**Defenses Proposed (3 marks):**

| Attack Path | Specific Defense |
|-------------|-----------------|
| Path 1 (Phishing) | Anti-phishing training, **MFA**, email filtering, DMARC |
| Path 2 (Replay) | **One-time nonces**, timestamps, sequence numbers, TLS 1.3 |
| Path 3 (SQLi) | **Parameterized queries**, WAF rules, code reviews |
| Path 4 (Insider) | **Role-based access control**, dual approval, audit logs, separation of duties |

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast **passive** and **active** security attacks. Provide two real-world examples for each and discuss detection difficulty.

**(b) [7 marks]** Design a **defense-in-depth architecture** for a cloud-based distributed system handling medical records. Justify each layer with a specific threat it addresses.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| Goal | Observe / collect information | Modify, disrupt, or fabricate |
| Modification of data | No | Yes |
| Detection difficulty | **High** — leaves no trace | **Moderate** — system state changes |
| Impact | Confidentiality only | Integrity + Availability |
| Examples | Sniffing, traffic analysis | DoS, MITM, SQL injection |

**Passive Attack Examples (2 marks):**
1. **Packet Sniffing** — Wireshark on a shared LAN captures HTTP cookies. The attacker impersonates the user. Detection: almost impossible without IDS rules.
2. **Traffic Analysis** — Even with encrypted traffic, an attacker observes that a hospital's bandwidth spikes at 2 AM daily, inferring that a scheduled job is running. Detection: very difficult.

**Active Attack Examples (2 marks):**
1. **Man-in-the-Middle** — Attacker intercepts a TLS handshake, downgrades it to SSLv3 (POODLE attack), and decrypts the session. Detection: modern HSTS policies and protocol version pinning help.
2. **DDoS** — Mirai botnet floods a healthcare portal with traffic. Detection: spike in anomalous traffic triggers IDS/IPS.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Layered Defense Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Edge - WAF and CDN] --> L2[Layer 2: Network - VPC Isolation, NACLs]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Identity - SSO, MFA, OAuth2]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Application - Input Validation, RBAC]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Data - AES-256 at Rest, TLS in Transit]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Audit - SIEM, Immutable Logs]
```

**Layer-by-Layer Justification (4 marks):**

| Layer | Threat Addressed | Specific Control |
|-------|-----------------|------------------|
| 1. Edge WAF + CDN | DDoS, XSS, SQLi | Cloudflare/AWS Shield, OWASP rule sets |
| 2. Network VPC | Sniffing, lateral movement | Private subnets, security groups |
| 3. Identity | Credential theft, impersonation | MFA, SAML/OAuth2, just-in-time access |
| 4. Application | Logic flaws, broken access control | OWASP ASVS controls, RBAC with ABAC |
| 5. Data | Data exfiltration, tampering | AES-256, envelope encryption, HSM-backed keys |
| 6. Audit | Insider abuse, forensics | SIEM (Splunk/ELK), immutable WORM storage |

This six-layer design ensures that even if an attacker breaches one layer, **four more** must be defeated before patient data is exposed — aligning with HIPAA's "reasonable safeguards" requirement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Security Threats and Vulnerabilities**

- [ ] **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability (extend with Authentication, Non-Repudiation).
- [ ] **Saltzer & Schroeder 4 Pillars** = Interception, Interruption, Modification, Fabrication.
- [ ] **Threat ≠ Vulnerability ≠ Attack ≠ Risk** — these are distinct terms; examiners test the difference.
- [ ] **Passive vs. Active attacks** — passive reads data, active modifies/disrupts.
- [ ] **Internal threats** are statistically more damaging than external — always mention insider risk.
- [ ] **Risk equation:** $R = L_T \times E_{\text{score}} \times I_T$ — risk is high only when all three align.
- [ ] **TCB (Trusted Computing Base)** — any flaw inside TCB compromises everything outside it.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth** — minimum 4–5 layers, each addressing a different threat class.
- [ ] **Attack Trees** — AND/OR graph used to enumerate attacker paths; defenders harden the cheapest path.
- [ ] **Availability formula** for parallel redundancy: $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod(1 - A_i)$.
- [ ] **Trust models** — Direct, Indirect, Hierarchical (PKI).
- [ ] **Middleware-specific threats** — RPC spoofing, CORBA security bypass, RMI deserialization attacks.
- [ ] **Mapping rule for exams:** Every threat → one pillar → one CIA property → one defense.
- [ ] **Always draw a diagram** in 14-mark questions (attack tree, layered defense, or trust boundary).
- [ ] **Mention nonces and timestamps** when discussing replay attack defenses.
- [ ] **Mention TCB** when asked "where should security mechanisms be implemented?"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authentication and Authorization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT742 - DISTRIBUTED SYSTEMS AND MIDDLEWARE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Authentication and Authorization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Authentication — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Authentication** is the security mechanism by which a distributed system verifies the claimed identity of a principal (user, process, or service) before allowing any interaction with protected resources. It answers the question: *"Are you really who you say you are?"*

In a distributed system, authentication is **not** a trivial local lookup. Since principals, services, and resources are spread across heterogeneous machines, identity must be propagated and validated through cryptographic protocols, trusted third parties, or token exchanges.

**Three Classical Authentication Factors:**

1. **Knowledge Factor (Something you know)** — Passwords, PINs, passphrases, security questions.
2. **Possession Factor (Something you have)** — Smart cards, hardware tokens, mobile authenticator apps, digital certificates.
3. **Inherence Factor (Something you are)** — Biometrics such as fingerprint, iris scan, voice recognition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** combines two or more independent factors, drastically reducing the success rate of credential-stuffing and phishing attacks. KTU examiners frequently ask for a 3-mark definition contrasting authentication vs authorization — ensure you explicitly state *which* factor a given technique belongs to.

## 1.2 Authorization — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Authorization** is the post-authentication process of determining and enforcing the access rights (read, write, execute, delete, admin) that an authenticated principal is permitted to perform on a given resource. It answers: *"Now that we know who you are, what are you allowed to do?"*

Authorization operates on **policies, roles, capabilities, or attributes** — never on raw identity alone. The outcome is a binary or graded decision: `GRANT` or `DENY` (or partial grants in ABAC systems).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Model

Imagine a modern international airport as a distributed system:

- **Authentication** is the passport control checkpoint. You show your passport (credential). The immigration officer (authenticator) cross-checks it against a central database (identity provider). Only if the database confirms your identity are you allowed past the counter.
- **Authorization** is the boarding gate. Your boarding pass (authorization token) determines which flight (resource) you may board, your seat class (privilege level), and whether you may access the lounge (fine-grained permission).
- A passenger cleared at immigration but holding the wrong boarding pass is **authenticated but not authorized** — and is stopped at the gate.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** *AuthN = "A**N**swering Who"* (verifies identity). *AuthZ = "A**Z**-level access"* (decides privileges).

## 1.4 Why Both Are Required in Distributed Systems

> [!WARNING]
> A common exam pitfall: Treating authentication and authorization as interchangeable. They are **sequentially dependent** but functionally distinct. Authentication must succeed **before** authorization can be meaningfully evaluated. A system that authorizes without authenticating is fatally insecure.

| Property | Authentication | Authorization |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | *Who are you?* | *What can you do?* |
| **Input** | Credentials (password, token, biometric) | Identity + Resource + Action |
| **Output** | Verified identity (or rejection) | Access decision (GRANT / DENY) |
| **Failure Mode** | Identity not proven → block entirely | Permission missing → block that operation |
| **Caching** | Sessions / tokens (short-lived) | ACLs, RBAC roles (longer-lived) |
| **Standards** | Kerberos, OAuth 2.0, SAML, OpenID | XACML, RBAC0, OAuth scopes, CASL |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Authentication Mechanisms

### 2.1.1 Password-Based Authentication (Knowledge)
The simplest but most vulnerable form. Security depends on password entropy and storage hygiene.

**Operational Steps:**
1. User submits username $u$ and password $p$.
2. System retrieves stored hash $H_{stored} = h(p_{original})$ from identity store.
3. Computes $H_{computed} = h(p_{entered})$ and compares: $\text{Match} \iff H_{stored} \equiv H_{computed}$.
4. On match, system issues a session token (cookie, JWT, or Kerberos TGT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hashing must be salted and slow.** Use algorithms like **bcrypt**, **scrypt**, or **Argon2id**. Never store plaintext or reversible encryption. The salt $s$ is unique per user: $H_{stored} = h(p \oplus s)$.

### 2.1.2 Token-Based Authentication
A short-lived opaque or self-contained token replaces repeated credential transmission.

**Two Major Token Families:**

- **Opaque tokens** (e.g., Kerberos tickets, session IDs) — require server-side lookup.
- **Self-contained tokens** (e.g., JWT — JSON Web Tokens) — carry claims, signed by the issuer.

### 2.1.3 Certificate-Based Authentication (PKI)
Uses asymmetric cryptography with **X.509** digital certificates issued by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**.

**Handshake (TLS Mutual Auth):**
1. Client presents its certificate $C_{client}$ containing public key $PK_C$ signed by CA.
2. Server verifies the CA chain: $\text{verify}(CA \rightarrow C_{client})$.
3. Client proves possession of the private key by signing a server challenge $n$: $\sigma = \text{sign}(SK_C, n)$.
4. Server verifies $\text{verify}(PK_C, \sigma, n) = \text{True}$.

### 2.1.4 Biometric Authentication
Uses physiological or behavioral characteristics. Accuracy is measured by:
- **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** — probability an impostor is accepted.
- **False Rejection Rate (FRR)** — probability a legitimate user is rejected.
- **Equal Error Rate (EER)** — point where FAR = FRR (lower is better).

### 2.1.5 Challenge–Response Protocols
The verifier sends a nonce $r$; the prover responds with $f(r)$ using a shared secret or private key.

- **Symmetric (HMAC):** Response $= \text{HMAC}(K, r)$.
- **Asymmetric:** Response $= \text{Sign}(SK_A, r)$.

## 2.2 Kerberos — The Canonical Distributed Authentication Protocol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerberos** is a ticket-based, symmetric-key authentication protocol developed at MIT as part of Project Athena. It is the de-facto standard for single sign-on (SSO) in enterprise distributed systems (Windows Active Directory uses a Kerberos variant).

**Key Roles:**
- **KDC (Key Distribution Center)** — the trusted third party, comprising AS + TGS.
- **AS (Authentication Server)** — verifies principal identity and issues TGT.
- **TGS (Ticket Granting Server)** — issues service tickets for specific resources.
- **Principal** — user or service identity.
- **Service Server (V)** — the resource being accessed.

**Five-Step Simplified Kerberos Exchange:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. } C \rightarrow AS &: \quad \text{ID}_C, \text{ID}_{TGS}, N_1 \\
\text{2. } AS \rightarrow C &: \quad E_{K_C}[K_{C,TGS}, \text{ID}_{TGS}, N_1, \text{Timestamp}_{TGT}], T_{TGS} \\
\text{3. } C \rightarrow TGS &: \quad \text{ID}_V, T_{TGS}, \text{Authenticator}_C \\
\text{4. } TGS \rightarrow C &: \quad E_{K_{C,TGS}}[K_{C,V}, \text{ID}_V, N_2, \text{Timestamp}_{ST}], T_V \\
\text{5. } C \rightarrow V &: \quad T_V, \text{Authenticator}_C'
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $T_{TGS}$ = Ticket Granting Ticket, encrypted with $K_{TGS}$ (TGS's master key).
- $T_V$ = Service Ticket for service $V$, encrypted with $K_V$.
- $\text{Authenticator}_C = E_{K_{C,TGS}}[\text{ID}_C, \text{ADC}, \text{TS}_C]$ — proves key possession.

> [!TIP]
> **Why timestamp nonces?** Kerberos defeats replay attacks. Each authenticator has a short lifetime (typically **5 minutes**), enforced via clock-skew tolerance (default **5 minutes** max skew).

## 2.3 Authorization Models

### 2.3.1 Access Control Lists (ACLs)
A list attached to **each resource**, enumerating principals and their permissions.

$$
\text{ACL}_{file} = \{ (\text{Alice}, \{r\}),\ (\text{Bob}, \{r, w\}),\ (\text{Group:Devs}, \{r, w, x\}) \}
$$

**Pros:** Per-resource granularity. **Cons:** Doesn't scale to many principals; hard to audit "what does Alice have access to?"

### 2.3.2 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
Access is granted to **roles**, and roles are assigned to users. The NIST RBAC standard defines four levels: $\text{RBAC}_0, \text{RBAC}_1, \text{RBAC}_2, \text{RBAC}_3$.

**Core Relations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U \times R &: \text{ User-to-Role assignment (many-to-many)} \\
R \times P &: \text{ Role-to-Permission assignment} \\
\text{User } u \text{ can do } p &: \iff \exists r \in R : (u, r) \in UA \land (r, p) \in PA
\end{aligned}
$$

**RBAC Hierarchies ($\text{RBAC}_1$):** Senior roles inherit permissions of junior roles.
$$
r_1 \geq r_2 \implies \text{permissions}(r_2) \subseteq \text{permissions}(r_1)
$$

**Separation of Duties ($\text{RBAC}_2$):** Mutually exclusive roles prevent fraud (e.g., the same person cannot be both cashier and auditor).

### 2.3.3 Capability-Based Access Control
An unforgeable **capability** is a tuple $(\text{object}, \text{rights}, \text{holder})$ held by the principal. Capabilities can be delegated.

$$
\text{Capability} = E_{K_{system}}[\text{ObjectID}, \text{Rights}, \text{HolderID}, \text{Expiry}]
$$

### 2.3.4 Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)
Decisions based on attributes of **subject, object, action, and environment**, evaluated against a policy.

**Boolean Policy Example:**

$$
\text{Grant} = (\text{role} = \text{"Doctor"}) \land (\text{department} = \text{patient.dept}) \land (\text{time} \in [08:00, 18:00]) \land (\text{action} = \text{"read"})
$$

Standard: **XACML** (eXtensible Access Control Markup Language).

### 2.3.5 OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect
- **OAuth 2.0** is an **authorization** framework (RFC 6749). It issues **access tokens** with defined **scopes**.
- **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** sits atop OAuth 2.0 and adds **authentication** via the **ID Token** (a signed JWT).

**Key OAuth Roles:** Resource Owner, Client, Authorization Server (AS), Resource Server (RS).
**Grant Types:** Authorization Code, Client Credentials, Implicit (deprecated), Password (deprecated), Device Code.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hash Match | $\text{Accept} \iff h(p_{\text{stored}}) = h(p_{\text{entered}})$ | Password authentication test | Boolean |
| HMAC Response | $\text{Resp} = \text{HMAC}(K, r)$ | Challenge–response | bits |
| EER Point | $\text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$ | Biometric accuracy | Percentage |
| RBAC Inheritance | $r_1 \geq r_2 \Rightarrow P(r_2) \subseteq P(r_1)$ | Role hierarchy | Set inclusion |
| XACML Decision | $\text{Grant} = f(\text{attrs}_{S}, \text{attrs}_{O}, \text{attrs}_{E})$ | ABAC policy evaluation | Boolean |
| Kerberos Lifetime | $T_{\text{auth}} \leq 5$ min | Replay protection window | Minutes |
| Clock Skew Tolerance | $\Delta t \leq 5$ min | Default Kerberos skew | Minutes |
| JWT Signature | $\sigma = \text{Sign}(SK_{\text{iss}}, \text{header.payload})$ | Token integrity | bits |
| ACL Size | $O(n \cdot p)$ | Worst-case per principal lookup | Operations |
| Salt Length | $\vert s \vert \geq 128$ bits | Per-user salt size | bits |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** When asked to compare RBAC vs ABAC in 7 marks, always structure the answer as: (1) definition, (2) decision inputs, (3) granularity, (4) scalability, (5) example, (6) tradeoff. Examiners reward structured answers with a diagram.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud microservices** use **OAuth 2.0 + JWT + RBAC** for fine-grained, stateless authorization.
- **Banking systems** layer **MFA** (password + OTP + biometric) over **ABAC** for fraud detection.
- **IoT and edge systems** lean on **mutual TLS** (X.509) and **capability tokens** to constrain resource-constrained devices.
- **Federated university networks** (eduGAIN) use **SAML + Shibboleth** for cross-institution SSO.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Salting Defeats Rainbow Tables

**Problem:** Pre-computed rainbow tables allow an attacker to invert unsalted hashes in $O(1)$ lookup time across billions of candidates.

**Setup:** Suppose passwords are 8 lowercase alphanumeric characters, hashed with MD5.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Search space (unsalted)} &= 36^8 \approx 2.8 \times 10^{12} \text{ hashes} \\
\text{Storage for rainbow table} &\approx 2.8 \times 10^{12} \times 16 \text{ bytes} \approx 45 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is feasible — modern GPUs compute ~50 billion MD5/s, so the entire table can be computed in under a minute.

**With a per-user random salt $s$ of length 128 bits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{stored}} &= h(p \oplus s) \\
\text{Distinct salts needed} &\geq 2^{128} \\
\text{Storage for rainbow tables} &\approx 2^{128} \times 16 \text{ bytes} \gg 10^{33} \text{ TB (infeasible)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Per-user random salts make precomputation attacks computationally infeasible. The attacker must now attack each hash individually via brute force, with each guess requiring the same amount of work as a single-hash system.

## 3.2 Derivation: Kerberos Replay Attack Defense

**Without timestamps:** A passive attacker who captures a service ticket $T_V$ and authenticator $\text{Auth}_C$ can replay it to impersonate $C$ to $V$.

**Kerberos defense (timestamped authenticator):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Auth}_C &= E_{K_{C,V}}[\text{ID}_C, \text{ADC}, \text{TS}_C] \\
\text{Acceptance rule at } V &: \quad \vert \text{now} - \text{TS}_C \vert \leq \Delta_{\text{skew}} \quad \text{(default 5 min)}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the attacker replays $\text{Auth}_C$ after 6 minutes, $|now - TS_C| > 5$ min, and $V$ rejects. Combined with the requirement that the authenticator is encrypted under the **session key** $K_{C,V}$ (itself delivered inside the encrypted service ticket $T_V$), the attacker cannot fabricate a fresh authenticator without $K_{C,V}$.

**Joint conditions for replay to succeed:**
1. Attacker must obtain $K_{C,V}$ (requires breaking TGS encryption — infeasible if AES-256 used).
2. Attacker must replay within $\Delta_{\text{skew}}$ window — narrow and easily shrunk.

## 3.3 Derivation: RBAC Role Hierarchy Permission Closure

Given a role hierarchy defined as a partial order $\geq$ on roles, the effective permission set of role $r$ is the **upward closure** of all junior roles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{eff}}(r) &= \bigcup_{r' \leq r} P(r') \\
\text{Constraint (no permission loss)} &: \quad r_1 \geq r_2 \implies P(r_2) \subseteq P(r_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Roles} &: \quad R = \{\text{Intern}, \text{Engineer}, \text{SeniorEng}, \text{Manager}\} \\
\text{Hierarchy} &: \quad \text{Intern} \leq \text{Engineer} \leq \text{SeniorEng} \leq \text{Manager} \\
P(\text{Intern}) &= \{\text{readCode}\} \\
P(\text{Engineer}) &= \{\text{readCode}, \text{commitCode}\} \\
P(\text{SeniorEng}) &= \{\text{readCode}, \text{commitCode}, \text{reviewPR}\} \\
P(\text{Manager}) &= \{\text{readCode}, \text{commitCode}, \text{reviewPR}, \text{deployProd}, \text{manageTeam}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective permission of Manager:**

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(\text{Manager}) = P(\text{Intern}) \cup P(\text{Engineer}) \cup P(\text{SeniorEng}) \cup P(\text{Manager}) = P(\text{Manager})
$$

The closure property holds trivially here because of the linear chain.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Authorization Models

| Property | ACL | RBAC | Capability | ABAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Decision Locus** | Resource | Role | Token | Policy engine |
| **Granularity** | Per-resource, per-user | Per-role | Per-object | Per-attribute combination |
| **Best For** | File systems | Enterprises with stable roles | OS, distributed objects | Dynamic, context-aware |
| **Scalability (users)** | Poor | Good | Good | Excellent |
| **Auditability (who can do X)** | Difficult | Easy | Difficult | Easy |
| **Delegation** | Manual | Role assignment | Native | Policy-driven |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(\vert ACL \vert)$ | $O(\text{role lookup})$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\text{policy eval})$ |
| **Standard** | POSIX ACLs, NTFS | NIST RBAC, XACML profile | E, Capsicum | XACML, ALFA |
| **Weakness** | Doesn't scale | Role explosion | Revocation hard | Policy complexity |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Cost of Brute Force on a Salted Hash

Suppose a system uses Argon2id with parameters tuned so a single guess takes $t = 0.5$ seconds, and an offline attacker has 1000 GPU instances.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Password entropy (8-char mixed)} &\approx \log_2(94^8) \approx 52.4 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Total search space} &= 2^{52.4} \approx 6.3 \times 10^{15} \\
\text{Guesses per second (cluster)} &= \frac{1000 \text{ GPUs} \times 1 \text{ hash}}{0.5 \text{ s}} = 2000 \text{ guesses/s} \\
\text{Expected time to crack (avg)} &= \frac{6.3 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 2000 \text{ s}} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{12} \text{ s} \approx 50{,}000 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows why a strong password policy + slow hash + salt = practical security against offline attack.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 High-Level Authentication & Authorization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Principal C] -->|1. Submit credentials| B[Edge Gateway]
    B -->|2. Forward to Identity Provider| C[Authentication Server AS / KDC]

    D -- No --> E[Reject and Log]
    D -- Yes --> F[Issue Auth Token TGT or JWT]
    F -->|4. Present TGT| G[Ticket Granting Server TGS]
    G -->|5. Request service ticket| H[Policy Decision Point PDP]
    H -->|6. Evaluate policy| I{Authorized?}
    I -- No --> J[Access Denied 403]
    I -- Yes --> K[Issue Service Ticket ST]
    K -->|7. Present ST| L[Resource Server V]
    L -->|8. Enforce action| M[Grant Access and Audit]
```

## 4.2 Kerberos Five-Step Exchange (Detailed Block View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client C
    participant AS as Authentication Server
    participant TGS as Ticket Granting Server
    participant V as Service Server V

    Note over C,AS: Step 1: Identity request
    C->>AS: ID_C, ID_TGS, N1
    Note over AS,C: Step 2: AS replies with TGT
    AS->>C: E_KC[K_C_TGS, ID_TGS, N1, Lifetime], T_TGS
    Note over C,TGS: Step 3: Request service ticket
    C->>TGS: ID_V, T_TGS, Authenticator_C
    Note over TGS,C: Step 4: TGS issues service ticket
    TGS->>C: E_KC_TGS[K_C_V, ID_V, N2], T_V
    Note over C,V: Step 5: Access service
    C->>V: T_V, Authenticator_C'
    V-->>C: Service Response
```

```mermaid
    P -->|Rules| PDP
    PDP -->|Decision| PEP[Policy Enforcement Point PEP]
    PEP -->|Permit / Deny| RS[Resource Server]
```
